EDCS-14400118.translation: Hilara, daughter of Adiutor, made this for herself while alive, and for her mother Batasia, and for her deceased father Adiutor, aged 50.
EDCS-14400118.summary: This funerary inscription records that Hilara dedicated a monument for herself and her parents, including her father who died at the age of 50.

EDCS-14400119.summary: This is a concise funerary inscription stating that sons honored their parents by erecting this monument.

EDCS-14400122.text_edition: \n et Annetia Ursa coniunx vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-14400122.translation: and Annetia Ursa, his wife, made this while alive.
EDCS-14400122.summary: This is a funerary inscription fragment where Annetia Ursa, a wife, dedicates something, likely for herself and her deceased husband.

EDCS-14400123.translation: Restitutius Provincial made this for his uncle while alive.
EDCS-14400123.summary: This funerary inscription records that Restitutius Provincial dedicated a monument for his uncle.

EDCS-14400124.text_edition: \n Finitus Terti filius et Ateressa Quinti filia vibi sic fecerunt Floro filio annorum XVIII et sibi\n
EDCS-14400124.translation: Finitus, son of Tertius, and Ateressa, daughter of Quintus, made this for themselves while alive, and for their son Florus, aged 18.
EDCS-14400124.summary: This funerary inscription records that Finitus and Ateressa dedicated a monument for themselves and their deceased son who died at age 18.

EDCS-14400125.text_edition: \n Seneca Primigeni filius et Vibena Vibeni filia uxor Ampliatus \n
EDCS-14400125.translation: Seneca, son of Primigenius, and Vibena, daughter of Vibenus, wife of Ampliatus.
EDCS-14400125.summary: This inscription fragment names Seneca and Vibena, possibly indicating their relationship and marital status, likely part of a larger funerary or commemorative text.

EDCS-14400126.text_edition: \n Lottonis filio etiulae Dab filiae ro filius et a filia filii parentibus fecerunt\n
EDCS-14400126.translation: The son made this for his father Lottonis and his daughter Dab, and a son and daughter for their parents.
EDCS-14400126.summary: This fragmented funerary inscription seems to record a son dedicating a monument for his parents, Lottonis and Dab, and possibly siblings as well.

EDCS-14400127.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II consul pater patriae \n
EDCS-14400127.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the second time, consul, father of the country.
EDCS-14400127.summary: This is a formal titulature of Emperor Trajan Decius, listing his imperial titles and honors, likely from a public monument.

EDCS-14400129.text_edition: \n Macus Titonis filius et Munita Titionis\n
EDCS-14400129.translation: Macus, son of Titu, and Munita, daughter of Titu.
EDCS-14400129.summary: This inscription fragment names Macus and Munita, likely siblings, possibly as dedicators or subjects of a funerary or commemorative monument.

EDCS-14400131.translation: Vercaio and his sons, alive, made this.
EDCS-14400131.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a monument made by Vercaio and his sons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400135.text_edition: \n Vercaius Ittaes sic filius et Agisiae Vindili filiae et Quarto filio annorum XXV et Mosica Ituni vvivi fecerunt sibi hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur et Vero nnorum XV\n
EDCS-14400135.translation: Vercaius, son of Ittaes, and Agisia, daughter of Vindilus, and their son Quartus, aged 25, and Mosica, children of Itunus, alive, made this monument for themselves; the heir shall not succeed them, and Vero, aged 15.
EDCS-14400135.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a family monument made by Vercaius and his relatives, specifying that no heir will inherit it, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400137.text_edition: \n Tiberius Iulius Fabiani libertus Quartus et Matrona Attae filia uxor vivi\n
EDCS-14400137.translation: Tiberius Iulius Quartus, freedman of Fabiani, and Matrona, daughter of Atta, his wife, alive.
EDCS-14400137.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording Tiberius Iulius Quartus and his wife Matrona, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400138.text_edition: \n filius Anniae Ogiae uxori Rucconi Val \n
EDCS-14400138.translation: son to Annia Ogia, his wife, and to Rucconius Val...
EDCS-14400138.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription that appears to record a son's dedication to his mother Annia Ogia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400139.text_edition: \n Domitius Attunis filius sibi et Mosicuni uxori et Quartae filiae pientissimae\n
EDCS-14400139.translation: Domitius, son of Attunis, for himself, and for Mosica, his wife, and for Quarta, their most dutiful daughter.
EDCS-14400139.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a dedication made by Domitius for himself, his wife, and his daughter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400141.text_edition: \n Secundus Melisses sic libertus vivus fecit sibi etovixtae sorori\n
EDCS-14400141.translation: Secundus, freedman of Melisses, alive, made this for himself and for his sister Ovixta.
EDCS-14400141.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a monument made by Secundus for himself and his sister, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400142.text_edition: \n Postumus Rufini servus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400142.translation: Postumus, slave of Rufinus, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-14400142.summary: This is a votive inscription recording a slave named Postumus fulfilling a vow to an unnamed deity, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400143.text_edition: \n Saxano Augusto sacrum Messor Cai Herenni Rufini servus ex vissu sic posuit\n
EDCS-14400143.translation: Messor, slave of Caius Herennius Rufinus, set this up to Saxanus Augustus, sacred, as a vision.
EDCS-14400143.summary: This is a votive inscription recording a slave named Messor setting up a sacred monument to Saxanus Augustus after a divine vision, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400144.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription possibly recording names, perhaps related to a dedication or tomb, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400145.text_edition: \n Ca<t=I>ia Quar<t=I>i liberta Iunonibus ancillarum votum solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-14400145.translation: Caetia Quarti, freedwoman, to the Iunones, attendants, fulfilled her vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-14400145.summary: This is a votive inscription recording a freedwoman named Caetia fulfilling a vow to the Iunones, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400146.text_edition: \n Baussus Cotulonis et Caletiu Vercai vivi fecerunt sibi et Quartae filiae annorum XII\n
EDCS-14400146.translation: Baussus and Caletius, sons of Cotulus and Vercaus, made this while alive for themselves and for their daughter Quarta, aged 12.
EDCS-14400146.summary: This funerary inscription, likely from a tombstone, records a dedication made by two individuals for themselves and their deceased daughter.

EDCS-14400147.text_edition: \n Biraco Montiae libertus vivus fecit sibi et cae coniugi\n
EDCS-14400147.translation: Biraco, freedman of Montius, made this while alive for himself and for his wife.
EDCS-14400147.summary: This funerary inscription records a self-dedication by a freedman for himself and his wife.

EDCS-14400149.text_edition: \n IIvir et praefectus iure dicundo Litullae parentibus optimis fecit\n
EDCS-14400149.translation: He made this for his most excellent parents, serving as aedile and prefect with judicial power.
EDCS-14400149.summary: This dedication honors parents and was made by an individual who held significant local magistracies.

EDCS-14400152.text_edition: \n Thallus Quinti Septuei Clementis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400152.translation: Thallus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Quintus Septuetus Clemens.
EDCS-14400152.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Thallus to Quintus Septuetus Clemens.

EDCS-14400160.text_edition: \n Aesculapio Augusto sacrum Caius Marius Lucanius Priscus decurio et IIvir iure dicundo Claudio Viruno et Cominia Quinti filia Celsinio cum Lucaniis Maximiano Aveto Spectato et Prisco filiis ex voto posuerunt libentes laeti merito\n
EDCS-14400160.translation: Gaius Marius Lucanius Priscus, decurion and aedile with judicial power, together with Claudius Virunus and Cominia, daughter of Quintus Celsinius, and his sons Maximianus, Avetus, Spectatus, and Priscus, set this up to Aesculapius Augustus in fulfillment of a vow, gladly, joyfully, and deservedly.
EDCS-14400160.summary: This votive inscription records a dedication to Aesculapius Augustus by a prominent local official, his family, and others, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400161.text_edition: \n Cantius Darbius vivus fecit sibi et Risiae Terti filiae coniugi et Terentio filio et Vercillae Viai filiae\n
EDCS-14400161.translation: Cantius Darbius made this while alive for himself, for his wife Risia, daughter of Tertius, and for his son Terentius, and for Vercilla, daughter of Via.
EDCS-14400161.summary: This funerary inscription records a self-dedication by Cantius Darbius for himself and several family members.

EDCS-14400162.text_edition: \n Maromogio pagi magistri votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-14400162.translation: The village magistrates willingly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-14400162.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by local village magistrates.

EDCS-14400164.text_edition: \n Drippio Lottae filio et Suttuni Agrippae filiae uxori Ingenuus filius vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14400164.translation: Ingenuus, son of Drippius, made this while alive for his son, Lotta, and for his wife, Agrippa, daughter of Suttunus.
EDCS-14400164.summary: This funerary inscription records a self-dedication by Ingenuus for his son and wife.

EDCS-14400165.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ursino Terti? filio et Sertoriae Serotini filiae parentibus Maturus Ursini filius fecit et coniugi karissimae et sibi\n
EDCS-14400165.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Maturus, son of Ursinius, made this for his parents Ursinius, son of Tertius (?), and Sertoria, daughter of Serotinus, and for his dearest wife, and for himself.
EDCS-14400165.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication to the Divine Shades and records the actions of Maturus for his parents, wife, and himself.

EDCS-14400167.text_edition: \n RCV LC et Augnuni Attuci filiae\n
EDCS-14400167.translation: For R.C.V.L.C. and Augnum, daughter of Attucus.
EDCS-14400167.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or memorial, though its full meaning is unclear due to abbreviations.

EDCS-14400168.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely dedicatory or funerary, mentioning 'Father Jupiter' and a relative, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400169.text_edition: \n Venimaro Secconis filio Messilla Attuci filia uxor viva fecit\n
EDCS-14400169.translation: Messilla, daughter of Attucus, his wife, made this for Venimarus, son of Secconis.
EDCS-14400169.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a wife's memorial for her husband, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400171.text_edition: \n Mercurio Quintus Volusius Priscus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400171.translation: Quintus Volusius Priscus gladly and deservedly paid his vow to Mercury.
EDCS-14400171.summary: This is a votive inscription recording a man named Quintus Volusius Priscus fulfilling a vow to the god Mercury, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400172.text_edition: \n Marco Aurelio Ursino veterano misso honesta missione ex legione II Italica obito nnorum LX marito optimo et Aelio Verano et Iuniae Celataes sic parentibus vivis Aelia Deivilla et Aurelii Ursina R \n
EDCS-14400172.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Ursus, a veteran discharged with honor from the II Italica legion, aged 60, his most excellent husband, and to Aelius Veranus and Iunia Celataes, their parents alive, Aelia Deivilla and Aurelia Ursina R.
EDCS-14400172.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring a veteran named Marcus Aurelius Ursus, set up by his wives and possibly children, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-14400175.text_edition: \n Augusto sacrum Terentius Agathopus et Attius Hilarus Barbius Vitalis votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-14400175.translation: Terentius Agathopus, Attius Hilarus, and Barbius Vitalis gladly and deservedly paid their vow, sacred to Augustus.
EDCS-14400175.summary: This is a collective votive inscription recording three individuals fulfilling a vow to the deified emperor Augustus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400176.text_edition: \n Marco Opellio Diadumeniano Cai Opelli Severi Macrini Imperatoris filio Caesari nobilissimo principi iuventutis ordo Teurniensium\n
EDCS-14400176.translation: To Marcus Opellius Diadumenianus, son of Caius Opellius Severus Macrinus, the Emperor, Caesar, most noble prince of youth, the council of the Teurnienses.
EDCS-14400176.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription honoring Marcus Opellius Diadumenianus, likely a member of the imperial family or a prominent youth, by the local council of Teurnia, probably from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-14400178.summary: This is a very brief inscription, potentially a dedication or a funerary marker, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400181.text_edition: \n Carminius Secundinus tia Primitiva vivi fecerunt sibi et Carminio Felicioni et Primitivae \n
EDCS-14400181.translation: Carminius Secundinus and tia Primitiva, while alive, made this for themselves and for Carminius Felicioni and Primitiva.
EDCS-14400181.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a couple, Carminius Secundinus and tia Primitiva, making a tomb for themselves and others, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400183.text_edition: \n O IO CIO umarae us Quordi Aneca uxor\n
EDCS-14400183.translation: O IO CIO ... umarae us Quordi, Aneca his wife.
EDCS-14400183.summary: This is a highly fragmentary inscription, possibly funerary, mentioning names and potentially a deity, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400184.text_edition: \n Adnamatus Lali Momma Attuis filia Priamo patrono vivo fecerunt sibi et suis\n
EDCS-14400184.translation: Adnamatus, Lali, Momma, daughter of Attuis, made this for Priamus, their living patron, for themselves and their families.
EDCS-14400184.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a group of freedmen or clients setting up a tomb for their living patron, Priamus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400186.text_edition: \n Devinato Boioni Restuti defuncto annorum XXXV Candida marito optimo et sibi viva fecit\n
EDCS-14400186.translation: Candida made this for her most excellent husband Restutus, son of Devinto Boioni, who died at 35 years, and for herself while alive.
EDCS-14400186.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a wife for her deceased husband and herself, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400187.text_edition: \n Vibiae F libertae et Att P libertae Vibiae \n
EDCS-14400187.translation: To Vibius Atticus, freedman, and to Vibius, freedwoman.
EDCS-14400187.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for two freed persons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400189.text_edition: \n Ulpius Dasio veteranus ex centurione et Ulpia Nigrina coniux fecerunt\n
EDCS-14400189.translation: Ulpius Dasius, a veteran and former centurion, and his wife Ulpia Nigrina had this made.
EDCS-14400189.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a veteran and his wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400190.text_edition: \n iorum Ingenuo et Marcellino filiis Sabino filio Av\n
EDCS-14400190.translation: To Ingenuus and Marcellinus, sons, and to Sabinus, son of Av[...]
EDCS-14400190.summary: This is a dedication or funerary epitaph fragment for multiple sons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400191.text_edition: \n Senanto Adiatulli filio et Epantiae Iuni filiae coniugi\n
EDCS-14400191.translation: To Senantus, son of Adiatullus, and to his wife Epantia, daughter of Junius.
EDCS-14400191.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a husband and wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400193.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ecronius Restutus et Suceliu\n
EDCS-14400193.translation: To the Divine Spirits, Ecronius Restutus and Sucelius.
EDCS-14400193.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Ecronius Restutus and Sucelius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400194.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Trausi filius Attucius missicius cohortis I Noricorum nnorum L stipendiorum XXVIII testamento fieri iussit sibi et Primo Fusco Prisco libertis isdem liberti et heredes fecerunt\n
EDCS-14400194.translation: Tiberius Claudius Attucius, son of Trausus, a soldier of the 1st Cohort of Noricans, 50 years old, with 28 years of service, ordered this to be made by his will for himself and for his freedmen Primus Fuscus and Priscus; his freedmen and heirs had this made.
EDCS-14400194.summary: This is a funerary epitaph commissioned by a soldier for himself and his freedmen, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400196.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sic viva fecit sibi et Ursino fabro coniugi carissimo\n
EDCS-14400196.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Sicca made this for herself while alive and for her dearest husband Ursinius the craftsman.
EDCS-14400196.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a wife for herself and her husband, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400198.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Crepereius Dionisius beneficiarius consularis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400198.translation: Gaius Crepereius Dionysius, a clerk of the governor, paid his vow freely and deservedly to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-14400198.summary: This is a dedication recording the fulfillment of a vow by an imperial official to Jupiter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400199.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus a Teurnia milia passuum IIII\n
EDCS-14400199.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus [mentions distance in] 4 miles from Teurnia.
EDCS-14400199.summary: This inscription likely records a road marker or boundary related to an imperial decree or construction project initiated by Emperor Severus Alexander.

EDCS-14400200.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli milia passuum IIII\n
EDCS-14400200.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, consul: four miles.
EDCS-14400200.summary: This is a milestone inscription, likely from the reign of Gordian III (mid-3rd century CE), indicating the distance of four miles from a starting point.

EDCS-14400201.text_edition: \n Pusio Cai Valentinii Flavi servus vivus fecit sibi et Tertiae coniugi pientissimae et Boniatae socrae\n
EDCS-14400201.translation: Pusio, slave of Caius Valentinius Flavinus, made this for himself while alive, and for Tertia, his most devoted wife, and for Boniata, his mother-in-law.
EDCS-14400201.summary: This is a tomb inscription from a slave named Pusio, who erected it for himself and his family, likely in the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-14400203.text_edition: \n P(ublius) Aelius Lucretius veteranus ex |(centurione) sibi et Butturae Cypaeri filiae uxori et Augustae filiae nnorum \n
EDCS-14400203.translation: Publius Aelius Lucretius, a veteran and former centurion, [made this] for himself and for Buttura Cypaeris, his daughter, his wife, and Augusta, his daughter, [who died at the age of]...
EDCS-14400203.summary: This is a tomb inscription from a Roman veteran, Publius Aelius Lucretius, who dedicated it to himself and his family, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-14400205.text_edition: \n Bellicus Sabini Verani vivus fecit sibi et Candidae coniugi et Saturnino fratri et Sporae matri et Senoni et Sextiliae sororibus\n
EDCS-14400205.translation: Bellicus, son of Sabinus Veranius, made this for himself while alive, and for Candida, his wife, and Saturninus, his brother, and Spora, his mother, and Seno and Sextilia, his sisters.
EDCS-14400205.summary: This is a tomb inscription from Bellicus, son of Sabinus Veranius, who erected it for himself and his family, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-14400207.summary: This is a fragment of a dedicatory inscription, likely to a person named Aelius, with illegible context and date.

EDCS-14400209.text_edition: \n Pudens conductoris ferrariarum Noricarum et assessoris ferrariarrum sic servos sic et Senecia mater Aniceto filio nnrum XVIII parentes vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-14400209.translation: Pudens, manager of the Norican iron mines and assessor of the iron mines, and his slaves, and Senecia, his mother, and Anicetus, his son, at the age of eighteen, their parents alive, made this.
EDCS-14400209.summary: This tomb inscription records a dedication made by parents for their son Anicetus, who died at eighteen, and also mentions Pudens, a manager in the iron mines, likely dating to the 2nd-4th century CE.

EDCS-14400210.summary: This is a dedication to the god Hercules Augustus, made by Satulius, likely a private religious offering from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-14400216.text_edition: \n Isidi Augustae sacrum Caius Iulius Onesimus et Pompeia Chronia et Iulia Ingenua filia votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-14400216.translation: To Isis Augusta. Gaius Julius Onesimus, Pompeia Chronia, and Julia Ingenua, daughter, fulfilled their vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-14400216.summary: This is a religious dedication to the goddess Isis Augusta, where Gaius Julius Onesimus and his family fulfilled a vow, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-14400224.text_edition: \n Vibebos Augustis sacrum Gaius Decius Successianus aedilicia potestate Claudi Viruni et Vibenia Masculina pro se et suis votum solverunt laeti libentes merito\n
EDCS-14400224.translation: To the Augusti. Gaius Decius Successianus, holding the office of aedile, and Claudius Viron and Vibenia Masculina, for themselves and their family, fulfilled their vow happily and willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-14400224.summary: This is a religious dedication to the deified emperors (Augusti), made by Gaius Decius Successianus and a family, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-14400225.text_edition: \n Murcius Sabinianus libens laetus votum solvit Vibebos\n
EDCS-14400225.translation: Murcius Sabinianus willingly and happily fulfilled his vow to the Augusti.
EDCS-14400225.summary: This is a religious dedication to the deified emperors (Augusti), where Murcius Sabinianus fulfilled a vow, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-14400226.text_edition: \n Casuontano L T C N S vic votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400226.translation: Casuontanus, son of Lucius, T. Claudius's grandson, willingly paid his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-14400226.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Casuontanus.

EDCS-14400228.text_edition: \n Attucio Adnamati filio et Attuciae Veterani filiae uxori filii vivi fecerunt et Sammae sorori\n
EDCS-14400228.translation: Sons living made this for their father Attucius, son of Adnamatus, and for their mother Attucia, daughter of Veteranus, wife of Attucius, and for their sister Samma.
EDCS-14400228.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by sons for their father, mother, and sister.

EDCS-14400229.summary: This is a funerary monument or dedication by Aulus Iulius Firmus.

EDCS-14400231.text_edition: \n Belesti Augustae sacrum Latinus Tapponi Macrini servus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400231.translation: Latinus, slave of Macrinus, of Tappo, willingly paid his vow to sacred Augusta, as was deserved.
EDCS-14400231.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Augusta by Latinus, a freedman.

EDCS-14400232.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Publius Aelius Verinus beneficiarius consularis pro se et suis votum solvit libens laetus merito Victorino et Severo consulibus\n
EDCS-14400232.translation: Publius Aelius Verinus, a legionar officer's aide, willingly and joyfully paid his vow for himself and his family to Jupiter Best and Greatest, as was deserved, under the consulship of Victorinus and Severus.
EDCS-14400232.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Publius Aelius Verinus, an imperial freedman, during the consulship of Victorinus and Severus.

EDCS-14400233.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Calventinius Maternus beneficiarius Claudi Prisciani procuratoris Augusti votum solvit merito Paullo II et Aproniano II consulibus\n
EDCS-14400233.translation: Calventinius Maternus, an imperial procurator's aide under Claudius Priscianus, willingly paid his vow, as was deserved, under the consulship of Paullus II and Apronianus II.
EDCS-14400233.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Calventinius Maternus, a freedman serving as an aide to an imperial procurator, during the consulship of Paullus II and Apronianus II.

EDCS-14400236.summary: This inscription likely records the name Successus, possibly as a dedicant or the deceased.

EDCS-14400238.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Valerio Tertullino aedili Claudia Teurnia Caius Valerius Tertullus et Rettia Iulia filio @(obito) annorum XXV et sibi vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-14400238.translation: To the departed spirits. Claudia Teurnia, Caius Valerius Tertullus, and Rettia Iulia made this for their son Cai Valerius Tertullinus, aedile, who died at 25 years of age, and for themselves while living.
EDCS-14400238.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by parents for their deceased son, Cai Valerius Tertullinus, who held the office of aedile.

EDCS-14400245.text_edition: \n Restitutius IIvir iure dicundi sic Claudio Viruno et Helvia Cai filia Attica pro salute Cai Restituti Ripani fili votum solverunt libentes laeti merito\n
EDCS-14400245.translation: Restitutius, holding the office of duumvir with judicial authority, and his wife Helvia, daughter of Caius, Attica, paid their vow for the health of Cai Restitutus Ripanus, son of Caius, willingly and joyfully, as was deserved.
EDCS-14400245.summary: This inscription records a vow made by a duumvir, Restitutius, and his wife for the health of a relative, Cai Restitutus Ripanus.

EDCS-14400251.text_edition: \n Mansueto et Cogitatae Fabiani servis et Samtuni Mandatius Secundio fratri optimo\n
EDCS-14400251.translation: Fabianus's slaves, Mansuetus and Cogitata, [made this] for their excellent brother Samtunus, Mandatius Secundio.
EDCS-14400251.summary: This monument was erected by slaves Mansuetus and Cogitata for their brother Mandatius Secundio.

EDCS-14400255.text_edition: \n Quintus Volusius Secundinus Volusio Civi et Anniae Uttilae parentibus et Volusio Accepto filio defuncto annorum XIII vivus fecit et sibi et Optatiae Tertullae coniugi\n
EDCS-14400255.translation: Quintus Volusius Secundinus erected this for his parents Volusia Civia and Annia Uttila, and for his son Volusius Acceptus, who died at the age of 13, and for himself and his wife Optatia Tertulla.
EDCS-14400255.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Quintus Volusius Secundinus for his deceased parents, son, himself, and wife.

EDCS-14400259.text_edition: \n Genio Cucullato sacrum Caius Iuventius Speratus et Cornelia Secundina votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-14400259.translation: Gaius Iuventius Speratus and Cornelia Secundina gladly and deservedly fulfilled their vow to the hooded god.
EDCS-14400259.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow made to the god Cucullatus by Gaius Iuventius Speratus and Cornelia Secundina.

EDCS-14400261.text_edition: \n Quintus Octavius Masculus vivus fecit sibi et Macriniae Secundinae coniugi\n
EDCS-14400261.translation: Quintus Octavius Masculus erected this for himself and his wife Macrinia Secundina.
EDCS-14400261.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Quintus Octavius Masculus for himself and his wife.

EDCS-14400262.text_edition: \n Clevius Rusci filius sibi et Elvissae Maticonis filiae coniugi vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14400262.translation: Clevius Rusci erected this for himself and his wife Elvissa Maticonis.
EDCS-14400262.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Clevius Rusci for himself and his wife.

EDCS-14400265.text_edition: \n Panenti Lavi filio Dasto Nepotis marito et sibi\n
EDCS-14400265.translation: For Panentius, son of Lasius, husband of Dasto Nepotis, and for himself.
EDCS-14400265.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Panentius, son of Lasius, and husband of Dasto Nepotis, and also for the dedicator.

EDCS-14400269.text_edition: \n IM RI no filio annorum XX\n
EDCS-14400269.summary: This is a brief funerary inscription for an unnamed son who died at the age of 20.

EDCS-14400270.text_edition: \n Optata sibi et Buccioni filio annorum XXV\n
EDCS-14400270.translation: Optata for herself and for her son Buccio, aged 25.
EDCS-14400270.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Optata for herself and her son Buccio.

EDCS-14400271.text_edition: \n Spectatae Tertullina matri et Macri filius miles cohortis II Batavorum \n
EDCS-14400271.translation: Spectata Tertullina for her mother, and her son, a soldier of the II Cohort of Batavians.
EDCS-14400271.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Spectata Tertullina for her mother and her son, who was a soldier.

EDCS-14400273.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Conservatori Publius Aelius Senecianus et Petronia Silvana filia votum solverunt libentes laeti merito pro se et suis\n
EDCS-14400273.translation: Publius Aelius Senecianus and his daughter Petronia Silvana gladly and joyfully fulfilled their vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Preserver, for themselves and their family.
EDCS-14400273.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter by Publius Aelius Senecianus and his daughter Petronia Silvana on behalf of their family.

EDCS-14400274.text_edition: \n Saetubolo Diastulli filio et Talsae Bremiatis filiae uxsori testamento fieri iussit\n
EDCS-14400274.translation: He ordered this to be made by his will for Saetubolus, son of Diastullus, and for his wife Talsa, daughter of Bremiatis.
EDCS-14400274.summary: This inscription records a posthumous dedication, ordered by will, for Saetubolus and his wife Talsa.

EDCS-14400275.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Campili Veri conductoris ferrariarum Fortunatus vilicus aquam perduxit\n
EDCS-14400275.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, for the safety of Campilius Verus, manager of the iron mines, Fortunatus, the overseer, brought the water.
EDCS-14400275.summary: This inscription records the construction of an aqueduct by Fortunatus, an overseer, dedicated to Jupiter for the well-being of the mine manager, Campilius Verus.

EDCS-14400276.summary: This inscription is a very brief funerary or dedicatory marker, identifying a freedman of a woman named Ms. Caae.

EDCS-14400277.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Bellicianus et Postumia Septumina vivi fecerunt sibi et Aelio Urso filio obito annorum VIIII\n
EDCS-14400277.translation: To the departed spirits. Aelius Bellicianus and Postumia Septumina, while alive, made this for themselves and for their son Aelius Ursus, who died at the age of nine.
EDCS-14400277.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument erected by Aelius Bellicianus and Postumia Septumina for themselves and their deceased son Aelius Ursus.

EDCS-14400278.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary dedication or epitaph, possibly for a freedman of a man with the praenomen Julius.

EDCS-14400280.translation: Allo, son of Adgelei, and Coma.
EDCS-14400280.summary: This inscription names Allo, son of Adgelei, and Coma, likely recording a joint dedication or familial mention.

EDCS-14400281.text_edition: \n Caius Sedatius Ca Catroniae Optatae et C\n
EDCS-14400281.translation: Gaius Sedatius [erected this] for Ca Catronia Optata and C[...].
EDCS-14400281.summary: This fragmentary inscription records a dedication or funerary monument by Gaius Sedatius for Ca Catronia Optata and another individual.

EDCS-14400282.text_edition: \n Atia Ate fecit Uc filio et sibi et Matum Tat\n
EDCS-14400282.translation: Atia Ate made [this] for Uc, her son, and for herself, and for Matum Tat[...].
EDCS-14400282.summary: This inscription records a monument made by Atia Ate for her son Uc, herself, and another individual, possibly her husband.

EDCS-14400283.text_edition: \n Quintus Valerius Valeri libertus Crispus vivus fecit sibi et Cattae matri et Fuscae uxori et Primae Crispi filiae\n
EDCS-14400283.translation: Quintus Valerius Crispus, freedman of Valerius, made [this] while alive for himself, his mother Catta, his wife Fusca, and his daughter Prima Crispi.
EDCS-14400283.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument made by Quintus Valerius Crispus for himself and his female family members: mother, wife, and daughter.

EDCS-14400285.text_edition: \n Restuta Solicuri viva fecit et sibi et Secundo filio et Covnerto filio et Cemenuni coniugi\n
EDCS-14400285.translation: Restuta Solicuri made [this] while alive for herself, her son Secundus, her son Covnertus, and her husband Cemenunus.
EDCS-14400285.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument made by Restuta Solicuri for herself and her male family members: two sons and her husband.

EDCS-14400286.text_edition: \n Atuconi Glabrionis filio et Rumae Rudsci filiae uxori filii fecerunt\n
EDCS-14400286.translation: His sons made [this] for Atuconius, son of Glabrio, and for Ruma, wife of Rudscius.
EDCS-14400286.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument erected by sons for their father, Atuconius, son of Glabrio, and their mother, Ruma.

EDCS-14400288.text_edition: \n Safrio Tamaco Quarta ma<t=I>er viva fecit \n
EDCS-14400288.translation: Safrio Tamaco made this while his mother Quarta was alive.
EDCS-14400288.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Safrio Tamaco, dated by the mention of his mother being alive.

EDCS-14400289.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus consul III pater patriae sacerdos amplissimus et Marcus Aurelius Alexander Caesar\n
EDCS-14400289.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, consul for the third time, father of the country, most revered priest, and Marcus Aurelius Alexander Caesar.
EDCS-14400289.summary: This text records the names and titles of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Caesar Marcus Aurelius Alexander, likely part of a larger official inscription.

EDCS-14400293.translation: Titus Flavius Aelianus, a performer of Homeric recitations.
EDCS-14400293.summary: This inscription identifies Titus Flavius Aelianus as a Homerista, likely on a tombstone or honorary monument.

EDCS-14400294.text_edition: \n Genio Norecorum sic Crispinius Adiutor votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400294.translation: Crispinius Adiutor willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the Genius of the Noreci.
EDCS-14400294.summary: This is a votive inscription by Crispinius Adiutor to the Genius of the Noreci, likely placed at a shrine.

EDCS-14400295.translation: Caius Secundius [dedicated this] to the Genius of the Norici.
EDCS-14400295.summary: This is a dedication to the Genius of the Norici by Caius Secundius.

EDCS-14400297.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo iussus sic Felix Auggustorum libertus tabularius\n
EDCS-14400297.translation: By order, Felix, freedman of the Augusti and archivist, [dedicated this] to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-14400297.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Best and Greatest by Felix, an archivist and freedman of the emperors.

EDCS-14400299.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Egronius Primianus et Aelia Valeria ex voto posuit sic\n
EDCS-14400299.translation: Egronius Primianus and Aelia Valeria set this up as vowed to Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus.
EDCS-14400299.summary: This is a votive inscription by Egronius Primianus and Aelia Valeria to Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus.

EDCS-14400311.text_edition: \n NFERE Ursulus filius vivus fecit sibi \n
EDCS-14400311.translation: Ursulus, son of Nferius, made this for himself while alive.
EDCS-14400311.summary: This is a funerary monument created by Ursulus for himself during his lifetime.

EDCS-14400316.text_edition: \n Quintus Curius Quinti filius Iunianus et Curia Sexti filia Quarta uxor vivi fecerunt sibi et Quinto Iunio Iuniano filio nnum V\n
EDCS-14400316.translation: Quintus Curius Iunianus, son of Quintus, and his wife Curia Quarta, daughter of Sextus, made this for themselves and for their son Quintus Iunius Iunianus, aged five.
EDCS-14400316.summary: This is a funerary monument created by Quintus Curius Iunianus and his wife Curia Quarta for themselves and their son.

EDCS-14400317.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Successae Claudi Quintiliani ancillae Vibius filius matri fecit\n
EDCS-14400317.translation: To the Divine Manes: Vibius made this for his mother, Successa, slave of Claudius Quintilianus.
EDCS-14400317.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Vibius to his mother, Successa, who was a slave.

EDCS-14400318.text_edition: \n Nardinae annorum V et Secundae annorum XV Casimae annorum XX \n
EDCS-14400318.translation: To Nardina, aged 5, and Secunda, aged 15, and Casima, aged 20.
EDCS-14400318.summary: This inscription lists three women with their ages, likely part of a funerary monument.

EDCS-14400319.text_edition: \n Quartus Caiani servus vivus fecit sibi et Sisiuni coniugi optimae et Tertullo filio annorum XXV\n
EDCS-14400319.translation: Quartus, a living slave of Caius, made this for himself, his excellent wife Sisiunia, and his son Tertullus, aged 25.
EDCS-14400319.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a living slave for himself and his family, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-14400320.text_edition: \n Excingo Magiovindi filius Excingomaro filio et suis vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14400320.translation: Excingo, son of Magiovindus, made this for his son Excingomarus and his family while alive.
EDCS-14400320.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Excingo for his son and family, indicating Gallic influence in naming conventions.

EDCS-14400321.text_edition: \n Iulius Victor vivus fecit sibi et Sisiae coniugi et Restuto Persici filio annorum XV\n
EDCS-14400321.translation: Julius Victor made this for himself, his wife Sisia, and Restutus, son of Persicus, aged 15, while alive.
EDCS-14400321.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a living individual for himself, his wife, and a young relative, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-14400322.text_edition: \n vivus fecit et sibi io filio et Viatori nepoti\n
EDCS-14400322.translation: He made this for himself, his son Io, and his grandson Viator, while alive.
EDCS-14400322.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a living individual for himself, his son, and grandson.

EDCS-14400326.text_edition: \n Martia Teti filia annorum XXXX Masculus Secundi filius annorum XXXXV Martia Teti filia annorum XXXX\n
EDCS-14400326.translation: Martia, daughter of Tetus, aged 40; Masculus, son of Secundus, aged 45; Martia, daughter of Tetus, aged 40.
EDCS-14400326.summary: This inscription lists three individuals with their ages and parentage, likely part of a funerary monument.

EDCS-14400330.text_edition: \n Cemeia Tromperi nnorum XXXX hic sita est\n
EDCS-14400330.translation: Cemeia, daughter of Tromperus, aged 40, is buried here.
EDCS-14400330.summary: This is a funerary inscription stating the name and age of the deceased, Cemeia.

EDCS-14400331.text_edition: \n Cocinae Aiunis libertae annorum Successa LII Success-- liberta vixit annos L\n
EDCS-14400331.translation: To Cocina, freedwoman of Aius, aged 52. Successa, freedwoman, lived 50 years.
EDCS-14400331.summary: This inscription records the ages of two freedwomen, possibly related.

EDCS-14400333.text_edition: \n Blaus Camai vixit annos IXX Saurus Atvorti filius nnrum L et Blaus Cadiasi filius derus -- libertus annorum \n
EDCS-14400333.translation: Blaus, son of Camaius, lived 70 years. Saurus, son of Atuortus, aged 50, and Blaus, son of Cadisius, a freedman, aged [---].
EDCS-14400333.summary: This inscription lists the ages of at least three individuals, including a freedman, possibly on a shared tomb.

EDCS-14400335.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavius Surio annorum Valeria Ingenua coniunx viva fecit sibi et \n
EDCS-14400335.translation: To the departed spirits. Flavius Surius, aged [---]. His wife Valeria Ingenua, alive, made this for herself and [---].
EDCS-14400335.summary: This funerary inscription begins with a dedication to the departed spirits and records a man's name and age, with his wife setting it up.

EDCS-14400336.text_edition: \n Aurelius Docimus miles legionis II Italicae piae fidelis et Aurelia Matura vivi sibi fecerunt et Aurelio Claudio filio obito nnrum XIIII\n
EDCS-14400336.translation: Aurelius Docimus, a soldier of the Second Italian Legion, loyal and faithful, and Aurelia Matura, while alive, made this for themselves and for their deceased son Aurelius Claudius, aged 14.
EDCS-14400336.summary: This is a tombstone erected by a soldier and his wife for their son, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-14400337.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Quartinus miles alae primae Commagenorum obitus annorum L Dubitatia Saturnina viva fecit sibi et coniugi\n
EDCS-14400337.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Quartinus, a soldier of the First Commagenian Ala, aged 50, died. Dubitatia Saturnina, while alive, made this for herself and her husband.
EDCS-14400337.summary: This is a tombstone set up by a soldier's wife for herself and her deceased husband, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-14400338.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Ulpius Adiutor pro se et suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400338.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Ulpius Adiutor fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly for himself and his family.
EDCS-14400338.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter by Ulpius Adiutor, likely a private votive offering, dating from the 1st to 3rd centuries AD.

EDCS-14400340.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Pompeio Celeri militi cohortis I Flaviae Brittonum annorum XXV Maximus co<h=R>eres faciendum curavit \n
EDCS-14400340.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Maximus, the co-heir, arranged for this to be made for Pompeius Celer, a soldier of the First Flavian Cohort of Britons, aged 25.
EDCS-14400340.summary: This tombstone was erected by a co-heir for a deceased soldier of the First Flavian Cohort of Britons, likely from the late 1st to 3rd centuries AD.

EDCS-14400341.text_edition: \n annorum XXII et Ulpi-- Marci fili-- \n
EDCS-14400341.translation: aged 22 and Ulpius, son of Marcus...
EDCS-14400341.summary: This fragment is part of a tombstone, likely for a young person, with a lacuna for the name and possibly family information, dating from the 1st to 3rd centuries AD.

EDCS-14400342.text_edition: \n Cai Ausoni Sergia Silvini annorum XXX Ingenuus titulum faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-14400342.translation: Caius Ausonius's Sergia Silvina, aged 30. Ingenuus arranged for this inscription to be made.
EDCS-14400342.summary: This is a tombstone for Sergia Silvina, erected by Ingenuus, likely from the 1st to 3rd centuries AD.

EDCS-14400343.text_edition: \n Genio sacrum Ulpius Liberalis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400343.translation: Sacred to the Genius. Ulpius Liberalis fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-14400343.summary: This is a private votive dedication to the Genius of a place or person, made by Ulpius Liberalis, likely from the 1st to 3rd centuries AD.

EDCS-14400345.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Cupitus veteranus et Cupita coniunx vivi fecerunt sibi et Aureli- \n
EDCS-14400345.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Cupitus, a veteran, and his wife Cupita, while alive, made this for themselves and for Aurelius...
EDCS-14400345.summary: This is a tombstone erected by a veteran and his wife for themselves and at least one other Aurelius, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-14400347.text_edition: \n Troucleimarus Demari filius eques alae Thracum annorum XXXXV stipendiorum XXVI collegae pro pietate \n
EDCS-14400347.translation: Troucleimarus, son of Demarus, an equestrian of the Thracian Ala, aged 45, with 26 years of service, [honored by] his colleagues for his devotion.
EDCS-14400347.summary: This inscription honors a cavalryman of the Thracian Ala, likely erected by his fellow soldiers, dating from the 1st to 3rd centuries AD.

EDCS-14400350.text_edition: \n Maveta Veregundi sic filia annorum III hic sita est Romana mater posuit\n
EDCS-14400350.translation: Maveta, daughter of Verecundus, aged 3, lies here. Her Roman mother placed this.
EDCS-14400350.summary: This is a tombstone for a young girl named Maveta, erected by her mother, likely from the 1st to 3rd centuries AD.

EDCS-14400351.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Gaius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus pontif<e=I>x maximus tribunicia potestate II consul pater patriae proconsul et Gaius Iulius Verus Maximus nobilissimus Caesar princeps iuventutis ddomini indulgentissimi pontes refecerunt vias munierunt miliaria restituerunt a Cetio milia passuum XXII \n
EDCS-14400351.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the second time, consul, father of the country, proconsul, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, our most indulgent rulers, rebuilt bridges, constructed roads, and restored milestones for 22 miles from Cetius.
EDCS-14400351.summary: This inscription records public works, including bridges, roads, and milestones, undertaken under the emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Maximus, dating to the period of Maximinus's second tribunician power (c. 236-238 AD).

EDCS-14400355.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Aurelio Vero Augusto tribunicia potestate III consuli II cohors II Thracum equitata pia fidelis\n
EDCS-14400355.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, with tribunician power for the third time, consul for the second time, the Second Cohort of Thracians, cavalry, pious and faithful.
EDCS-14400355.summary: This is a dedication by the Second Cohort of Thracians to Emperor Lucius Aurelius Verus, likely during his third tribunate and second consulship (c. 166-169 AD).

EDCS-14400359.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Quartinus cornicen legionis II Italicae piae fidelis vivus fecit sibi et Aureliae Crispinae coniugi vivae fecit et Aeliae Quartinae filliae @(obitae) annorum XIIII et filiis duobus vivis fecit\n
EDCS-14400359.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aelius Quartinus, horn-blower of the Second Italian Legion, pious and faithful, made this himself while alive for himself and for his wife Aurelia Crispine, alive when it was made, and for Aelia Quartina, his daughter who died at the age of 14, and for his two living sons.
EDCS-14400359.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Aelius Quartinus for himself and his family, including his deceased daughter, from the Second Italian Legion.

EDCS-14400360.text_edition: \n Tertinius Terentius vivus fecit sibi et Rufiae Helpidi coniugi et Tertio nnorum CX et Respectae nnorum LXXXX parentibus Tertinia Grata filia bene merentibus scribendum curavit\n
EDCS-14400360.translation: Tertinius Terentius, while alive, made this for himself and for his wife Rufia Helpidi, and for his parents Tertius (aged 110) and Respecta (aged 90). His daughter Tertinia Grata saw to the inscription of this due to their well-deserving nature.
EDCS-14400360.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Tertinius Terentius for himself, his wife, and his exceptionally aged parents, with the inscription commissioned by his daughter.

EDCS-14400362.text_edition: \n Legionis II Italicae aras principatu suo Tabellius Victoris faciendas curavit\n
EDCS-14400362.translation: During his command, Tabellius Victor saw to the construction of altars for the Second Italian Legion.
EDCS-14400362.summary: This inscription records Tabellius Victor undertaking the construction of altars for the Second Italian Legion during his command.

EDCS-14400363.translation: Aurelius Successus, a soldier of the Legion.
EDCS-14400363.summary: This is a very brief inscription identifying Aurelius Successus as a soldier of an unnamed legion.

EDCS-14400365.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Tiberius Claudius Soni filius Provincialis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400365.translation: Tiberius Claudius Provincialis, son of Soni, fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus freely and deservedly.
EDCS-14400365.summary: This is a votive inscription by Tiberius Claudius Provincialis to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-14400368.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius.
EDCS-14400368.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription mentioning Aurelius.

EDCS-14400369.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Mogiancus.
EDCS-14400369.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription mentioning Mogiancus.

EDCS-14400370.text_edition: \n Genio Cai Domiti Montani Septani Romani praefecti alae I Pannoniorum Tampianae victricis Castricius Sabinus duplicarius alae eiusdem aram consecravit votum libens merito solvit\n
EDCS-14400370.translation: To the Genius of Caius Domitius Montanus Septanius Romanus, prefect of the First Pannonian Wing Tampiana Victrix, Castricius Sabinus, duplicarius of the same wing, dedicated this altar, fulfilling his vow freely and deservedly.
EDCS-14400370.summary: This is a dedication of an altar by Castricius Sabinus, a duplicarius of the First Pannonian Wing, to the Genius of his prefect, Caius Domitius Montanus Septanius Romanus.

EDCS-14400375.text_edition: \n Aureliae Alexandriae @(obitae) annorum XXV et Aureliae Marcellinae filiae Aurelius Eutiches sexvir Augustalis coniugi carissimae vivus \n
EDCS-14400375.translation: To Aurelia Alexandria, who died at the age of 25, and to her daughter Aurelia Marcellina, Aurelius Eutiches, a *sexvir Augustalis*, erected this for his dearest wife, while he was alive.
EDCS-14400375.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a husband and father, Aurelius Eutiches, to his deceased wife and daughter, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400377.text_edition: \n In<g=C>enua anno--- L Publius I Ovilavensium libert-? \n
EDCS-14400377.translation: In Genoa, in the year [---] L, Publius [---] of the freedmen of Ovilia [---].
EDCS-14400377.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription from Genoa, possibly recording a freedman's name and status, with an incomplete date.

EDCS-14400378.text_edition: \n Adiutor Seniunis vivus et Martiae coniugi eius obitae annorum IXXV \n
EDCS-14400378.translation: Adiutor Seniunis, while he was alive, and to his wife Martia, who died at the age of 75.
EDCS-14400378.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Adiutor Seniunis for himself and his deceased wife Martia.

EDCS-14400379.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Pontiae Pompeianae filiae @(obitae) annorum VII Gaius Pontius Pompeianus |(centurio) legionis II Italicae piae fidelis <e=F>t Servilia Vestina parentes fecerunt \n
EDCS-14400379.translation: To the Divine Shades: Gaius Pontius Pompeianus, a centurion of the II Italian Legion, pious and loyal, and Servilia Vestina, his parents, made this for their daughter Pontia Pompeiana, who died at the age of 7.
EDCS-14400379.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young girl, Pontia Pompeiana, erected by her parents, one of whom is a centurion in the II Italian Legion, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400386.translation: Aurelius Surus, a soldier, to the genius of the century.
EDCS-14400386.summary: This inscription records a dedication by a soldier named Aurelius Surus to the genius of his military century.

EDCS-14400387.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Aurelius Marinus vir perfectissimus cum suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400387.translation: Marcus Aurelius Marinus, a most distinguished man, fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest, willingly and deservedly, with his household.
EDCS-14400387.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus Aurelius Marinus, a high-ranking official, to Jupiter, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400388.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Minervae Augustae ceterisque dis deabusque Aelius Restutus vir perfectissimus agens vice praesidis votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-14400388.translation: Aelius Restutus, a most distinguished man, acting in the capacity of governor, fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest, Juno Regina, Minerva Augusta, and all the other gods and goddesses, willingly, joyfully, and deservedly.
EDCS-14400388.summary: This is a comprehensive votive inscription by Aelius Restutus, acting governor, to the Capitoline Triad and other deities, expressing gratitude and fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-14400389.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Conservatori dis deabusque omnibus Aurelius Severus \n
EDCS-14400389.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Protector, and to all the gods and goddesses, Aurelius Severus [made this].
EDCS-14400389.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aurelius Severus dedicated to Jupiter as Protector and all other gods and goddesses.

EDCS-14400390.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo tutori Marius Messorianus miles legionis pro se et omnibus suis votum solvit laetus libens merito\n
EDCS-14400390.translation: Marius Messorianus, a soldier of the [---] legion, fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest, the protector, for himself and all his family, joyfully, willingly, and deservedly.
EDCS-14400390.summary: This is a votive inscription by a soldier, Marius Messorianus, to Jupiter as protector for himself and his family.

EDCS-14400394.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly a dedication or prayer for the health of a woman named Attia.

EDCS-14400398.text_edition: \n Claudio Augusto Domitio Celeri Respectio Vitali Aurelio Apolau<s>to Aurelio Onesimo Sattio Marciano Claudio Iuliano A M A I \n
EDCS-14400398.translation: To Claudio Augusto Domitio Celer, Respectio Vitalis, Aurelius Apollaus, Aurelius Onesimus, Sattius Marcianus, Claudio Iulianus, a soldier of the Emperor, of the Augustan Guard, a member of the imperial administration.
EDCS-14400398.summary: This is a dedication to several individuals, likely officials or associates of Claudio Augusto Domitio Celer, possibly related to imperial administration.

EDCS-14400399.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Capitonio Urso @(obito) annorum XII Aurelius Capitonius candidatus legionis II Italicae \n
EDCS-14400399.translation: To the departed spirits: Aurelius Capitonius, a candidate in the II Italian Legion, dedicated this for Capitonia Ursa, who died at the age of twelve.
EDCS-14400399.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Aurelius Capitonius for his twelve-year-old daughter Capitonia Ursa, a soldier's child.

EDCS-14400401.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely the name of a woman, possibly a funerary epitaph.

EDCS-14400405.text_edition: \n Crispiae coniugi obitae annorum XL Saecularis Sabiniae Dignae servus vivus fecit sibi et suis\n
EDCS-14400405.translation: A slave, Saecularis Sabinia Digna, who is alive, made this for his wife Crispia, who died at the age of forty, for himself and his family.
EDCS-14400405.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a slave for his deceased wife, Crispia, and for himself and his family.

EDCS-14400408.text_edition: \n Herculi sacrum votum pro Quinto et suis Hermes posuit\n
EDCS-14400408.translation: Sacred to Hercules. Hermes dedicated this vow for Quintus and his family.
EDCS-14400408.summary: This is a dedication of a votive offering to Hercules by Hermes for Quintus and his family.

EDCS-14400409.text_edition: \n Victoriae Augustae Verbicius vilicus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400409.translation: Verbicius, the farm manager, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Victory Augusta.
EDCS-14400409.summary: This is a votive dedication to the goddess Victoria Augusta by Verbicius, a farm manager.

EDCS-14400410.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iulius Florinus ex voto pro se et suiis sic libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-14400410.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Iulius Florinus, as vowed, willingly and joyfully and deservedly, for himself and his family.
EDCS-14400410.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Iulius Florinus for himself and his family.

EDCS-14400412.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly a dedication to Vulcan and Augustus.

EDCS-14400413.text_edition: \n Marco Sacconio Parato annorum LX patrono optimo et Marco Antonio Clementi annorum LV coniugi karissimo et Rucconi filio annorum III Sacconia Docilis fecit sibi vivae et Vitali et Festo filiis vivis\n
EDCS-14400413.translation: Sacconia Docilis, alive, made this for Marcus Sacconius Paratus, aged sixty, her excellent patron, and for Marcus Antonius Clemens, aged fifty-five, her dearest husband, and for Rucconius, her son, aged three, and for herself, and for her sons Vitalis and Festus, who are alive.
EDCS-14400413.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Sacconia Docilis for her patron, her husband, her deceased son, and for herself and her living sons.

EDCS-14400414.text_edition: \n Ulpia Pacata viva fecit sibi et Iulio Successo coniugi @(obito) annorum LV et Ulpiis Optato filio @(obito) annorum XXX et Cogitato filio @(obito) annorum XXV\n
EDCS-14400414.translation: Ulpia Pacata, alive, made this for herself, and for her husband Iulius Successus, who died at the age of fifty-five, and for her son Ulpius Optatus, who died at the age of thirty, and for her son Cogitatus, who died at the age of twenty-five.
EDCS-14400414.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Ulpia Pacata for herself and her deceased husband and two deceased sons.

EDCS-14400415.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Chartius Tagaduni eques alae Augustae singularis natione Tunger annorum XXXV Valens Ianuari heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-14400415.translation: To the Divine Shades. Valens Ianuari, heir, saw to the making of this monument for Chartius Tagadunus, a trooper of the Augustan cavalry, a Tungrian by birth, aged 35.
EDCS-14400415.summary: This is a funerary monument for a Roman cavalry trooper named Chartius Tagadunus, erected by his heir.

EDCS-14400423.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vicciae Gai filiae Severae obitae annorum XXXII coniugi karissimae Caius Iulius Pontianus et sibi et suis vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14400423.translation: To the Divine Shades. Caius Iulius Pontianus made this for Viccia Severa, daughter of Gaius, who died at age 32, his dearest wife, and for himself and his family, during his lifetime.
EDCS-14400423.summary: This is a funerary monument for Viccia Severa, erected by her husband Caius Iulius Pontianus for himself and his family.

EDCS-14400428.text_edition: \n Marci fili-- IIvir iure dicundo ri \n
EDCS-14400428.translation: Son of Marcus... IIvir for dispensing justice...
EDCS-14400428.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription referring to a man named Marcus who held the office of IIvir iure dicundo.

EDCS-14400430.text_edition: \n ina ellius Candidus [] [] Primus posuerunt libentes laeti merito dono dederunt die Nonis Septembribus\n
EDCS-14400430.translation: ...and his wife Candidus [] [] Primus erected this willingly, gladly, and deservedly as a gift on the Ides of September.
EDCS-14400430.summary: This incomplete inscription records the dedication of a gift by Candidus and Primus on a specific date.

EDCS-14400431.text_edition: \n Genio domini Paratus di Rufi servus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400431.translation: Paratus, a slave of Rufus, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the genius of his master.
EDCS-14400431.summary: This is a votive inscription by a slave named Paratus to the genius of his master.

EDCS-14400432.text_edition: \n Volusiae Cai filiae Saturninae nnorum XVI Volusia Latina soror et Caius Vitorius Cupitus vir sibi et Cleuvoni Veitoris filio patri Voltisemae Bussumari filiae matri Vitorio Latino filio Vitoriae Secundinae filiae vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-14400432.translation: Volusia Latina, sister, and Caius Vitorius Cupitus, husband, made this during their lifetime for Volusia Saturnina, daughter of Caius, aged 16, and for themselves and for Cleuvonus Veitoris, son of Veitoris, father, and Voltisema Bussumari, daughter of Bussumar, mother, and Vitorius Latinus, son, and Vitoria Secundina, daughter.
EDCS-14400432.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Volusia Latina and Caius Vitorius Cupitus for Volusia Saturnina, and also for their parents and themselves.

EDCS-14400434.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription likely naming a person, possibly Decimus Opetrius.

EDCS-14400437.text_edition: \n Marco Iulio Philippo nobilissimo Caesari ab Agunto milia passuum IIII\n
EDCS-14400437.translation: Four miles from Marcus Julius Philippus, most noble Caesar, from Aguntum.
EDCS-14400437.summary: This inscription marks a distance of four miles from Aguntum to Marcus Julius Philippus, a noble Caesar, likely related to a road or boundary marker.

EDCS-14400438.text_edition: \n Dacus Coponi Luciani servus sibi et Matronae coniugi obitae nnum XXXX et suis vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14400438.translation: Dacus, a slave of Coponius Lucianus, made this during his lifetime for himself and for his wife Matrona, who died at age 40, and for his family.
EDCS-14400438.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a slave named Dacus for himself, his deceased wife Matrona, and his family.

EDCS-14400439.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et dis deabusque omnibus Turbonius Fuscinus beneficiarius consularis legionis II Italicae piae fidelis pro se et suis votum solvit libens merito dominis nnostris Severo III et Antonino consulibus\n
EDCS-14400439.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to all the gods and goddesses, Turbonius Fuscinus, a staff officer of a consular legate of the II Italian Legion, pious and loyal, fulfilled his vow for himself and his family, gladly and deservedly, under our lords Severus III and Antoninus, consuls.
EDCS-14400439.summary: This is a votive inscription by Turbonius Fuscinus, an officer in the II Italian Legion, to Jupiter and other gods, dated to the consulship of Severus III and Antoninus.

EDCS-14400440.text_edition: \n Quintus Sabinius Euprepes vivus fecit sibi et Sabiniae Tertullae uxori optimae obitae nnorum XXXV\n
EDCS-14400440.translation: Quintus Sabinius Euprepes made this for himself and for his excellent wife Sabinia Tertulla, who died at the age of 35.
EDCS-14400440.summary: A funerary monument erected by Quintus Sabinius Euprepes for himself and his deceased wife, Sabinia Tertulla.

EDCS-14400443.text_edition: \n Fortunae Augustae pro salute Luci Pomponi Aquilini Potentis Florentina nutrix votum solvit libens laeta merito\n
EDCS-14400443.translation: For the August Empress, the nurse Florentina freed from her vow willingly, happily, and deservedly for the safety of Lucius Pomponius Aquilinus Potens.
EDCS-14400443.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Fortuna Augusta by a nurse for the well-being of Lucius Pomponius Aquilinus Potens.

EDCS-14400444.text_edition: \n Cocceio Cupito militi legionis II Italicae adiutori principis consularis @(obito) nnorum XXII stipendiorum VII Cocceius Proculus beneficiarius consularis fratri fecit\n
EDCS-14400444.translation: For Cocceius Cupitus, a soldier of the II Italica legion, assistant to the consular princeps, who died at the age of 22 after 7 years of service; his brother Cocceius Proculus, a beneficiary of the consul, made this.
EDCS-14400444.summary: A funerary monument erected by Cocceius Proculus for his brother, Cocceius Cupitus, a soldier in the II Italica legion.

EDCS-14400446.summary: A fragmentary inscription that appears to be a dedication or funerary monument to a person named Cupitia.

EDCS-14400447.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae pro salute Luci Albi Attici Crius curante Cattio Seio legato Deo Invicto Mithrae pro salute Luci A<l=I>bi Attici Crius rius curante Cattio Seio legato \n
EDCS-14400447.translation: For the Unconquered God Mithras, for the safety of Lucius Albius Atticus Crius, under the supervision of Cattius Seius, legate.
EDCS-14400447.summary: A dedication to the Unconquered God Mithras, made for the safety of Lucius Albius Atticus Crius under the supervision of the legate Cattius Seius.

EDCS-14400449.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Pio Felici Invicto Augusto patri patriae a Teurnia milia passuum XLV\n
EDCS-14400449.translation: From Teurnia, 45 Roman miles to Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus, the Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered, Augustus, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-14400449.summary: An inscription indicating a distance of 45 miles from Teurnia to the emperor Marcus Julius Philippus, likely on a road marker.

EDCS-14400451.text_edition: \n Lucius Vedius Optatus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400451.translation: Lucius Vedius Optatus freed from his vow willingly, and deservedly.
EDCS-14400451.summary: A votive inscription by Lucius Vedius Optatus, expressing the fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-14400453.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Gaius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate III consul II pater patriae proconsul et Gaius Iulius Verus Maximus nobilissimus Caesar princeps iuventutis domini indulgentissimi pontes refecerunt et vias munierunt et miliaria restituerunt mille passuum I\n
EDCS-14400453.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus, the Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered, Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, the most noble Caesar, prince of youth, their most indulgent lords, rebuilt the bridges, paved the roads, and restored the milestones for one Roman mile.
EDCS-14400453.summary: An inscription recording public works by Emperors Maximinus and his son Caesar Maximus, including bridge repair, road paving, and milestone restoration.

EDCS-14400454.text_edition: \n Marco Haterio Luci filio Claudia Summo decurioni municipii Iuvavi IIviro iure dicundo plebes sic oppidana optimo civi ob annonam relevatam\n
EDCS-14400454.translation: For Marcus Haterius, son of Lucius, of the Claudia tribe, to Claudia Summa, the finest citizen of the town of Iuvavum, duumvir with judicial power, for relieving the people of the grain shortage.
EDCS-14400454.summary: A dedication to Claudia Summa by Marcus Haterius for her service in alleviating a grain shortage in the municipality of Iuvavum.

EDCS-14400457.summary: A simple inscription likely commemorating an individual named Seccius Victor.

EDCS-14400459.translation: To the spirits of the underworld for Candidus.
EDCS-14400459.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated to the deceased Candidus, likely from a tomb or grave marker.

EDCS-14400460.text_edition: \n Profuturo vestigiatori Lolli Honorati obito nnorum XXX Barb et rina parentes et coniux vivi posuerunt\n
EDCS-14400460.translation: For Profuturus, architect of Lollius Honoratus, who died at the age of 30. His parents and wife, while alive, erected this.
EDCS-14400460.summary: A funerary epitaph for Profuturus, an architect, who died at 30, erected by his parents and wife.

EDCS-14400461.text_edition: \n Vindicio Verecundi vivus fecit sibi et Secundae coniugi et suis\n
EDCS-14400461.translation: Vindicius Verecundus made this for himself, his wife Secunda, and his family while he was alive.
EDCS-14400461.summary: A funerary monument erected by Vindicius Verecundus for himself, his wife Secunda, and his family.

EDCS-14400462.text_edition: \n io Ingenuo et filiis viva fecit Aurelia\n
EDCS-14400462.translation: Aurelia made this for Ingenuus and his sons while she was alive.
EDCS-14400462.summary: A funerary monument made by Aurelia for Ingenuus and his sons during her lifetime.

EDCS-14400465.text_edition: \n viva fecit sibi et marito et privigno obito annorum XV\n
EDCS-14400465.translation: She made this for herself, her husband, and her stepson, who died at the age of 15, while alive.
EDCS-14400465.summary: A funerary monument erected by a woman for herself, her husband, and her deceased stepson.

EDCS-14400468.text_edition: \n Ottus Devilli filius Bouda Momuci filia coniunx vivi filii fecerunt\n
EDCS-14400468.translation: Ottus, son of Devillus, and his wife Bouda, daughter of Momucus, made this while alive, along with their sons.
EDCS-14400468.summary: A funerary monument erected by Ottus and his wife Bouda, and their sons.

EDCS-14400470.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Moderatiae Ursulae -- @(obitae) annorum XLVIIII coniugi carissimae pientissimae et opsequentissimae sic Cupitius Cupitanus decurio civitatis et sibi et Cupitanus filius fecerunt\n
EDCS-14400470.translation: To the spirits of the underworld for Moderatia Ursula, who died at the age of 49, his most beloved, most dutiful, and most obedient wife, thus Cupitius Cupitanus, a decurion of the city, and his son Cupitanus made this for them and themselves.
EDCS-14400470.summary: A funerary epitaph for Moderatia Ursula, erected by her husband Cupitius Cupitanus and their son, dated by Cupitanus's decurionate.

EDCS-14400471.summary: A fragmentary inscription, possibly funerary, mentioning a woman named Titia Valentina.

EDCS-14400472.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Successus Summi et Accepta Accepti filia coniux eius vivi fecerunt sibi et Citato filio @(obito) annorum II\n
EDCS-14400472.translation: To the spirits of the underworld for Successus Summus and his wife Accepta, daughter of Acceptus, they made this while alive for themselves and their son Citatus, who died at the age of 2.
EDCS-14400472.summary: A funerary monument erected by Successus Summus and his wife Accepta for themselves and their young son Citatus.

EDCS-14400474.text_edition: \n Nimphis sic Geminis sacrum Gaius Annius Iuvenalis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400474.translation: Gaius Annius Iuvenalis willingly and deservedly paid his vow to the Twin Nymphs.
EDCS-14400474.summary: A votive inscription by Gaius Annius Iuvenalis to the Twin Nymphs.

EDCS-14400476.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a 15-year-old boy named Titius Vibianus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-14400478.text_edition: \n Avita Ingenui filia annorum et Avito filio annorum Crispinus vivus posuit\n
EDCS-14400478.translation: Crispinus, alive, erected [this monument] for Avita, daughter of Ingenuus, and for his son Avito.
EDCS-14400478.summary: A funerary monument erected by Crispinus for his wife Avita and son Avito, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-14400482.text_edition: \n Cupitus Cammi Sabini servus fecit sibi et Pri Vibiae coniugi annorum XXV et Samudae Cupiti filiae annorum II\n
EDCS-14400482.translation: Cupitus, slave of Cammius Sabinus, made [this monument] for himself and for his wife Pri, daughter of Vibius, aged 25, and for his daughter Samuda, daughter of Cupitus, aged 2.
EDCS-14400482.summary: A funerary monument created by Cupitus, a slave, for himself, his wife Pri, and his daughter Samuda, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-14400483.text_edition: \n Secundo Tecci filio et Tamisietae Avetae filiae et Avito filio annorum XXV heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-14400483.translation: His heirs had [this monument] made for Secundus, son of Teccius, and for Tamisieta, daughter of Aveta, and for his son Avitus, aged 25.
EDCS-14400483.summary: A funerary epitaph for Secundus, Tamisieta, and Avitus, whose heirs arranged for the monument's construction, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-14400484.summary: An incomplete epitaph for Covnertus, identified as a veteran, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-14400485.text_edition: \n filio annorum XV Respectus frater fecit\n
EDCS-14400485.translation: His brother Respectus made [this monument] for his son, aged 15.
EDCS-14400485.summary: A funerary monument erected by Respectus for his 15-year-old son, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-14400487.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Claudio Pio Felici invicto Augusto pontifici maximo patri patriae tribunicia potestate consuli procon suli ordo Solvensium\n
EDCS-14400487.translation: The council of the people of Solva [erected this] to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Claudius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, father of the country, holder of tribunician power, consul, proconsul.
EDCS-14400487.summary: An honorific inscription dedicated by the ordo of Solvensium to the Emperor Claudius Gothicus, dating to his reign (268-270 AD).

EDCS-14400488.text_edition: \n Caio Memmio Cavarino et Donniae Vennonis filiae matri et patri filii fecerunt\n
EDCS-14400488.translation: His sons made [this monument] for Gaius Memmius Cavarinus and for his mother Donnia Vennonis, and for his father.
EDCS-14400488.summary: A funerary monument erected by sons for their father Gaius Memmius Cavarinus and his mother Donnia Vennonis, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-14400489.text_edition: \n Honoratus annorum LXX et Materna coniux Finitus filius et Vibena coniux \n
EDCS-14400489.translation: Honoratus, aged 70, and his wife Materna, and their son Finitus, and his wife Vibena [erected this].
EDCS-14400489.summary: A funerary monument for Honoratus, his wife Materna, their son Finitus, and Finitus's wife Vibena, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-14400491.text_edition: \n Sammus Masculi filius annorum et Adnamiano Siricci sic filio annorum\n
EDCS-14400491.translation: Sammus, son of Masculus, aged [restored], and his son Adnamianus, son of Siriccius, aged [restored].
EDCS-14400491.summary: An incomplete funerary epitaph for Sammus, son of Masculus, and his son Adnamianus, son of Siriccius, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-14400492.text_edition: \n Itto Expediti et Nigella Cotelei et Covrunae filiae\n
EDCS-14400492.translation: Itto, son of Expeditius, and Nigella, daughter of Cottius, and to Covruna.
EDCS-14400492.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication or memorial to Itto, Nigella, and Covruna, possibly family members, with their patronymics indicating lineage. The exact nature and date are not specified.

EDCS-14400493.text_edition: \n Vi<t=I>ali Ursini Pinitu Satullaes sic liberta marito optimo et sibi fecit \n
EDCS-14400493.translation: Satullaes made this for Vitalis Ursinus of Pinitu, her freedman, her excellent husband, and for herself.
EDCS-14400493.summary: This epitaph records Satullaes setting up a tomb for her excellent freedman and husband, Vitalis Ursinus, and herself, indicating a personal memorial with familial and social relationships.

EDCS-14400494.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelia Vera viva fecit sibi et S Secundino coniu<g=C>i carissimo @(obito) annorum L \n
EDCS-14400494.translation: To the departed spirits. Aelia Vera, while alive, made this for herself and for her dearest husband Secundinus, who died at 50 years of age.
EDCS-14400494.summary: This epitaph records Aelia Vera commissioning her own tomb, along with that of her dearly beloved husband Secundinus, who died at the age of 50, emphasizing their marital bond and preparation for death.

EDCS-14400495.text_edition: \n Tertius Vallauni vivus fecit sibi et Deivillae Duni filiae uxori karissimae\n
EDCS-14400495.translation: Tertius Vallauni, while alive, made this for himself and for Deivilla Duni, his very dear wife.
EDCS-14400495.summary: This epitaph records Tertius Vallauni commissioning his own tomb and that of his very dear wife, Deivilla Duni, while still alive, highlighting their marriage and preparedness.

EDCS-14400496.text_edition: \n Tertulliano II semis Dis Manibus Restutus Dubitani et coniugem sic Meduni Rep et VAM sima XXX\n
EDCS-14400496.translation: To Tertullianus. Restutus Dubitani and his wife Medunia, and VAM sima, 30 years old, made this for the departed spirits.
EDCS-14400496.summary: This funerary inscription, possibly a tombstone, is dedicated to Tertullianus by Restutus Dubitani, his wife Medunia, and possibly another individual named VAM sima, aged 30, for the deceased.

EDCS-14400497.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Uxlemitano Aurelius Celsinus ex voto pro Marcellino filio milite legionis XIIII geminae votum solvit laetus libens merito\n
EDCS-14400497.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, the Uxlemitan god. Aurelius Celsinus fulfilled his vow for his son Marcellinus, a soldier of the Legio XIIII Gemina, gladly, willingly, and deservedly.
EDCS-14400497.summary: This votive inscription records Aurelius Celsinus fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for his son Marcellinus, a soldier in the Legio XIIII Gemina, indicating a personal religious act and family connection.

EDCS-14400499.text_edition: \n Niger Hilari vivus fecit sibi et Moniae coniugi et Respecto Terti filio annorum XXVI\n
EDCS-14400499.translation: Niger Hilarius, while alive, made this for himself, and for his wife Monia, and for his son Respectus, son of Tertius, 26 years old.
EDCS-14400499.summary: This epitaph records Niger Hilarius commissioning his own tomb and those of his wife Monia and son Respectus (aged 26), indicating a familial memorial erected by the father while alive.

EDCS-14400500.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus positum Sexto Sacretio Prisco Quirina Solva genero pientissimo annorum XXVI et Capitoniae Iuliae filiae pientissimae annorum XX Iustinus Similis et Capitonia Ingenuina coniux \n
EDCS-14400500.translation: Dedicated to the departed spirits. Placed by Iustinus Similis and his wife Capitonia Ingenuina for their most dutiful son-in-law Sextus Sacretio Priscus, 26 years old, and for their most dutiful daughter Capitonia Iulia, 20 years old.
EDCS-14400500.summary: This epitaph records Iustinus Similis and his wife Capitonia Ingenuina dedicating a memorial to the departed spirits for their most dutiful son-in-law Sextus Sacretio Priscus (aged 26) and their most dutiful daughter Capitonia Iulia (aged 20).

EDCS-14400503.text_edition: \n Tito Turbonio Blasto annorum LX et Novello filio annorum XV Tito Turbonio Callisto annorum LXXXX et Turboniae Fuscae annorum LXV parentibus pientissimis\n
EDCS-14400503.translation: To Titus Turbonius Blastus, 60 years old, and to his son Novellus, 15 years old, and to Titus Turbonius Callistus, 90 years old, and to Turbonia Fusca, 65 years old, parents most dutiful.
EDCS-14400503.summary: This epitaph commemorates four family members: Titus Turbonius Blastus (60), his son Novellus (15), Titus Turbonius Callistus (90), and Turbonia Fusca (65), honoring them as most dutiful parents.

EDCS-14400507.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sabinio Exuperanti et Sabinia Orea coniugi carissimo fecit vi<v=B>a annor<u=O>(m) LXX obito \n
EDCS-14400507.translation: To the departed spirits. Sabinia Orea made this for her husband Sabinius Exuperans, her dearest, while alive, 70 years of age, deceased.
