EDCS-14400507.summary: This epitaph records Sabinia Orea commissioning a memorial for her husband Sabinius Exuperans, whom she dearly loved, who died at the age of 70, indicating a wife's devotion.

EDCS-14400508.text_edition: \n uxori suai sic et Ingenuai sic filiae\n
EDCS-14400508.translation: To his wife and to Ingenua, his daughter.
EDCS-14400508.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring a wife and daughter, likely from a tombstone or monument.

EDCS-14400510.text_edition: \n Sammus Terti et Valentina parentes vivi fecerunt sibi [et ------\n
EDCS-14400510.translation: Sammus Terti and Valentina, the living parents, made this for themselves and [for others].
EDCS-14400510.summary: This funerary inscription records Sammus Terti and Valentina dedicating a monument for themselves and others while alive, probably a family tomb.

EDCS-14400512.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Invictus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate imperator X consul III pater patriae proconsul dominus indulgentissimus a Solva milia passuum XL\n
EDCS-14400512.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, supreme pontiff, holder of tribunician power for the 10th time, imperator for the 10th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, proconsul, most indulgent lord, 40 Roman miles from Solva.
EDCS-14400512.summary: This is an imperial dedication by Emperor Alexander Severus, likely marking a road or public work, dated to his 10th tribunician power and 3rd consulship.

EDCS-14400514.summary: This is a simple dedication to an individual named Covnertus, son of Erbitton.

EDCS-14400515.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus perpetuae securitati Longinus et Lupa liberti Proculines sic vivi fecerunt sibi et Lucio filio pientissimo @(obito) annorum XX\n
EDCS-14400515.translation: To the Divine Shades. Longinus and Lupa, freedpersons of Proculines, made this while alive for their perpetual peace, for themselves and for their most dutiful son Lucius, who died at 20 years of age.
EDCS-14400515.summary: This is a funerary inscription by freedpersons Longinus and Lupa for themselves and their deceased son, dedicating a tomb or monument for perpetual peace.

EDCS-14400517.text_edition: \n Censorinus Adiutoris et Iantumara Finiti vivi fecerunt sibi et Ursino filio militi in cohorte Breucorum annorum XXV et Censorino filio annorum XXX\n
EDCS-14400517.translation: Censorinus and Iantumara Finiti, alive, made this for themselves and for their son Ursinus, a soldier in the cohort of the Breuci, aged 25, and for their son Censorinus, aged 30.
EDCS-14400517.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Censorinus and Iantumara Finiti, dedicating a monument for themselves and their two sons, one of whom was a soldier.

EDCS-14400518.text_edition: \n Caio Annaeo Cai liberto Spectato et Iuliae Terentinae uxori\n
EDCS-14400518.translation: To Gaius Annaeus Spectatus, son of Gaius, and to his wife Iulia Terentina.
EDCS-14400518.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Gaius Annaeus Spectatus and his wife Iulia Terentina.

EDCS-14400519.text_edition: \n Adiutor Terti filius vivus fecit sibi et Avetae Quarti filiae coniugii suae\n
EDCS-14400519.translation: Adiutor, son of Tertius, alive, made this for himself and for his wife Aveta, daughter of Quarti.
EDCS-14400519.summary: This funerary inscription records Adiutor, son of Tertius, dedicating a monument for himself and his wife Aveta while alive.

EDCS-14400520.text_edition: \n Sabinus Esulli filius vivos sic fecit sibi et uxori Tuioni Saxionis filiae et Noibioni filio annorum X\n
EDCS-14400520.translation: Sabinus, son of Esullus, alive, made this for himself and for his wife Tuiona, daughter of Saxio, and for his son Noibio, aged 10.
EDCS-14400520.summary: This funerary inscription records Sabinus, son of Esullus, dedicating a monument for himself, his wife, and his young son while alive.

EDCS-14400521.text_edition: \n Sammus Ucci fecit vivus sibi et filio anni I\n
EDCS-14400521.translation: Sammus, son of Uccus, made this alive for himself and for his son, aged 1 year.
EDCS-14400521.summary: This funerary inscription records Sammus, son of Uccus, dedicating a monument for himself and his infant son while alive.

EDCS-14400524.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Marco Iulio Philippo nobilissimo Caesari Pio Felici Invicto Augusto\n
EDCS-14400524.translation: To our lord Marcus Julius Philippus, most noble Caesar, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered, Augustus.
EDCS-14400524.summary: This is a dedication, likely from the 3rd century CE, to the emperor Philip the Arab.

EDCS-14400525.text_edition: \n Adiutor Cupiti libertus annorum fecit sibi et Suttae matri\n
EDCS-14400525.translation: Adiutor, freedman of Cupitus, made this for himself and his mother Sutta.
EDCS-14400525.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a tomb made by Adiutor for himself and his mother, likely dating to the 1st-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-14400526.text_edition: \n Autoscutto Montissi filio et Septimae Ressimari filiae uxori filii fecerunt\n
EDCS-14400526.translation: His sons made this for Autoscutto, son of Montissus, and for his daughter Septima, wife of Ressimus.
EDCS-14400526.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a tomb built by sons for their father and father's sister (or wife if Autoscutto is the father and Septima is the mother), likely dating to the 1st-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-14400530.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Depulsori Aureliis Flavii Severus et Senator et Bellianus et Severianus iuvenior sic ex voto solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-14400530.translation: Aurelius Severus, Senator, Bellianus, and Severianus the Younger, having received this from Jupiter Optimus Maximus the Driver-Away, fulfilled their vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-14400530.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely from the 2nd-3rd centuries CE, by Aurelius Severus and his sons fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-14400531.text_edition: \n Genio Attiorum Marciani et Vitelliani Marcellus libertus Genio Attiorum Marciani et Vitelliani Marcellus libertus\n
EDCS-14400531.translation: To the Genius of the Attii Marcianus and Vitellianus, Marcellus, freedman. To the Genius of the Attii Marcianus and Vitellianus, Marcellus, freedman.
EDCS-14400531.summary: This is a dedication, likely from the 2nd-3rd centuries CE, by a freedman named Marcellus to the genius of his patrons.

EDCS-14400541.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Hostilio Tertino annorum XL et Valeriae Sedatae coniugi Hostilii Tertullinus et Tertullus parentibus optimis filii fecerunt\n
EDCS-14400541.translation: Hostilius Tertullinus and Tertullus, his sons, made this for their most excellent parents, Hostilius Tertinus, aged 40, and his wife Valeria Sedata.
EDCS-14400541.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a tomb built by sons for their parents, likely dating to the 2nd-3rd centuries CE.

EDCS-14400542.summary: This is a funerary dedication, likely from the 1st-3rd centuries CE, for Fuccia Secundina.

EDCS-14400543.text_edition: \n Nemesi Augustae Kanius Tertullinus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400543.translation: Kanius Tertullinus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Nemesis Augusta.
EDCS-14400543.summary: This is a votive inscription to Nemesis Augusta, likely from the 2nd-3rd centuries CE, by Kanius Tertullinus.

EDCS-14400544.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Quadrubiis sic Augustis Gabinius Quintus pro salute sua suorum votum solvit libens laetus meritu sic\n
EDCS-14400544.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus in the Quadrubia district, Gabinius Quintus, for his own health and that of his family, willingly, happily, and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-14400544.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely from the 2nd-3rd centuries CE, by Gabinius Quintus for his family's well-being.

EDCS-14400547.text_edition: \n Ursus pro se suisque votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400547.translation: Ursus fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly for himself and his family.
EDCS-14400547.summary: This is a votive inscription, likely from the 1st-3rd centuries CE, by Ursus for himself and his family.

EDCS-14400548.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Donatius Surus et Aurelia Zosima vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-14400548.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Donatius Surus and Aurelia Zosima, while alive, made this.
EDCS-14400548.summary: A funerary inscription by Donatius Surus and Aurelia Zosima for themselves, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400549.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Bottius Aventinus et Valerius Ursinus \n
EDCS-14400549.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest. Bottius Aventinus and Valerius Ursinus.
EDCS-14400549.summary: A votive dedication to Jupiter by Bottius Aventinus and Valerius Ursinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400550.text_edition: \n Maximus pro se et suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400550.translation: Maximus paid his vow willingly and deservedly for himself and his family.
EDCS-14400550.summary: A votive inscription by Maximus, expressing fulfillment of a vow for himself and his household, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400553.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio huius loci Olympius Augusti nostri vilicus stationis Escensis aedem et aram dedicavit\n
EDCS-14400553.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, and to the Genius of this place, Olympius, overseer of our emperor's station at Esca, dedicated this temple and altar.
EDCS-14400553.summary: A dedication of a temple and altar to Jupiter and the Genius loci by Olympius, an overseer at the Esca station, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400554.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Terentinus Taurionis filius e ac obitus annorum XXX et Veria Cosivia mater obita annorum LXV Aurelius Taurio coniugi et filio et Flavina filia eius vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-14400554.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Terentinus, son of Taurionis, died aged 30, and Veria Cosivia, his mother, died aged 65. Aurelius Taurio, for his wife and son, and his daughter Flavina, while alive, made this.
EDCS-14400554.summary: A funerary inscription by Aurelius Taurio for his deceased wife, son (Terentinus), and daughter (Flavina), dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400559.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcellinus.
EDCS-14400559.summary: A simple funerary inscription for Marcellinus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400561.text_edition: \n XXXVI Maurus servus Matuli Iuvenis coniugi karissimae vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14400561.translation: For 36 years. Maurus, slave of Matulus Iuvenis, made this for his dearest wife while alive.
EDCS-14400561.summary: A funerary inscription by Maurus, a slave, for his wife, indicating their lifespan or the wife's age, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400564.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aviliae Ledae defunctae annorum XXXV Mutilius Fortunatus et Avilius Gratus alumni fecerunt\n
EDCS-14400564.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Avilia Leda, deceased aged 35. Mutilius Fortunatus and Avilius Gratus, foster-brothers, made this.
EDCS-14400564.summary: A funerary inscription for Avilia Leda by her foster-brothers Mutilius Fortunatus and Avilius Gratus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400565.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Hermianus Augustorum nnostrorum scrutator stationis Bilachiniensis et Leontia coniux fecerunt Caprae filiae karissimae obitae nnorum V mensium XI dierum XIII\n
EDCS-14400565.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Hermianus, scrutator of our emperors' station at Bilachin, and his wife Leontia, made this for their dearest daughter Caprae, deceased aged 5 years, 11 months, and 13 days.
EDCS-14400565.summary: A funerary inscription by Hermianus and Leontia for their young daughter Caprae, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400566.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII imperator XII consul II pater patriae proconsul et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus tribunicia potestate IIII proconsul et Publius Septimius [Geta] miliaria vetustate collapsa restituerunt curam agente Marco Iuventio Suro Proclo legato pro praetore ab Agunto milia passuum LXVII\n
EDCS-14400566.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 9th time, acclaimed imperator 12 times, consul twice, father of the fatherland, proconsul, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, with tribunician power for the 4th time, proconsul, and Publius Septimius [Geta], restored the milestones, which had collapsed from ancient wear, under the governorship of Marcus Iuventius Suro Proclus, legate, 67 miles from Aguntum.
EDCS-14400566.summary: An inscription recording the restoration of milestones by Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla (and likely Geta) along the road from Aguntum, overseen by the legate M. Iuventius Suro Proclus, likely dating to between 202 and 210 AD.

EDCS-14400568.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII imperator XII consul II pater patriae proconsul et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus tribunicia potestate IIII proconsul [et Publius Septimius Geta] [nobilissimus Caesar] miliaria vetustate conlabsa sic restituerunt curante Marco Iuventio Suro Proculo legato pro praetore ab Agunto milia passuum XXXXV\n
EDCS-14400568.translation: Emperors Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 9th time, imperator for the 12th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, with tribunician power for the 4th time, proconsul [and Publius Septimius Geta] [most noble Caesar] rebuilt these milestones, having collapsed from age, under the supervision of Marcus Iuventius Surus Proculus, legatus pro praetore, 45 Roman miles from Aguntum.
EDCS-14400568.summary: This is a milestone inscription from the Severan period, recording the reconstruction of milestones by Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and possibly Geta, under the governor Marcus Iuventius Surus Proculus.

EDCS-14400570.text_edition: \n Termunibus sic Augustis Quintus Proculeius Finitus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400570.translation: To Termunibus, as to the Augusti, Quintus Proculeius Finitus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-14400570.summary: This is a dedication to the deity Termunibus, recording the fulfillment of a vow by Quintus Proculeius Finitus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400571.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription possibly recording a dedication or a name from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400572.text_edition: \n In memoriam - decurionis Claudiae Celeiae Quintus Terentius\n
EDCS-14400572.translation: In memory of [---], decurion, of Claudia Celeia, Quintus Terentius [erected this].
EDCS-14400572.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription commemorating a decurion of Claudia Celeia, erected by Quintus Terentius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400573.text_edition: \n Germani v(ivus)? f(ecit)? sibi et uriae osiae Secundinae libert- et estiae Firmina onius Firmus XXXV hic sit- est\n
EDCS-14400573.translation: Germani, alive, [erected this?] for himself and for Uriasia Secundina, freedwoman, and for Estia Firmina. Onius Firmus [died?] aged 35. Here lies.
EDCS-14400573.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription recording Germani and Onius Firmus, and dedicating the tomb to Uriasia Secundina and Estia Firmina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400578.text_edition: \n Sedato Augusto et cultoribus eius Lucius Aufillenius Crispinus siginum et aram donum dedit\n
EDCS-14400578.translation: Lucius Aufillenius Crispinus gave as a gift a statue and an altar to the deified Augustus and his worshippers.
EDCS-14400578.summary: This is a dedication of a statue and altar to the deified Augustus and his worshippers by Lucius Aufillenius Crispinus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-14400580.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Gaiano cives sic Surus sic decurioni Italicae @(obito) annorum L et Aurelio Maximo nepoti @(obito) annorum XXXV Caius Gaianus et Aurelius Granus faciendum <c=Q>uraverunt \n
EDCS-14400580.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caius Gaianus and Aurelius Granus had this made for Aurelius Gaianus, citizen of Surus, decurion of Italica, who died at the age of 50, and for Aurelius Maximus, his nephew, who died at the age of 35.
EDCS-14400580.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius Gaianus and his nephew Aurelius Maximus, commissioned by Caius Gaianus and Aurelius Granus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400582.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Nundinus Respecti vivus fecit sibi et Secundinae Secundini filiae annorum XXX et Firminam filiam annorum XL et Iunius Lucillianus annorum LX\n
EDCS-14400582.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Nundinus Respecti, alive, made [this] for himself and for Secundina, daughter of Secundinus, aged 30, and for Filmina, daughter [of?], aged 40, and for Iunius Lucillianus, aged 60.
EDCS-14400582.summary: This is a funerary inscription from the Roman Imperial period where Nundinus Respecti, alive, made provision for himself and for Secundina, Firmina, and Iunius Lucillianus.

EDCS-14400583.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Primus Finiti filius vivus sibi fecit et coniugi suae posuit\n
EDCS-14400583.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Primus, son of Finitus, alive, made [this] for himself and placed it for his wife.
EDCS-14400583.summary: This is a funerary inscription from the Roman Imperial period where Primus, son of Finitus, alive, made a tomb for himself and his wife.

EDCS-14400584.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et dis deabusque omnibus Victorius Crispinus liberatus periculis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400584.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, and to all the gods and goddesses, Victorius Crispinus, having been freed from dangers, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-14400584.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Victorius Crispinus to Jupiter and all the gods for being freed from dangers, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400585.text_edition: \n In memoriam Luci Matti Publi fili Claudia Probi decurionis Celeiae Publius Mattius Luci filius Bellicus IIvir iure dicundo posuit In memoriam Luci Matti Publi fili Claudia Probi decurionis Celeiae Publius Mattius Luci filius Bellicus IIvir iure dicundo posuit\n
EDCS-14400585.translation: In memory of Lucius Mattius, son of Publius, of the Claudia tribe, son of Probus the decurion of Celeia. Publius Mattius Bellicus, son of Lucius, son of Publius, IIvir with the power of jurisdiction, erected this in memory of Lucius Mattius, son of Publius, of the Claudia tribe, son of Probus the decurion of Celeia. Publius Mattius Bellicus, son of Lucius, son of Publius, IIvir with the power of jurisdiction.
EDCS-14400585.summary: This funerary inscription records a dedication by Publius Mattius Bellicus, a IIvir, in memory of Lucius Mattius, likely a relative, and possibly a decurion named Probus.

EDCS-14400587.text_edition: \n SV TES suo I et Iu \n
EDCS-14400587.translation: To Sutess, his own, and Iu.
EDCS-14400587.summary: This very fragmentary inscription appears to be a dedication to a deity named Sutess, possibly with other individuals mentioned.

EDCS-14400588.summary: This brief inscription simply identifies an individual as the freedman of someone named Quintus.

EDCS-14400589.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Felici Invicto Augusto divi Antonini filio divi Severi nepoti consuli II patri patriae\n
EDCS-14400589.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, son of the deified Antoninus, grandson of the deified Severus, consul for the second time, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-14400589.summary: This is a titulary inscription, listing the imperial titles of Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (Caracalla), likely set up during his second consulship.

EDCS-14400590.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Claudio Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribuniciae potes<ta>tis\n
EDCS-14400590.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Claudius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power.
EDCS-14400590.summary: This titulary inscription records the titles of Emperor Claudius (likely Claudius Gothicus), emphasizing his religious and political authority.

EDCS-14400591.text_edition: \n MaX @(obit-) annorum et Ma infelicissim eorum AMTIA\n
EDCS-14400591.translation: died at X years of age and [...] unhappy their AMTIA
EDCS-14400591.summary: This fragmented inscription records the death of an individual at a specific age and mentions someone named AMTIA.

EDCS-14400593.text_edition: \n Stritto Brem filius vivus fecit et sibi Camona Attonis filia annorum LX et Ressatus annorum XXX et Rumo annorum XX hic siti sunt Darbosa posuit\n
EDCS-14400593.translation: Stritto, son of Brem, made this while alive, and for himself, Camona, daughter of Atto, aged 60, and Ressatus, aged 30, and Rumo, aged 20, are buried here. Darbosa placed this.
EDCS-14400593.summary: This is a funerary monument recording the death and burial of several individuals, including Stritto (who erected it), Camona, Ressatus, and Rumo, with Darbosa identified as the setter.

EDCS-14400594.text_edition: \n Ateloudo Sabini filio nnorum C et Boniatae Tutor(is) filiae uxori annorum Atin \n
EDCS-14400594.translation: To Ateloudus, son of Sabinus, aged [unknown number] years, and to Boniata, daughter of Tutor, his wife, aged [unknown number] years.
EDCS-14400594.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Ateloudus and his wife Boniata, providing their patronymics and ages.

EDCS-14400596.text_edition: \n A Et Maximo filio annorum XXX Dis Manibus Aulo Vedernae Maximo harispici sic annorum LXXV et Masculiniae Res<p>ectae coniugi annorum XL Aulus Vederna Maximinus parentibus et Vedernae Maximinae sorori annorum XXII et Tucciae Secundinae coniugi annorum XXX pientissimae\n
EDCS-14400596.translation: To Max, son of A and E, aged 30. To the Divine Shades. To Aulus Vedernus Maximus, the haruspex, aged 75, and his wife Masculinia Respecta, aged 40. Aulus Vedernus Maximinus [erected this] for his parents and for his sister Vederna Maximina, aged 22, and for his wife Tuccia Secundina, aged 30, most devoted.
EDCS-14400596.summary: This funerary inscription honors multiple family members, including Aulus Vedernus Maximus (a haruspex), his wife Masculinia, their son Aulus Vedernus Maximinus, his sister Vederna Maximina, and his wife Tuccia Secundina.

EDCS-14400597.text_edition: \n Mogio Tritouti libertus vivus fecit sibi et suis Dubnia Mogionis filia annorum XX hic sita est Vetra Mogionis nnum \n
EDCS-14400597.translation: Mogius, freedman of Tritout, made this while alive, and for himself and his family. Dubnia, daughter of Mogius, aged 20, is buried here. Vetra, daughter of Mogius [unknown age]
EDCS-14400597.summary: This funerary inscription was erected by the freedman Mogius for himself and his family, and records the burial of his daughter Dubnia and another daughter, Vetra.

EDCS-14400598.text_edition: \n Romani filius sibi et Antonio Iudino filio annorum VI \n
EDCS-14400598.translation: Romani, son of Romani, for himself and his son Antonio Iudino, aged VI years.
EDCS-14400598.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording Romani's memorial for himself and his son Antonio Iudino.

EDCS-14400600.text_edition: \n Boniatus Bardonis vivus fecit sibi et Mogiae Terti annorum L Septimius Secundinus miles legionis II Italicae stupendiorum sic XV annorum XL Aurelia Ursa annorum XXX\n
EDCS-14400600.translation: Boniatus, son of Bardo, made this while alive for himself and Mogia Tertia, aged L. Septimius Secundinus, a soldier of the Second Italian Legion, of stupendous achievements, aged XL. Aurelia Ursa, aged XXX.
EDCS-14400600.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Boniatus, Mogia Tertia, Septimius Secundinus, and Aurelia Ursa, with Boniatus making his own monument.

EDCS-14400601.text_edition: \n Silvanus Maximi filius annorum LXX testamento fieri iussit Bella viva fecit coniugi et filio\n
EDCS-14400601.translation: Silvanus, son of Maximu, aged LXX, ordered this to be made by his will; Bella, while alive, made it for her husband and son.
EDCS-14400601.summary: This funerary inscription records Silvanus's posthumous commission and Bella's creation of the monument for her husband and son.

EDCS-14400603.text_edition: \n Noibio Elgiati filius nnorum C hic est Courtula Adbugiouni filia nnorum LXX hic est Iunius gi filius NOVTVISVI\n
EDCS-14400603.translation: Noibius, son of Elgiatus, aged C. Here lies Courtula, daughter of Adbugioun, aged LXX. Iunius, son of Iunius, [dedicated this].
EDCS-14400603.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Noibius and Courtula, and possibly a dedication by Iunius.

EDCS-14400604.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Defensori sacrum Aurelius Coriphus pro se et suis ex visu votum solvit libens merito Sabino et Venusto consulibus\n
EDCS-14400604.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Defender. Aurelius Coriphus fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly for himself and his family, after seeing a vision, under the consulship of Sabinus and Venustus.
EDCS-14400604.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Best and Greatest, dedicated by Aurelius Coriphus for himself and his family, likely dating to the consulship of Sabinus and Venustus.

EDCS-14400605.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Aurelius Coriphus Barthiae filius pro se et suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400605.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest. Aurelius Coriphus, son of Barthias, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly for himself and his family.
EDCS-14400605.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Best and Greatest, dedicated by Aurelius Coriphus for himself and his family.

EDCS-14400608.text_edition: \n Atecurus Docnimari <f=E>(ilius) annorum XLI Sexta Mittonis <f=E>(ilia) \n
EDCS-14400608.translation: Atecurus, son of Docnimarus, aged XLI. Sexta, daughter of Mitton, aged [unknown].
EDCS-14400608.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the ages of Atecurus and Sexta.

EDCS-14400610.summary: This is a funerary inscription, likely a name alone, possibly indicating a freedman.

EDCS-14400611.text_edition: \n Marco Ulpio Catullino qui vixit nnos XLV Marcus Ulpius Severianus et Ulpia Severiana filii et Iulia Ianuaria uxsor heredes faciendum curaverunt secundum voluntatem testamenti\n
EDCS-14400611.translation: Marcus Ulpius Severianus and Ulpia Severiana, children, and their mother Julia Ianuaria, the heirs, took care to have this made for Marcus Ulpius Catullinus, who lived XLV years, in accordance with the terms of his will.
EDCS-14400611.summary: This funerary inscription records the posthumous construction of a monument for Marcus Ulpius Catullinus by his children and wife, following his will.

EDCS-14400612.text_edition: \n Herculi sacrum Gaius Iulius Eptino votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400612.translation: Sacred to Hercules. Gaius Iulius Eptinus fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-14400612.summary: This is a votive inscription to Hercules, dedicated by Gaius Iulius Eptinus.

EDCS-14400622.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iulius Peculiaris votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400622.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Iulius Peculiaris gladly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-14400622.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter by Iulius Peculiaris, likely an individual soldier or freedman.

EDCS-14400625.text_edition: \n Atemeriae Atecnati filiae uxori annorum XXXX\n
EDCS-14400625.translation: To Atemeria, daughter of Atecnatus, wife, aged 40.
EDCS-14400625.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Atemeria, recorded as a wife and daughter, with her age indicated.

EDCS-14400627.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Culminali Valerius Ingenuus pro se et suis votum solvit merito libens\n
EDCS-14400627.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Culminalis, Valerius Ingenuus paid his vow for himself and his family, deservedly and gladly.
EDCS-14400627.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Culminalis by Valerius Ingenuus, who offers the vow for his family.

EDCS-14400628.text_edition: \n Quintus Ennius Liberalis et Ennia Oppidana fecerunt sibi et Kalendinae filiae annorum XVII et Vitulo filio annorum XXX\n
EDCS-14400628.translation: Quintus Ennius Liberalis and Ennia Oppidana made this for themselves and for their daughter Kalendina, aged 17, and their son Vitulus, aged 30.
EDCS-14400628.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Quintus Ennius Liberalis and Ennia Oppidana for themselves and their deceased children, Kalendina and Vitulus.

EDCS-14400630.text_edition: \n Caiae Septimiae Cai filiae Iustae annorum LV uxori Spectati Prisciani Caio Spectatio Cai filio Claudia Prisciano IIviro iure dicundo annorum V Caius Spectatius Finitus IIvir iure dicundo Claudiae Celeiae pater infelicissimus fecit MATV ON\n
EDCS-14400630.translation: To Caia Septimia Iusta, daughter of Caius, aged 55, wife of Spectatus Priscianus; to Caius Spectatius Priscianus, son of Caius, aged 5, [and] to Caius Spectatius Finitus, father [of Claudia Prisciana], former chief magistrate with judicial authority; [erected by] Caius Spectatius Finitus, father, most unhappy, [for] Claudia Prisciana, former chief magistrate with judicial authority of Claudia Celeia.
EDCS-14400630.summary: This funerary inscription records multiple family members, including officials of Claudia Celeia, and is dedicated by a grieving father.

EDCS-14400631.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Spectatius Avitus annorum LXXX Aurelia Severina Caius Spectatius Secundianus vivus fecit sibi et Tutoriae Avitae coniugi carissimae annorum LV et Caio Spectatio Cervae filio annorum XXVIII et Caio Rusticio Tutori nepoti annorum XII et Rusticium sic Albinum! filium! XXX Spectatiae Severinae annorum XXV\n
EDCS-14400631.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Spectatius Avitus, aged 80, Aurelia Severina, Caius Spectatius Secundianus, while living, made this for themselves and for Tutoria Avita, dearest wife, aged 55, and for Caius Spectatius Cervus, son, aged 28, and for Caius Rusticius Tutor, grandson, aged 12, and for Rusticius Albinus, son, aged 30, and for Spectatia Severina, aged 25.
EDCS-14400631.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Spectatius Avitus and Aurelia Severina for themselves and several other deceased family members of varying ages.

EDCS-14400632.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Statutius Secundianus et Cerva coniunx filio Statutio Secundo obito annorum IIII\n
EDCS-14400632.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Statutius Secundianus and Cerva, his wife, [erected this] for their son Statutius Secundus, deceased, aged 4.
EDCS-14400632.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to a young child, Statutius Secundus, by his parents.

EDCS-14400633.text_edition: \n Caius Vindonius Successus aedilis Claudiae Celeiae fecit sibi et Iuliae Sexti filiae Ingenuae uxori fidelissimae annorum L Dis Manibus\n
EDCS-14400633.translation: To the spirits of the departed: Caius Vindonius Successus, aedile of Claudia Celeia, made this for himself and for Iulia Ingenua, daughter of Sextus, his most faithful wife, aged 50.
EDCS-14400633.summary: This funerary inscription was erected by Caius Vindonius Successus, an official, for himself and his deceased wife.

EDCS-14400634.summary: This is a very short funerary inscription, possibly a tomb marker or dedication, to Rustic Tertullianus.

EDCS-14400639.text_edition: \n Publius Aelius Iunius vivus fecit sibi et Nemetae Nigellionis filiae coniugi et Vibio filio annorum XVII\n
EDCS-14400639.translation: Publius Aelius Iunius, while living, made this for himself and for Nemeta Nigellionis, daughter of Nigellio, his wife, and for his son Vibius, aged 17.
EDCS-14400639.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Publius Aelius Iunius for himself, his wife, and his son.

EDCS-14400640.text_edition: \n Adsallutae sacrum Lucius Servilius Eutyches cum suis gubernatoribus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400640.translation: Lucius Servilius Eutyches, with his captains, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Adsalluta.
EDCS-14400640.summary: This is a votive inscription by Lucius Servilius Eutyches to the deity Adsalluta, likely dedicated by a maritime official and his crew.

EDCS-14400641.text_edition: \n Savo et Adsallutae Caius Memmius votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-14400641.translation: Caius Memmius willingly, joyfully, and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Savo and Adsalluta.
EDCS-14400641.summary: This is a votive inscription by Caius Memmius to the deities Savo and Adsalluta.

EDCS-14400642.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II pater patriae consul pro consule et Marcus Opellius Antoninus Diadumenianus nobilissimus Caesar princebs sic iuventutis providentissimi Augusti fecerunt a Celeia milia passuum III\n
EDCS-14400642.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the second time, father of the country, consul, proconsul, and Marcus Opellius Antoninus Diadumenianus, most noble Caesar, prince of the youth, most provident Augustus, made this, 3 miles from Celeia.
EDCS-14400642.summary: This is a milestone inscription from the reign of Macrinus and Diadumenianus, marking a distance of three miles from Celeia.

EDCS-14400643.text_edition: \n Imperator Nerva Traianus Caesar Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate pater patriae consul IIII III\n
EDCS-14400643.translation: Emperor Nerva Traianus Caesar Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, with tribunician power, father of the country, consul for the fourth and third time.
EDCS-14400643.summary: This inscription records the titles and consulships of Emperor Trajan, likely on a public monument.

EDCS-14400645.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVI consul III pater patriae proconsul III\n
EDCS-14400645.translation: Emperor Caesar Traianus Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the deified Nerva, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 16th time, consul for the third time, father of the country, proconsul for the third time.
EDCS-14400645.summary: This inscription lists the titles and powers of Emperor Hadrian, likely on a public monument, indicating his 16th year of tribunician power and third consulship.

EDCS-14400646.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pater patriae pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate imperator II consul III III\n
EDCS-14400646.translation: Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, chief priest, with tribunician power, emperor for the second time, consul for the third and third time.
EDCS-14400646.summary: This inscription records the titles and consulships of Emperor Antoninus Pius, indicating his second acclamation as emperor.

EDCS-14400652.text_edition: \n Inisus fecit sibi et et Ursi\n
EDCS-14400652.translation: Inisus made for himself and for Ursi.
EDCS-14400652.summary: This is a dedication made by Inisus for himself and possibly a relative or companion named Ursi, likely on a tomb or a private monument.

EDCS-14400653.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Invicto Mithrae pro salute Marci Aureli Feliciani et Marci Aureli Felicissimi filii eius et Aureli Feliciani iunioris Philumenus amicus eorundem ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-14400653.translation: Philumenus, their friend, dedicated this to the unconquered god Mithras for the safety of Marcus Aurelius Felicianus, his son Marcus Aurelius Felicissimus, and Aurelius Felicianus the younger, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-14400653.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the god Sol Invictus Mithras by Philumenus for the well-being of three individuals.

EDCS-14400654.text_edition: \n Bardo Primi filius vivus fecit sibi et \n
EDCS-14400654.translation: Bardo, son of Primus, made while alive for himself.
EDCS-14400654.summary: This is a funerary or dedication inscription by Bardo, son of Primus, for himself, likely on a tomb or a building.

EDCS-14400659.summary: This is a dedication to the god Mars Augustus, begun by Quintus Art...

EDCS-14400660.translation: Claudia Celeia and her heirs erected this.
EDCS-14400660.summary: A funerary monument erected by Claudia Celeia and her heirs.

EDCS-14400662.text_edition: \n Cominius Tedsicnati filius vivus fecit sibi et suis Successae Adiutoris filiae coniugi annorum L\n
EDCS-14400662.translation: Cominius, son of Tedsicnatus, erected this in his lifetime for himself and for his wife Successa, daughter of Adiutor, aged 50.
EDCS-14400662.summary: A funerary monument erected by Cominius for himself and his wife Successa, who died at age 50.

EDCS-14400664.text_edition: \n Egetumario Marci filio annorum LXXII hic situs est\n
EDCS-14400664.translation: Egetumarius, son of Marcus, aged 72, lies here.
EDCS-14400664.summary: A funerary inscription recording the burial of Egetumarius, son of Marcus, aged 72.

EDCS-14400665.text_edition: \n Tatulo Tricci filius vivus fecit sibi et A Macerinae coniugi et Titus A Celemes nnorum XXI\n
EDCS-14400665.translation: Tatulus, son of Triccus, erected this in his lifetime for himself, his wife A. Macerina, and Titus A. Celemes, aged 21.
EDCS-14400665.summary: A funerary monument erected by Tatulus for himself, his wife, and his son Titus A. Celemes, who died at age 21.

EDCS-14400666.text_edition: \n Avitianus us Avitiani nus Secundini a annorum S S niani annorum VAV \n
EDCS-14400666.translation: Avitianus, son of Avitianus, son of Secundinus, aged [---], and [---]niani, aged 5, [---].
EDCS-14400666.summary: A fragmented funerary inscription likely recording the age and parentage of at least two individuals.

EDCS-14400667.translation: Serenus and Marullus erected this for Hercules.
EDCS-14400667.summary: A dedication to Hercules, erected by Serenus and Marullus.

EDCS-14400668.text_edition: \n Maximus Finiti annorum LX Latinus LX\n
EDCS-14400668.summary: A funerary inscription recording the ages of two individuals, Maximus Finiti and Latinus.

EDCS-14400669.text_edition: \n D(is) Manibus Acceptus Senilis vivus fecit sibi Secundinaastiva Restitut? \n
EDCS-14400669.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed: Acceptus Senilis erected this in his lifetime for himself, Secundina Astiva, and Restituta [?].
EDCS-14400669.summary: A funerary monument erected by Acceptus Senilis for himself and two women, Secundina Astiva and Restituta.

EDCS-14400670.text_edition: \n vixit XXX Secund vivus fecit titulum Ursinae Ingenuus ITVS XXX SV XVI LIV LIV \n
EDCS-14400670.translation: Lived 30 years. Secundus erected this monument in his lifetime for Ursinia Ingenuus [?]; [---] lived 30 years, [---] lived 16 years, [---] lived [---] years.
EDCS-14400670.summary: A funerary inscription recording the lifetimes of at least three individuals, including Secundus and Ursinia.

EDCS-14400673.text_edition: \n Herculi titulum posuit Celerinus libens visu monitus\n
EDCS-14400673.translation: Celerinus willingly placed this monument for Hercules, having been moved by what he saw.
EDCS-14400673.summary: A dedication to Hercules by Celerinus, who was moved to erect the monument.

EDCS-14400675.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Lucius Venonius Rufus.

EDCS-14400676.text_edition: \n Iano Gemino Augusto sacrum Restitutus Secundi cultor eius donum dedit\n
EDCS-14400676.translation: Restitutus, a devotee of Janus Geminus Augustus, dedicated this gift.
EDCS-14400676.summary: This is a dedication to Janus Geminus Augustus, made by his devotee Restitutus.

EDCS-14400678.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Proculeiae Boniatae annorum LX ENII \n
EDCS-14400678.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, Proculeia Boniata, aged 60.
EDCS-14400678.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Proculeia Boniata, who died at the age of 60.

EDCS-14400679.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Iulius Cerialis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14400679.translation: Gaius Julius Cerialis willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-14400679.summary: This inscription records Gaius Julius Cerialis fulfilling a vow to Jupiter.

EDCS-14400680.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Celsilla Maximi annorum LXXV Variniana annorum LXX \n
EDCS-14400680.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, Celsilla, daughter of Maximu, aged 75, and Variniana, aged 70.
EDCS-14400680.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Celsilla and Variniana, aged 75 and 70 respectively.

EDCS-14500001.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aquilini Caesaris nostri annorum XXXXVI Iulia Stratonice coniugi pientissimo\n
EDCS-14500001.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, Aquilinus, our Caesar, aged 46; to Iulia Stratonice, his most devoted wife.
EDCS-14500001.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Aquilinus, referred to as 'our Caesar', and his wife Iulia Stratonice.

EDCS-14500002.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Venustae nnrum XXII dedicatus Utilio \n
EDCS-14500002.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, Iulia Venusta, aged 22; dedicated by Utilus.
EDCS-14500002.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Iulia Venusta, aged 22, dedicated by Utilus.

EDCS-14500003.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quintus Mutilius Chrestus vivus fecit sibi et Florentiniae Secundinae coniugi rarissimae nnorum XXV et Mutiliae Fortunatae matri nnorum LXX et Mutiliae Crispinae nepti nnorum IIII\n
EDCS-14500003.translation: Quintus Mutilius Chrestus, while alive, made this for himself and for Florentinia Secundina, his most rare wife, aged 25, and for Mutilia Fortunata, his mother, aged 70, and for Mutilia Crispina, his granddaughter, aged 4.
EDCS-14500003.summary: This is a funerary epitaph made by Quintus Mutilius Chrestus for himself, his wife, mother, and granddaughter.

EDCS-14500004.text_edition: \n Secundinus Secundi et Bruttia Kalandina sic coniux et sic sibi vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-14500004.translation: Secundinus, son of Secundus, and his wife Bruttia Kalandina made this for themselves while alive.
EDCS-14500004.summary: This is a funerary epitaph made by Secundinus and his wife Bruttia Kalandina for themselves.

EDCS-14500006.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Hermes vivus fecit sibi et Iuliae Eutychiae coniugi karissimae!\n
EDCS-14500006.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, Iulius Hermes, while alive, made this for himself and for Iulia Eutychia, his most cherished wife!
EDCS-14500006.summary: This is a funerary epitaph made by Iulius Hermes for himself and his wife Iulia Eutychia.

EDCS-14500007.text_edition: \n Herculi Augusto sacrum Gaius Camerius Iuvenalis et Lucania Decorata aedem resstitueruntt\n
EDCS-14500007.translation: Sacred to Hercules Augustus. Gaius Camerius Iuvenalis and Lucania Decorata restored the temple.
EDCS-14500007.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a temple to Hercules Augustus by Gaius Camerius Iuvenalis and Lucania Decorata.

EDCS-14500008.text_edition: \n Acuto IIII aedilicia potestate IIII iure dicundo pr<ae=NI>fecto iure dicundo Quintus C Lupus fratri pientissimo \n
EDCS-14500008.translation: To Acutus, holding aedilic power for the fourth time, and Quintus C. Lupus, prefect for the fourth time in dispensing justice, for his most devoted brother.
EDCS-14500008.summary: This inscription is an dedication to Acutus and Quintus C. Lupus, who held significant judicial and administrative roles, likely as a funerary dedication for a brother.

EDCS-14500009.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Amando Titi Iuli Saturnini servo |contrascriptori Maturus et Mercator vilici bene merenti\n
EDCS-14500009.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Amandus, the scribe of Titius Iulius Saturninus, Maturus and Mercator, the bailiffs, his well-deserving servants.
EDCS-14500009.summary: This funerary inscription honors Amandus, a scribe, by his servants Maturus and Mercator.

EDCS-14500010.text_edition: \n Genio Augusti sacrum Gaius! Iulius Verecundus et Gaius! Iulius Mercator et Gaia! Iulia Iuliana et Gaius! Iulius Secundus\n
EDCS-14500010.translation: Sacred to the Genius of Augustus. Gaius Iulius Verecundus, Gaius Iulius Mercator, Gaia Iulia Iuliana, and Gaius Iulius Secundus.
EDCS-14500010.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Genius of Augustus by four individuals: Gaius Iulius Verecundus, Gaius Iulius Mercator, Gaia Iulia Iuliana, and Gaius Iulius Secundus.

EDCS-14500011.text_edition: \n Caius Antistius Celer votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14500011.translation: Gaius Antistius Celer gladly fulfilled his vow as deserved.
EDCS-14500011.summary: This inscription records Gaius Antistius Celer fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-14500012.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a dedication by someone named Quintus, whose other names or titles are abbreviated.

EDCS-14500013.text_edition: \n Caius Antestius Ambudsuilus aedilis Teurniae S vivus fecit sibi et suis et Atestati Bricconis filio patri Devvae Atiougonis filiae matri Caius Antestius Cai filius Lutumarus Marcus Antestius Cai filius Vogitoutus Antestia Cai filia Banona\n
EDCS-14500013.translation: Gaius Antestius Ambudsuilus, as aedile of Teurnia, made this while alive for himself and his family, and for Atesta, daughter of Bricconis, his mother, and Devva, daughter of Atiougonis, his mother; Gaius Antestius, son of Caius, Lutumarus; Marcus Antestius, son of Caius, Vogitoutus; Antestia, daughter of Caius, Banona.
EDCS-14500013.summary: This is a lengthy funerary monument inscription made by Gaius Antestius Ambudsuilus, an aedile, for himself, his family, and various relatives, with additional family members listed.

EDCS-14500014.text_edition: \n Nonia Enobugi uxor defuncta annorum Enobux Gnauo et Adnamius P\n
EDCS-14500014.translation: Nonia, wife of Enobugis, deceased, aged [?] years. Enobux Gnauo and Adnamius P.
EDCS-14500014.summary: This funerary inscription records the death and age of Nonia, wife of Enobugis, and mentions two other individuals, Enobux Gnauo and Adnamius P.

EDCS-14500015.text_edition: \n Herculi Invicto sacrum Gaius Donnicius Rufinus et Valeria Attica cum suis templum vetustate conlabsum sic restituerunt ex voto\n
EDCS-14500015.translation: Sacred to Hercules Invictus. Gaius Donnicius Rufinus and Valeria Attica, with their families, restored the temple, which had collapsed from age, according to their vow.
EDCS-14500015.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a temple to Hercules Invictus by Gaius Donnicius Rufinus and Valeria Attica due to its decay from age.

EDCS-14500016.text_edition: \n Caio Rhesio Dextro quaestori Redsomarus Brito et Litugena patri viv- fecerunt\n
EDCS-14500016.translation: For Gaius Rhesius Dexter, quaestor. Redsomarus the Briton and Litugena made [this] for [their] father while alive.
EDCS-14500016.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Redsomarus Brito and Litugena for their father, likely in honor of Gaius Rhesius Dexter, a quaestor.

EDCS-14500017.translation: To Lucius Quordnius, son of Marcus, from Divgnamaia.
EDCS-14500017.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Lucius Quordnius by Divgnamaia, likely from the Roman Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-14500018.text_edition: \n Sabiniae Severinae libertae Iucundae annorum XL Caius Sabinius Plocamus coniugi pientissimae et sibi et Graecinae et Aprili et Rufo filiis vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14500018.translation: Caius Sabinius Plocamus made this tomb for his most dutiful wife, Sabinia Severina, freedwoman of Iucunda, aged 40, and for himself, and for his children Graecina, Aprilis, and Rufus, while he was alive.
EDCS-14500018.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Caius Sabinius Plocamus for his wife Sabinia Severina and their children, and for himself, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-14500019.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Lampridius Faustinus Iuniae Veronillae coniugi de se optime meritae et Lampridiae Faustinae filiae annorum XXII faciendum curavit in hoc heredem monumentum sequetur\n
EDCS-14500019.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caius Lampridius Faustinus arranged for this monument to be made for his wife Iunia Veronilla, who had deserved exceedingly well of him, and for his daughter Lampridia Faustina, aged 22; the heir will succeed to this monument.
EDCS-14500019.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Caius Lampridius Faustinus for his wife and daughter, indicating that the tomb will pass to an heir, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-14500020.text_edition: \n Ientumaro Adnami et Secundinae coniugi et Vitali genero Ientumarus vivus fecit et Restitutae filiae\n
EDCS-14500020.translation: Ientumaro made this tomb while alive for Adnamius and his wife Secundina, and for his son-in-law Vitalis, and for his daughter Restituta.
EDCS-14500020.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Ientumaro for his family members, including his wife, son-in-law, and daughter, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500021.text_edition: \n Ategnatae Amuronis filiae optimae coniugi Ateboduus Vercombogi filius vivus fecit et sibi et suis\n
EDCS-14500021.translation: Ateboduus, son of Vercombogus, made this tomb while alive for Ategnata, daughter of Amoris, his excellent wife, and for himself and his family.
EDCS-14500021.summary: This is a funerary monument built by Ateboduus for his wife Ategnata and their family, likely from a Roman provincial context.

EDCS-14500022.text_edition: \n Cupitianus Cupitinaes sic filius Cupitinae et Asellioni parentibus optimis et sibi vivus fecit et Saturnino avo militi defunctis\n
EDCS-14500022.translation: Cupitianus, son of Cupitinaes, for his parents Cupitina and Asellio, the best parents, and for himself, made this while alive, and for Saturninus his grandfather, a soldier.
EDCS-14500022.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Cupitianus for his parents and grandfather, a soldier, indicating it was built during his lifetime, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500024.text_edition: \n Constanti Vottici Provincialis nnum XVIII Votticius Cupitus et Votticia Ategenta parentes vivi fecerunt et sibi\n
EDCS-14500024.translation: Votticius Cupitus and Votticia Ategenta, parents, made this tomb while alive for their son Constantius, son of Votticus of Provincia, aged 18, and for themselves.
EDCS-14500024.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by parents Votticius Cupitus and Votticia Ategenta for their deceased son Constantius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500025.text_edition: \n Cauti Lucius Albius Atticus et Caius Albius Avitus\n
EDCS-14500025.translation: Lucius Albius Atticus and Caius Albius Avitus.
EDCS-14500025.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or a marker of ownership by Lucius Albius Atticus and Caius Albius Avitus, possibly related to a public building or a joint venture.

EDCS-14500027.text_edition: \n Syrascus Valerianus Eutyches Albii Maximi Nemesi Augustae\n
EDCS-14500027.translation: Syrascus Valerianus Eutyches for Nemesis Augusta, wife of Albius Maximus.
EDCS-14500027.summary: This is an inscription dedicating something to Nemesis Augusta, wife of Albius Maximus, by Syrascus Valerianus Eutyches, likely a votive or funerary dedication.

EDCS-14500028.text_edition: \n Sanctius Herennius Epictetus INEO gratias agit\n
EDCS-14500028.summary: This is a simple inscription of thanks by Sanctius Herennius Epictetus, possibly recording a favor received or a general expression of gratitude, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500030.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Vibio Afinio Gallo Veldumniano Volusiano Pio Felici Invicto Augusto ordo Teurniensium devotus numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-14500030.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Afinius Gallus Veldumnianus Volusianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, the town council of Teurnia, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-14500030.summary: This is a dedication by the town council of Teurnia to Emperor Gallus Volusianus, likely dating to his reign (251-253 AD).

EDCS-14500032.text_edition: \n Atitoni Attalonis et Lediae Casilli filiae Atismerius\n
EDCS-14500032.translation: Atismerius, son of Atitonius and Ledia Casilla.
EDCS-14500032.summary: This is a simple genealogical marker identifying Atismerius and his parents, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500033.text_edition: \n Ingenuo Optati et Exepiae parentibus Optatus et sibi et Celerinae coniugi vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14500033.translation: Optatus made this, while alive, for his parents Optatus and Exepia, and for himself and his wife Celerina.
EDCS-14500033.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Optatus for his parents and himself and his wife, indicating a desire for a shared burial space, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500034.text_edition: \n Lucio Iunio Luci filio Vegeto annorum Tettia Secunda coniunx \n
EDCS-14500034.translation: To Lucius Junius Vegetus, son of Lucius; Tettia Secunda, his wife.
EDCS-14500034.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Lucius Junius Vegetus by his wife Tettia Secunda, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500035.text_edition: \n Caius Lollius Trophimus et Lollia Probata vivi fecerunt sibi et Florentinae Bruti pientissimae obitae annorum XXVIII\n
EDCS-14500035.translation: Gaius Lollius Trophimus and Lollia Probata made this, while alive, for themselves and for Florentina Bruti, most dutiful, who died at age 28.
EDCS-14500035.summary: This is a funerary monument jointly erected by Gaius Lollius Trophimus and Lollia Probata for themselves and for Florentina, who died at 28, indicating a shared burial plan, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500037.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Onotnius ac Rufinae karissimae coniugi @(obitae) nnorum XXXV sibi suis vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14500037.translation: To the Divine Shades of Othonius and his dearest wife Rufina, who died at 35 years; he made this for himself and his family while alive.
EDCS-14500037.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Othonius for himself, his deceased wife Rufina, and his family, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500041.text_edition: \n Invicto Deo Mithrae pro salute Marci P Potentis Ursulus libertus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14500041.translation: To the unconquered god Mithras, Ursulus, a freedman, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for the health of Marcus P. Potens.
EDCS-14500041.summary: This is a votive offering to the god Mithras by the freedman Ursulus for the health of Marcus P. Potens, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500042.text_edition: \n Tinco Redsati filius et Ban<o=A>na Venimari filia uxor vivi fecerunt Ambidrabo sic filio equiti auxsiliario sic annorum XX hic situs est \n
EDCS-14500042.translation: Tinco, son of Redsatus, and Banona, daughter of Venimarus, his wife, made this while alive for their son Ambidrabus, a cavalryman in the auxiliary cohort, who died at age 20. He is buried here.
EDCS-14500042.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by parents for their son, an auxiliary cavalryman who died at 20, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500044.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caiantiae Sumariae coniugi optimae obitae nnorum LXXX Caiantius Primitivus vivus fecit et sibi\n
EDCS-14500044.translation: To the Divine Shades of Caiantia Summaria, an excellent wife, who died at 80 years; Caiantius Primitivus made this while alive for himself and for her.
EDCS-14500044.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Caiantius Primitivus for his wife Caiantia Summaria and himself, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500045.text_edition: \n Seccioni Tottiionis filio et Acceptae Montani filiae uxsori sic fili-- viv-- fec---\n
EDCS-14500045.translation: To Seccio, son of Tottio, and his wife, daughter of Montanus, and his son(s)... made...
EDCS-14500045.summary: This is a partially preserved funerary monument for Seccio, his wife, and son(s), dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500046.text_edition: \n AVIVL EA MS et Venae filiae\n
EDCS-14500046.translation: To Avia (daughter of) Aemus and Vena.
EDCS-14500046.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Avia, daughter of Aemus and Vena.

EDCS-14500047.text_edition: \n Noviae Spuri filiae Maximae et Noviae Spuri filiae Proculae Lucius Regontius Natalis et Lucius Regontius Luci filius Iustus vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-14500047.translation: Lucius Regontius Natalis and Lucius Regontius Iustus, son of Lucius, made (this) while alive for Novia Maxima, daughter of Spurius, and Novia Procula, daughter of Spurius.
EDCS-14500047.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Lucius Regontius Natalis and Lucius Regontius Iustus for Novia Maxima and Novia Procula, daughters of Spurius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500049.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Depulsori Vibius Fortunatus cum suis urbanis iussit libens merito\n
EDCS-14500049.translation: Vibius Fortunatus, with his household, willingly and deservedly dedicated (this) to Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Repeller.
EDCS-14500049.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Vibius Fortunatus and his family, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500050.text_edition: \n Saturninus Severi vivus fecit sibi et Kaniae Ursulae coniugi carissimae Accepto filio annorum VIIII Masculo filio annorum XIII\n
EDCS-14500050.translation: Saturninus, son of Severus, made (this) while alive for himself and for his dearest wife Kania Ursula, and his son Acceptus, aged 9, and his son Masculus, aged 13.
EDCS-14500050.summary: This is a funerary monument commissioned by Saturninus for himself and his family, including his wife Kania Ursula and two sons, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500051.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vibianus vivus fecit sibi et L Quientillae @(obitae) annorum XXXV\n
EDCS-14500051.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Vibianus made (this) while alive for himself and for L. Quientilla, deceased at the age of 35.
EDCS-14500051.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by Vibianus for himself and his deceased wife L. Quientilla.

EDCS-14500052.text_edition: \n Vegetoni et Itucae Civilis servis Longinus filius fecit\n
EDCS-14500052.translation: Longinus, son (of) ?, made (this) for Vegeto and Ituca, slaves of Civilis.
EDCS-14500052.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by Longinus for the slaves Vegeto and Ituca, belonging to Civilis.

EDCS-14500053.text_edition: \n Calendinus Ategnati filius et Secunda Senicionis filia coius sic carissuma sic filii fecerum sic\n
EDCS-14500053.translation: Calendinus, son of Ategnatus, and Secunda, daughter of Senicio, his most beloved wife, and his sons, made (this).
EDCS-14500053.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Calendinus and Secunda for themselves and their sons.

EDCS-14500054.text_edition: \n Phileto Motri filius et Severa Calvi filia vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-14500054.translation: Philetus, son of Motrus, and Severa, daughter of Calvus, made (this) while alive.
EDCS-14500054.summary: This funerary monument was jointly commissioned by Philetus and Severa, likely a married couple.

EDCS-14500056.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sillvia Vindilla et Silvio Vindil<l=I>o et Secundae parentibus viva fecit et sibi \n
EDCS-14500056.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Silvia Vindilla made (this) while alive for her parents Sillvius Vindillus and Secunda, and for herself.
EDCS-14500056.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Silvia Vindilla for her parents, Sillvius Vindillus and Secunda, and for herself.

EDCS-14500057.text_edition: \n erno Secundi filio Secundinus filius vivus fecit et Boniatae uxori\n
EDCS-14500057.translation: Secundinus, son of Secundus, made (this) while alive for Ernus, son of Secundus, and for Boniata, his wife.
EDCS-14500057.summary: This funerary monument was commissioned by Secundinus for his father Ernus and his mother Boniata.

EDCS-14500058.text_edition: \n Publius Petronius A Sincoria vivi fecerunt sibi et \n
EDCS-14500058.translation: Publius Petronius and A Sincoria, while alive, made [this for] themselves and...
EDCS-14500058.summary: This is a funerary or dedicatory inscription by Publius Petronius and A Sincoria, likely for themselves and others, possibly a tomb or altar.

EDCS-14500059.text_edition: \n Pessae filiae S cius et mmi filia uxor ae filiae nnorum \n
EDCS-14500059.translation: Pessae, daughter of Scius, and Mmi, daughter of... and wife of... and of... daughters of...
EDCS-14500059.summary: This inscription is a fragmented funerary epitaph for several women, listing their names and parentage.

EDCS-14500060.text_edition: \n Titus Valentinius Titi libertus Firmus et Valentinia Prisci liberta Sucela vivi fecerunt sibi et Firmino Prisci servo filio annorum XVIII\n
EDCS-14500060.translation: Titus Valentinius Firmus, freedman of Titus, and Valentinia Sucela, freedwoman of Priscus, while alive made [this] for themselves and for Firminus, slave of Priscus, their son, aged 18.
EDCS-14500060.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating two freed persons and their son, Firminus, likely marking a family tomb.

EDCS-14500061.text_edition: \n Deo omnipotenti Mithrae Invicto RSVus beneficiarius \n
EDCS-14500061.translation: To the all-powerful, unconquered god Mithras, RSVus, beneficiary [of a legate], ...
EDCS-14500061.summary: This is a dedication to the god Mithras by RSVus, identified as a beneficiary officer, likely on a shrine or altar.

EDCS-14500063.text_edition: \n Belesti Augustae Titus Tapponius Macrinus et Iulia Sexti filia Cara cum suis votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-14500063.translation: To Belestus Augusta, Titus Tapponius Macrinus and Iulia Cara, daughter of Sextus, with their family, gladly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-14500063.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to the goddess Belestus Augusta by Titus Tapponius Macrinus and Iulia Cara.

EDCS-14500064.text_edition: \n Belino Augusto sacrum Caius Marius Severus donum dedit\n
EDCS-14500064.translation: Sacred to Belinus Augustus, Caius Marius Severus gave this offering.
EDCS-14500064.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription to the god Belinus Augustus by Caius Marius Severus.

EDCS-14500065.text_edition: \n Dis deabusque omnibus sacrum Marcus Ulpius Servatus et Peccia Primitiva ex visu pro et suis omnibus posuerunt\n
EDCS-14500065.translation: Sacred to all the gods and goddesses, Marcus Ulpius Servatus and Peccia Primitiva, by vision, placed [this] for themselves and for all their family.
EDCS-14500065.summary: This inscription records a dedication to all gods and goddesses by Marcus Ulpius Servatus and Peccia Primitiva, likely placed after a divine revelation.

EDCS-14500066.text_edition: \n Eponae Augustae sacrum Gaius Licinius Civilis beneficiarius consularis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14500066.translation: Sacred to Epona Augusta, Gaius Licinius Civilis, beneficiary of a consular legate, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-14500066.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to the goddess Epona Augusta by Gaius Licinius Civilis, an official's beneficiary.

EDCS-14500073.text_edition: \n Genio Augusti sacrum iuventutis Manliensium gentiles qui consistunt in Manlia in hoc donum dedit Campanius Acutus HS nummum C\n
EDCS-14500073.translation: Sacred to the Genius of Augustus, the youth of the Manliensian tribe, who reside in Manlia, gave this offering: Campanius Acutus [for] 100 sesterces.
EDCS-14500073.summary: This is a dedication to the Genius of Augustus by the youth of the Manliensian tribe in Manlia, funded by Campanius Acutus.

EDCS-14500075.text_edition: \n Genio Noricorum Mascillius Mascillinus et Secundinius Vibianus votum solverunt libentes laeti merito\n
EDCS-14500075.translation: To the Genius of the Norici, Mascillius Mascillinus and Secundinius Vibianus fulfilled their vow, gladly and happily and deservedly.
EDCS-14500075.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to the Genius of the Norici by Mascillius Mascillinus and Secundinius Vibianus.

EDCS-14500076.text_edition: \n Genio Mercuri Auggustorum liberti tabularii Aurelius Fortunatus libertus\n
EDCS-14500076.translation: To Mercury Augustus, the freedmen of the records office, Aurelius Fortunatus, freedman.
EDCS-14500076.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Genius of Mercury Augustus by freedmen of the records office, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-14500085.text_edition: \n Iovi Depulsori et Nymphis Quintus Luccius Maximus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14500085.translation: To Jupiter the Repeller and to the Nymphs, Quintus Luccius Maximus willingly paid his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-14500085.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Jupiter Depulsor and the Nymphs by Quintus Luccius Maximus, likely a private dedication.

EDCS-14500086.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Caius Masculinius Masculus |(centurio) legionis I adiutricis frumentarius\n
EDCS-14500086.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest; Caius Masculinius Masculus, a frumentarius (supply officer) of the First Adjutant Legion.
EDCS-14500086.summary: This inscription records a sacred dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by a centurion and supply officer of the Legio I Adjutrix, probably dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-14500087.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Trebius Marci filius Palatina Alfius equo publico praefectus iure dicundo Aquileiae conductor ferrariarum Noricarum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14500087.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Marcus Trebius, son of Marcus, of the Palatine tribe, Alfius, an equestrian, prefect with the right to judge, and contractor of the Norican iron mines in Aquileia, willingly paid his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-14500087.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Trebius Alfius, an equestrian official and contractor in Aquileia, likely from the late 1st to 3rd century AD.

EDCS-14500089.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Depulsori Tiberius Claudius Quintilianus et Rutenia Severina pro se et suis votum dederunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-14500089.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Repeller, Tiberius Claudius Quintilianus and Rutenia Severina, for themselves and their family, willingly and deservedly gave their vow.
EDCS-14500089.summary: This inscription records a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Depulsor by Tiberius Claudius Quintilianus and Rutenia Severina on behalf of their family, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-14500090.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Severinius Silvinus et Aurelius Boniatus milites legionis II Italicae Severianae votum solverunt libentes laeti merito\n
EDCS-14500090.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus, Severinius Silvinus and Aurelius Boniatus, soldiers of the Second Italian Severian Legion, willingly, joyfully, and deservedly paid their vows.
EDCS-14500090.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus by two soldiers of the Legio II Italica Severiana, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-14500094.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae pro salute Antoni Severini Helvius Acceptinus ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-14500094.translation: To the Unconquered God Mithras, Helvius Acceptinus, in fulfillment of his vow, placed this for the safety of Antonius Severinus.
EDCS-14500094.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Mithras by Helvius Acceptinus for the health of Antonius Severinus, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-14500095.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae templum vetustate conlabsum sic quot sic fuit per annos amplius L desertum Aurelius Hermodorus vir perfectissimus praeses provinciae Norici mediterranei a novo restitui fecit quot sic aedificatum est divo Maximiano VIII et Maximino iterum Auggustis consulibus Quartinio Ursiniano curante\n
EDCS-14500095.translation: To the Unconquered God Mithras, the temple, which had collapsed from age and had been deserted for over 50 years, Aurelius Hermodorus, a most accomplished man, governor of the province of Noricum Mediterraneum, had rebuilt as it was, when it was built in the consulship of the divine Maximian for the second time and of Maximius for the third time, Quartinius Ursianus overseeing.
EDCS-14500095.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding of a temple to Mithras by the governor of Noricum Mediterraneum, Aurelius Hermodorus, during the consulship of Maximian and Maximius (likely Maximian II and Maximinus III, c. 307 AD).

EDCS-14500096.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae Diadumenus Nicolai Augusti dispensatoris arcarius regni Norici donum dedit\n
EDCS-14500096.translation: To the Unconquered God Mithras, Diadumenus, freedman and cashier of the Augustan treasury of the kingdom of Noricum, gave this gift.
EDCS-14500096.summary: This inscription records a gift to Mithras by Diadumenus, an imperial freedman and treasurer in Noricum, likely from the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-14500098.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae in honorem domus divinae Eppius Ariminensis filius\n
EDCS-14500098.translation: To the Unconquered God Mithras, in honor of the divine house, Eppius Ariminensis, son of [---].
EDCS-14500098.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Mithras in honor of the imperial family by Eppius Ariminensis, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-14500099.text_edition: \n Pro salute Augusti in honorem domus divinae Soli Invicto Mythrae sic Hilarus Augusti libertus tabularius procuratoris regni Norici et Epictetus arkarius sic Augusti nostri templum vetustate conlapsum sumptu suo cum pictura refecerunt Imperatore domino nostro Gordiano Augusto et Aviola consulibus sacerdote Licinio Marcello patre sac(rorum) dedicatum VIII Kalendas Iulias Quinto Vi\n
EDCS-14500099.translation: For the health of Augustus and in honor of the divine house, Hilarus, freedman of Augustus, a clerk of the procurator of the province of Noricum, and Epictetus, an arkarius (treasurer) of our Augustus, rebuilt this temple of the Unconquered Sun Mithras, which had collapsed from age, at their own expense, with paintings, dedicating it on the eighth day before the Kalends of July, during the consulship of our Lord Emperor Gordian Augustus and Aviola, with Licinius Marcellus as the father of the sacred rites.
EDCS-14500099.summary: This inscription records the renovation of a temple dedicated to Sol Invictus Mithras, funded by two imperial officials, and dated to the reign of Gordian III.

EDCS-14500100.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Invicto Iuventinus leo votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-14500100.translation: To the Unconquered Sun God, Iuventinus willingly and joyfully paid his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-14500100.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to the Unconquered Sun God by Iuventinus.

EDCS-14500101.text_edition: \n Invicto patrio Ulbius Gaianus praefectus vehiculorum\n
EDCS-14500101.translation: To the Unconquered Father, Ulbius Gaianus, prefect of the transport service.
EDCS-14500101.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Unconquered Father by Ulbius Gaianus, an official overseeing transportation.

EDCS-14500102.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae Ulpius Valerius speculator legionis primae Noricorum votum a patre suscepto solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-14500102.translation: To the Unconquered God Mithras, Ulpius Valerius, a scout of the First Norican Legion, willingly and joyfully paid a vow undertaken by his father, as was deserved.
EDCS-14500102.summary: This inscription records a soldier's fulfillment of a vow to Sol Invictus Mithras, made by his father.

EDCS-14500103.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto M<i=E>thras Ursinus donum posu<i=E>t \n
EDCS-14500103.translation: To the Unconquered God Mithras, Ursinus placed this offering.
EDCS-14500103.summary: This inscription records an offering made to the Unconquered God Mithras by Ursinus.

EDCS-14500104.text_edition: \n Nemnic Lucius Barbius Luci libertus Pilocles sic donum dedit\n
EDCS-14500104.translation: To Nemesis, Lucius Barbius Pilocles, freedman of Lucius, gave this offering.
EDCS-14500104.summary: This inscription records an offering made to Nemesis by Lucius Barbius Pilocles.

EDCS-14500105.text_edition: \n Noreiae Augustae sacrum Quintus Fabius Modestus domo Roma decurio alae I Augustae Thracum phialam argenteam pondo II |(quadrantem) emblemata Noreiae aurea uncias duas donum dedit\n
EDCS-14500105.translation: Sacred to Noreia Augusta, Quintus Fabius Modestus from Rome, a decurion of the First Augusta Thracian Auxiliary Cavalry Wing, gave as an offering a silver phiale weighing two and a quarter pounds, and golden emblems for Noreia weighing two ounces.
EDCS-14500105.summary: This inscription records a dedication of silver and gold items to the goddess Noreia Augusta by Quintus Fabius Modestus, a cavalry decurion.

EDCS-14500107.text_edition: \n Noreiae Chrysanthus Cypaeri Tiberi Claudi Caesaris Augusti servi vicarius votum solvit\n
EDCS-14500107.translation: To Noreia, Chrysanthus, deputy of Cypaerus, slave of Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus, paid his vow.
EDCS-14500107.summary: This inscription records a vow paid to Noreia by Chrysanthus, a slave and deputy serving an imperial official.

EDCS-14500108.text_edition: \n Isidi Noreiae votum solvit libens merito pro salute Quinti Septuei Clementis conductoris ferrariarum Noricarum Pannoniarum Dalmatarum et Tiberi Claudi Heraclae et Cnaei Octavi Secundi procuratorum ferrariarum Quintus Septueius Valens procurator ferrariarum\n
EDCS-14500108.translation: To Isis Noreia, Quintus Septueius Valens, procurator of the iron mines of Noricum, Pannonia, and Dalmatia, willingly and deservedly paid a vow for the health of Quintus Septueius Clementis, contractor of the iron mines of Noricum, Pannonia, and Dalmatia, and for Tiberius Claudius Heracla and Gnaeus Octavius Secundus, procurators of the iron mines.
EDCS-14500108.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Isis Noreia made by a mining procurator for the health of various officials and contractors involved in the Norican, Pannonian, and Dalmatian iron mines.

EDCS-14500109.text_edition: \n Noreiae Isidi E Aulus Trebonius procurator Augusti provinciae Norici \n
EDCS-14500109.translation: To Noreia Isis, Aulus Trebonius, imperial procurator of the province of Noricum.
EDCS-14500109.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Noreia Isis by Aulus Trebonius, the imperial procurator of Noricum.

EDCS-14500110.text_edition: \n Victoriae Augustae sacrum Marcus Bellicius Saturninus tribunus cohortis I Flaviae Brittonum et Finitia Verbicia cum Bellicia Saturnina et Bellicio Finitiano filiis votum susceptum solverunt laeti libentes merito Paterno et Archesilao consulibus\n
EDCS-14500110.translation: To the August Victory. Marcus Bellicius Saturninus, tribune of the I Flavia Brittonum cohort, and Finitia Verbicia, with their children Bellicia Saturnina and Bellicius Finitianus, joyfully, willingly, and deservedly fulfilled their vow. In the consulship of Paternus and Archesilaus.
EDCS-14500110.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to the August Victory by a military tribune and his family, dated by consuls.

EDCS-14500112.text_edition: \n Victoriae Augustae sacrum Flavius Tacitus summus curator alae Augustae - Aelius Martius summus curator cohortis I Aeliae Brittonum pro se suisque omnibus votum solverunt laeti libentes merito Kalendis Februariis Pio et Pontiano consulibus\n
EDCS-14500112.translation: To the August Victory. Flavius Tacitus, chief curator of the Ala Augusta, and Aelius Martius, chief curator of the I Aeliae Brittonum cohort, for themselves and all their kin, joyfully, willingly, and deservedly fulfilled their vow on the Kalends of February. In the consulship of Pius and Pontianus.
EDCS-14500112.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to the August Victory made by two military officers, dated by consuls and a specific date.

EDCS-14500113.text_edition: \n Victoriae Augustae pro salute Luci Lydaci Honorati Lucius Lydacius Ingenuus IIvir iure dicundo et Rufia Severa parentes votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-14500113.translation: To the August Victory. For the health of Lucius Lydacus Honoratus. Lucius Lydacius Ingenuus, duovir with judicial authority, and Rufia Severa, his parents, willingly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-14500113.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to the August Victory for the health of an individual, made by his parents who held local magistracies.

EDCS-14500114.text_edition: \n Victoriae Augustae Lucius Lydacius Ingenuus IIvir iure dicundo adlectus sacerdos et flamen \n
EDCS-14500114.translation: To the August Victory. Lucius Lydacius Ingenuus, duovir with judicial authority, appointed priest and flamen.
EDCS-14500114.summary: This inscription records the appointment of Lucius Lydacius Ingenuus to priesthood and flaminates, dedicated to the August Victory.

EDCS-14500115.text_edition: \n Aulus Poblicius Decimi libertus Antiochus Tiberius Barbius Quinti Publi libertus Tiber Marcus Gallicinus Vindili filius Lucius Barbius Luci libertus Philotaerus procurator Craxsantus sic Barbi Publi servus\n
EDCS-14500115.translation: Aulus Poblicius Antiochus, freedman of Decimus; Tiberius Barbius Philotaerus, freedman of Quintus Publius Tiber; Marcus Gallicinus, son of Vindilus; Lucius Barbius, freedman of Lucius; the procurator Craxsantus, the slave of Barbius Publius.
EDCS-14500115.summary: This inscription lists several individuals, including freedmen and a procurator, possibly related to a property or administrative record.

EDCS-14500117.text_edition: \n DOMINI SAR PCHIS CENCURA FRanCII C SVRVSA lius Secundinus s Secundus Rusticus Victor tus A S D\n
EDCS-14500117.translation: To the Lord Sarpchis Cencura, the Frankish and Sorusan [region?].lius Secundinus, S. Secundus Rusticus Victor... [dedicates?]...
EDCS-14500117.summary: This inscription, with significant lacunae, appears to be a dedication to a deity or local lord named Sarpchis, possibly related to a Frankish or Sorusan region.

EDCS-14500118.text_edition: \n beneficiarius consularis legionis II Italicae piae fidelis Augusti nostri ut vovit dedicavit Pio et Pontiano consulibus VIIII Kalendas Iulias\n
EDCS-14500118.translation: By the beneficiarius of the consular legate of the II Italica pia fidelis legion, of our Emperor, as he vowed, he dedicated. In the consulship of Pius and Pontianus, on the ninth day before the Kalends of July.
