EDCS-14500944.summary: A funerary epitaph for Obiledo Quinti and Speratia Maxima, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-14500945.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Hadriani filio divi Traiani Parthici nepoti divi Nervae pronepoti Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis consuli III patri patriae ala I Augusta Thracum optimo principi\n
EDCS-14500945.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, consul for the third time, father of the country, to the First Wing of Thracians, Augusta, the best of princes.
EDCS-14500945.summary: An honorific inscription for Emperor Antoninus Pius, addressing him as the recipient of honors from the ala I Augusta Thracum, dating to his reign (138-161 CE).

EDCS-14500946.text_edition: \n Caio Iulio Agricolae veterano exs sic armorum custodi alae I Augustae annorum XXXXV Titus Aelius Quartio veteranus alae eiusdem heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-14500946.translation: Titus Aelius Quartio, a veteran of the same wing, aged 45, had this made for Gaius Julius Agricola, veteran and former armour custodian of the ala I Augusta.
EDCS-14500946.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Julius Agricola, erected by his heir Titus Aelius Quartio, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-14500947.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelio Iusto anno--- LXX ---? f(aciendum?) c(uravit?) --- \n
EDCS-14500947.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Aelius Justus, aged [---] LXX [---], [he/she/they] made [it].
EDCS-14500947.summary: A fragmentary funerary epitaph for Aelius Justus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-14500948.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aracintho Petroni Prisci tribuni laticlavii servo collegia Herculis et Dianae fecerunt\n
EDCS-14500948.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. The collegia of Hercules and Diana made this for Aracinthus, slave of Petronius Priscus, a tribune with the laticlavian rank.
EDCS-14500948.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aracinthus, a slave, commissioned by the guilds of Hercules and Diana, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-14500949.text_edition: \n August- sacrum in memoriam Marci Ulpi Sperati patris optimi Marcus Ulpius Spectatus IIvir iure dicundo augur municipii Aeli Cetiensium\n
EDCS-14500949.translation: Sacred to the Divine. Marcus Ulpius Spectatus, IIvir for administering justice and augur of the municipium of Aelia Cetium, in memory of his excellent father Marcus Ulpius Speratus.
EDCS-14500949.summary: A dedication to an ancestor by Marcus Ulpius Spectatus, a prominent local official in Aelia Cetium, likely from the late 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-14500951.text_edition: \n obito expeditione Parthica annorum V et Tito Aelio et Aeliae Ursae et ? Aelio Verino vivo ex beneficiario procuratoris Augusti filius vivus fecit ni Aquilini filiae uxori optimae \n
EDCS-14500951.translation: To the departed. Marcus Aquilinus, son of Titus Aelius and Aelia Ursa, and [---] Aelius Verinus, while alive, made this monument, being a son of a beneficiary of the imperial procurator, and for his daughter, Aquilina, his most excellent wife, while alive. [---]
EDCS-14500951.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Marcus Aquilinus for his deceased family members, likely in the 2nd century CE, commemorating his parents and wife.

EDCS-14500952.text_edition: \n Marco Nammio Marci liberto Surioni nnorum L Ulpius Speratus amico fecit\n
EDCS-14500952.translation: Ulpius Speratus made this for Marcus Nammio Surio, freedman of Marcus, aged 50, his friend.
EDCS-14500952.summary: This epitaph records that Ulpius Speratus erected a monument for his friend, Marcus Nammio Surio, a freedman, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-14500953.text_edition: \n Marco Sextio Vettoniano aedili municipii Aelii Cetiensium annorum LXX et Vindae Terti filiae coniugi annorum L Publio Aelio Marciano filio militi cohortis IIII praetoriae annorum XX stipendiorum III et Avvae Maxsimi sic filiae coniugi annorum LXXX Marco Sextio Sextiano quaestorio municipii Aelii Cetiensium annorum L filio Potta Croti filia et Marco Sextio Sammo annorum XLVII filio suo et ae annorum et Sextio Sammo militi legionis II Severianae annorum XXV\n
EDCS-14500953.translation: To Marcus Sextius Vettonianus, aedile of the municipium of Aelius Cetiensis, aged 70, and to his wife Vinda Tertia, aged 50, and to their son Publius Aelius Marcianus, a soldier of the IV Praetorian cohort, aged 20, with 3 years of service, and to his wife Avva, daughter of Maxsimus, aged 80, and to their son Marcus Sextius Sextianus, quaestor of the municipium of Aelius Cetiensis, aged 50, son of Potta daughter of Crotius, and to their son Marcus Sextius Sammo, aged 47, and to [---], aged [---], and to Sextius Sammo, soldier of II Severiana legion, aged 25.
EDCS-14500953.summary: This extensive epitaph commemorates multiple members of the Sextius family, including an aedile and a quaestor of the municipium of Aelius Cetiensis, and a praetorian guardsman, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-14500954.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tutuia Crispae liberta viva fecit sibi et Obilo Iusti liberto marito annorum LX\n
EDCS-14500954.translation: To the departed spirits. Tutuia Crispa, a freedwoman, made this while alive for herself and for Obilo Iustus, freedman, her husband, aged 60.
EDCS-14500954.summary: This funerary inscription records a freedwoman, Tutuia Crispa, dedicating a monument for herself and her deceased husband, Obilo Iustus, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-14500955.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Conalius Domiti filius vivus fecit sibi et Ioventinae Curionis filiae\n
EDCS-14500955.translation: To the departed spirits. Conalius, son of Domitius, made this while alive for himself and for Ioventina, daughter of Curio.
EDCS-14500955.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Conalius, son of Domitius, for himself and his wife Ioventina, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-14500956.text_edition: \n V E Ingenuae Primigeni filiae matri\n
EDCS-14500956.translation: To Ingenua, daughter of Primigenius, mother.
EDCS-14500956.summary: This very brief inscription records Ingenua, daughter of Primigenius, as a mother, likely a dedication on her tombstone.

EDCS-14500957.text_edition: \n Successus et Ursina coniux vivi fecerunt sibi et Successiano filio obito Romae annorum XX ex Bon Belnaes servo\n
EDCS-14500957.translation: Successus and his wife Ursina, while alive, made this for themselves and for their son Successianus, deceased in Rome at age 20, former slave of Belnaes.
EDCS-14500957.summary: This monument was erected by Successus and his wife Ursina for their deceased son Successianus, who had been a former slave, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-14500958.text_edition: \n Tertius Sennonis miles cohortis I Flaviae Brittonum stipendiorum VI annorum XXI Lucius Baebius Buttus heres et parentes vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-14500958.translation: Tertius Sennonis, a soldier of the I Flavia Brittonum cohort, aged 21 with 6 years of service, had this made by Lucius Baebius Buttus, his heir, and his parents, while alive.
EDCS-14500958.summary: This funerary inscription records that Lucius Baebius Buttus and his parents erected a monument for Tertius Sennonis, a soldier in the I Flavia Brittonum cohort, likely in the late 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-14500959.text_edition: \n Aiuccioni Nigrini filio annorum XVI et Secundinae filiae annorum VI Cupita mater viva fecit et sibi\n
EDCS-14500959.translation: To Aiuccio, son of Nigrinus, aged 16, and to his daughter Secundina, aged 6. Cupita, their mother, made this while alive for herself.
EDCS-14500959.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Cupita for her deceased son Aiuccio and daughter Secundina, and for herself, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-14500960.text_edition: \n Marco Ulpio Melei filio Longino veterano annorum LX Firmus libertus faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-14500960.translation: Firmus, a freedman, saw to the construction of this for Marcus Ulpius Longinus, son of Meleius, a veteran, aged 60.
EDCS-14500960.summary: This epitaph records that Firmus, a freedman, arranged for the construction of a monument for Marcus Ulpius Longinus, a veteran, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-14500962.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Seccius Secundinus veteranus legionis II Italicae piae fidelis et Iulia Severio coniux eius sibi et Secciae Secundinae filiae et Mariis Maximo et Secundo nepotibus suis vivi fecerunt et Iulio Apricio filio militi legionis suprascriptae beneficiario praefecti stipendiorum VI @(obito) annorum XXV\n
EDCS-14500962.translation: To the divine shades. Seccius Secundinus, a veteran of the Second Italian Legion, pious and faithful, and his wife Iulia Severio, made this for themselves, their daughter Seccia Secundina, and their grandsons Marius Maximus and Marius Secundus, while still alive. And to their son, Iulius Apricius, a soldier of the aforesaid legion, beneficiarius of the prefect of studies, who died at the age of 25, they [also made this].
EDCS-14500962.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by a veteran and his wife for themselves, their daughter, grandsons, and son, who died at 25, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-14500963.text_edition: \n Marmogio sacrum Marcus V Rutilius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14500963.translation: Sacred to Marmogius. Marcus V. Rutilius willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-14500963.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus V. Rutilius, likely dedicated to a local deity or hero named Marmogius, probably from the Roman period.

EDCS-14500964.text_edition: \n Aelio Valenti veterano legionis II Italicae obito annorum LX et Annamatiae Verae coniugi eiius sic et Aelio Eutichiano sic liberto eiius sic Aurelius Ursinianus immunis legionis supra scriptae primus heres et Vera coniux Valentis fiendum sic curaaverunt et Luus libertus eiius sic\n
EDCS-14500964.translation: Aurelius Ursinianus, immunis of the aforesaid legion, first heir, and Vera, wife of Valens, took care to have this made for Aelius Valens, veteran of the Second Italian Legion, who died at the age of 60, and for his wife Annamatia Vera, and for his freedman Aelius Eutichianus, and also for Luus, his freedman.
EDCS-14500964.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a veteran of the 2nd Italian Legion and his family, including his wife, freedman, and another freedman, arranged by his heir and wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500965.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a name on a tomb or monument, possibly belonging to Vibius Togionis.

EDCS-14500966.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Publicio Ingenuo nnorum VIIII Caius Publicius Synecdemus nnorum L testamento f(ieri)? i(ussit)\n
EDCS-14500966.translation: To the divine shades. Caius Publicius Synecdemus, aged 50, ordered this to be made by his will for Caius Publicius Ingenuus, aged 9.
EDCS-14500966.summary: This is a funerary inscription commissioned by will by Caius Publicius Synecdemus for his son Caius Publicius Ingenuus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500967.text_edition: \n Secundinus vi filius annorum LX Secundina ri filia I C R filius\n
EDCS-14500967.translation: Secundinus, son of Vi, aged 60. Secundina, daughter of Ri, [son of] I C R.
EDCS-14500967.summary: This inscription appears to be a funerary record listing individuals with their patronymics and ages, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-14500968.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Claudi-
EDCS-14500968.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription beginning a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, with the name Claudius.

EDCS-14500969.text_edition: \n Nymphis Augustis sacrum Mallius Vicarius et Valerius Crispinus aediles collegii iuvenum votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-14500969.translation: Sacred to the August Nymphs. Mallius Vicarius and Valerius Crispinus, aediles of the association of young men, willingly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-14500969.summary: This is a votive inscription by two aediles of a youth association, dedicated to the August Nymphs, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500971.text_edition: \n Aulo Barbio Auli filio Grato Velina <a>nnorum LXXV et Cominiae Titi filiae Pupae nnorum LXX Tito Barbio Auli filio Quinto militi legionis XV Apollinaris nnorum XXV hic siti sunt Aulus Barbius Adiutor Lucius Barbius Iustus Caius Barbius Aptus Barbia Tertia Barbia Hospita Barbia Satulla Barbia Lucia filii et filiae pro pietate\n
EDCS-14500971.translation: Here lie Aulus Barbius Gratus of the Velina tribe, son of Aulus, aged 75, and Cominia Pupa, daughter of Titus, aged 70, and Titus Barbius, son of Aulus, a soldier of the Fifteenth Apollinaris Legion, aged 25. Aulus Barbius Adiutor, Lucius Barbius Iustus, Caius Barbius Aptus, Barbia Tertia, Barbia Hospita, Barbia Satulla, Barbia Lucia, sons and daughters, for their filial piety.
EDCS-14500971.summary: This is a funerary monument for Aulus Barbius Gratus, his wife Cominia Pupa, their son Titus Barbius (a soldier), and mentions several other children who erected it for filial piety, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500972.text_edition: \n Aurelius I Celeia signifer legionis II Italicae Annamato militi @(obito) annorum stipendiorum XIIX et Aano @(obito) annorum fecit et sibi et Epictesi coniugi et Aureliis Priscin et Florent\n
EDCS-14500972.translation: Aurelius I[o] Celeius, standard-bearer of the Second Italian Legion, [made this] for Annamatus, a soldier, who died at the age of [XX] years and [X] campaigns, and for Aanus, who died at the age of [XX] years, and for himself and Epictesis, his wife, and Aurelia Prisca and Aurelia Florent[ina].
EDCS-14500972.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a standard-bearer of the 2nd Italian Legion for himself, his wife, and two other individuals, one of whom was a soldier who died young, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14500973.text_edition: \n Iulio Ptano optioni legionis II Italicae @(obito) annorum XL stipendiorum XXmarito et Rufinio Sta \n
EDCS-14500973.translation: To Julius Ptianus, optio of the Second Italian Legion, who died at the age of 40 with 20 years of service, [as a monument to] his husband and Rufinius Sta[tinus?].
EDCS-14500973.summary: This is a tombstone commemorating Julius Ptianus, an optio of the Second Italian Legion, likely erected by his husband.

EDCS-14500975.text_edition: \n Aelio Gannaloni @(obito) annorum LXX et Aeliae Secundae coniugi @(obitae) annorum XL Aelius Candidus \n
EDCS-14500975.translation: To Aelius Gannalo, who died at the age of 70, and to his wife Aelia Secunda, who died at the age of 40. Aelius Candidus [erected this].
EDCS-14500975.summary: This tombstone records the death of Aelius Gannalo and his wife Aelia Secunda, erected by Aelius Candidus.

EDCS-14500976.text_edition: \n Privatius Silvester vivus fecit sibi et Privatiae Silvinae filiae obitae annorum XII\n
EDCS-14500976.translation: Privatius Silvester, while alive, made this for himself and for his daughter Privatia Silvina, who died at the age of 12.
EDCS-14500976.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Privatius Silvester for himself and his deceased daughter.

EDCS-14500977.text_edition: \n Publi filio Fusco militi legionis II Italicae @(obito) annorum XLV Ulpia Aventina heres coniugi karissimo et sibi et Ulpio Mariniano filio vivi fecerunt <F=E>lorius Bellicianus \n
EDCS-14500977.translation: Ulpia Aventina, his heir, [made this] for her husband Publius Fuscus, soldier of the Second Italian Legion, who died at the age of 45, and for herself and for her son Ulpius Marianus, while alive. Florius Bellicianus [made it? or perhaps a witness?].
EDCS-14500977.summary: This is a tombstone erected by Ulpia Aventina for her deceased husband, a soldier of the Second Italian Legion, and for herself and their son.

EDCS-14500978.text_edition: \n lio nitoni tonis filio ex beneficario procuratoris \n
EDCS-14500978.translation: [This monument is for?] Antonius, son of ...lio, formerly beneficarius of a procurator.
EDCS-14500978.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary inscription for Antonius, who previously served as a beneficarius for a procurator.

EDCS-14500979.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Rustius Iunianus beneficarius consularis legionis II Italicae piae fidelis Severianae pro salute sua suorumque votum solvit libens merito Agricola et Clementino consulibus pridie Idus Maias\n
EDCS-14500979.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Marcus Rustius Iunianus, beneficarius of the consular governor of the Second Italian Legion, pious and faithful, Severian, fulfilled his vow for his own safety and that of his family, willingly and deservedly, with Agricola and Clementinus as consuls, on the day before the Ides of May.
EDCS-14500979.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Rustius Iunianus, a beneficarius of the Second Italian Legion, for his and his family's well-being, dated to 174 AD.

EDCS-14500980.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Faustiniano vectigalis Illyrici vilico Ingenuus filius et Felix contrascriptor ex vikario sic eius bene merenti pro pietate\n
EDCS-14500980.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Faustinianus of the Illyrian tax collection, a vilicus, Ingenuus, his son, and Felix, his assistant (contrascriptor) and former deputy (ex vikario), [made this] for him, who deserved well of them for his dutifulness.
EDCS-14500980.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Faustinianus, a vilicus in the Illyrian tax collection, erected by his son and assistant.

EDCS-14500983.text_edition: \n Caius Duronius Martialis vivus fecit sibi et Proculeiae Proclae coniugi annorum XXX et Caio Duronio Ursino filio annorum XXX\n
EDCS-14500983.translation: Caius Duronius Martialis, while alive, made this for himself and for his wife Proculeia Procla, aged 30, and for his son Caius Duronius Ursus, aged 30.
EDCS-14500983.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Caius Duronius Martialis for himself, his wife, and his son.

EDCS-14500988.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II pater patriae consul pro consule et Marcus Opellius Antoninus Diadumenianus nobilissimus Caesar princeps iuventutis providentissimi Auggusti fecerunt ab Agunto milia passuum LVI\n
EDCS-14500988.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the second time, father of the fatherland, consul, proconsul, and Marcus Opellius Antoninus Diadumenianus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, most provident son of Augustus, [ordered] the construction of this road from Aguntum, 56 Roman miles.
EDCS-14500988.summary: This is a milestone marking a road built on the orders of Emperors Macrinus and his son Diadumenianus, dating to their joint rule (217-218 AD).

EDCS-14500989.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus I\n
EDCS-14500989.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus [erected this].
EDCS-14500989.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription honoring Emperor Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, likely part of a public monument.

EDCS-14500993.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Augustus, likely from a public building or monument, possibly from the 1st century BC or AD.

EDCS-14500994.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex sic tribunicia potestate VIIII imperator XII consul II pater patriae pro consule et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius <Antoninus> Pius Augustus tribunicia potestate IIII pro consule a Teurnia milia passuum IIXXX\n
EDCS-14500994.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 9th time, imperator for the 12th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul; and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, proconsul; [the road is] 38 miles from Teurnia.
EDCS-14500994.summary: This inscription records a road construction or repair project, specifying the distance from Teurnia and likely dating to the period when both Septimius Severus and Caracalla held power, around 202-205 AD.

EDCS-14500997.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate consul a Teurnia milia passuum XLI\n
EDCS-14500997.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, consul; [the road is] 41 miles from Teurnia.
EDCS-14500997.summary: This inscription records a road of 41 miles from Teurnia, dedicated to Emperor Philip the Arab, likely dating to his reign between 244 and 249 AD.

EDCS-14500998.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Marco Iulio Philippo nobilissimo Caesari Pio Felici Invicto Augusto\n
EDCS-14500998.translation: To our lord Marcus Iulius Philippus, most noble Caesar, Pius Felix Invictus Augustus.
EDCS-14500998.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Iulius Philippus (Philip the Arab) as Caesar and later as Augustus, likely from a monument or building, dating to his co-regency with his son or his reign, around 244-247 AD.

EDCS-14500999.text_edition: \n Inperator sic Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex sic tribunicia potestate VIIII inperator sic XII consul II proconsul et Inperator sic Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus tribunicia potestate IX pater patriae proconsul XLII\n
EDCS-14500999.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 9th time, imperator for the 12th time, consul for the 2nd time, proconsul; and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, holding tribunician power for the 9th time, father of the fatherland, proconsul [the road is] 42 miles.
EDCS-14500999.summary: This inscription indicates a road project of 42 miles, featuring the titles of Septimius Severus and Caracalla, likely from the period when both were in power, around 211 AD.

EDCS-14501001.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus tribunicia potestate IX imperator XII consul II proconsul et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus tribunicia potestate IIII proconsul a Teurnia milia passuum LIIII\n
EDCS-14501001.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 9th time, imperator for the 12th time, consul for the 2nd time, proconsul; and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, proconsul; [the road is] 54 miles from Teurnia.
EDCS-14501001.summary: This inscription details a road of 54 miles from Teurnia, listing the imperial titles of Septimius Severus and Caracalla, likely dating to 205 AD.

EDCS-14501007.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestati sic II pater patriae consul proconsul et Marcus Opellius Diadumenianus nobilissimus Caesar princeps iuventutis Auggusti fecerunt a Viruni sic milia passuum XV\n
EDCS-14501007.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, consul, proconsul; and Marcus Opellius Diadumenianus, most noble Caesar, princeps iuventutis, son of Augustus, made [this road]; 15 miles from Virunum.
EDCS-14501007.summary: This inscription records a road of 15 miles from Virunum, commissioned by Emperor Macrinus and his son Diadumenianus, dating to their brief reign in 218 AD.

EDCS-14501009.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate pater patriae proconsul a Viruno milia passuum XV\n
EDCS-14501009.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, father of the fatherland, proconsul; [the road is] 15 miles from Virunum.
EDCS-14501009.summary: This inscription documents a road of 15 miles from Virunum, dedicated to Emperor Philip the Arab, likely dating to his reign between 244 and 249 AD.

EDCS-14501010.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Flavio Valerio Constantino Pio Felici Invicto Augusto V milia passuum XXXII\n
EDCS-14501010.translation: To our lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus, Pius Felix Invictus Augustus; 32 miles.
EDCS-14501010.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Constantine the Great, likely marking a road of 32 miles, dating to his reign, possibly between 307 and 324 AD.

EDCS-14501011.text_edition: \n Imperator Nerva Traianus Caesar Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate pater patriae consul IIII VI\n
EDCS-14501011.translation: Emperor Nerva Traianus Caesar Augustus Germanicus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul for the 4th time; 6 [miles?].
EDCS-14501011.summary: This inscription records the titles of Emperor Trajan, including his victory in Germania and his fourth consulship, possibly marking a distance of 6 miles, dating to around 103-107 AD.

EDCS-14501012.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVI consul III pater patriae proconsul VI\n
EDCS-14501012.translation: Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus, son of the divine Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the divine Nerva, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 16th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul for the 6th time.
EDCS-14501012.summary: This is an imperial titulature inscription for Emperor Hadrian, likely dating to 113 AD.

EDCS-14501013.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pater patriae pontifex maximus tribuniciae potes<t>atis imperator II consul III VI\n
EDCS-14501013.translation: Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 2nd time, emperor for the 2nd time, consul for the 3rd time.
EDCS-14501013.summary: This is an imperial titulature inscription for Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely dating to 139-140 AD.

EDCS-14501014.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIII imperator XII pater patriae consul proconsul et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus maximus Britannicus maximus Germanicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVII imperator III consul IIII pater patriae proconsul miliaria vetustate conlabsa sic restitui iusserunt VI\n
EDCS-14501014.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 8th time, emperor for the 12th time, father of the fatherland, consul, proconsul, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus Germanicus Maximus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 17th time, emperor for the 3rd time, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, ordered these anciently collapsed milestones to be restored.
EDCS-14501014.summary: This inscription records the restoration of milestones by Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, likely dating to 204 AD.

EDCS-14501015.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II pater patriae consul proconsul et Marcus Opellius Antoninus Diadumenianus nobilissimus Caesar princeps iuventutis providentissimi Augusti fecerunt VI S C LX IIII S\n
EDCS-14501015.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, consul, proconsul, and Marcus Opellius Antoninus Diadumenianus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, most provident son of Augustus, made these.
EDCS-14501015.summary: This inscription records the creation of something by Emperors Macrinus and his son Diadumenianus, likely dating to 217-218 AD.

EDCS-14501017.text_edition: \n Imp (erator) Nerva Traianus Caesar Augustus Germanicus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate pater patriae consul IIII VIII\n
EDCS-14501017.translation: Emperor Caesar Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul for the 4th time.
EDCS-14501017.summary: This is an imperial titulature inscription for Emperor Trajan, likely dating to 102 AD.

EDCS-14501018.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Cornelio Val(eriano) nobilissimo Caesari Domino nostro Flavio Claudio Constantino Pio Felici Augusto bono rei publicae nato\n
EDCS-14501018.translation: To our lord Cornelius Valerianus, most noble Caesar, our lord Flavius Claudius Constantinus, pious, fortunate Augustus, born for the good of the state.
EDCS-14501018.summary: This is a dedication to the emperors Valerian and Gallienus and the Caesar Constantius Chlorus, likely dating to the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-14501021.text_edition: \n Imp (erator) Caesar Gaius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus pontifex maximus pater patriae tribunicia potestate consul pro consule imperator bis et Gaius Iulius Verus Maximus \n
EDCS-14501021.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, father of the fatherland, holder of tribunician power, consul, proconsul, emperor for the second time, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus.
EDCS-14501021.summary: This is an imperial titulature inscription for Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Caesar Maximinus, likely dating to 236 AD.

EDCS-14501022.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pater patriae pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate imperator II consul III XII\n
EDCS-14501022.translation: Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 2nd time, emperor for the 2nd time, consul for the 3rd time.
EDCS-14501022.summary: This is an imperial titulature inscription for Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely dating to 139-140 AD.

EDCS-14501024.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico maximo Britannico maximo pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate III imperatori VII consuli II patri patriae proconsuli et Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Invicto Augusto Parthico maximo Britannico maximo Germanico maximo pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XVI imperatori III consuli IIII patri patriae proconsuli fortissimo ac felicissimo principi domino indulgentissimo milia passuum XI\n
EDCS-14501024.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 3rd time, emperor for the 7th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, and to Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Invictus Augustus Parthicus Maximus Britannicus Maximus Germanicus Maximus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 16th time, emperor for the 3rd time, consul for the 4th time, father of the fatherland, proconsul, the most courageous and most fortunate prince, the most indulgent lord, 11 miles.
EDCS-14501024.summary: This inscription records the distance of 11 miles restored by Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, likely dating to 208 AD.

EDCS-14501025.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Parthicus maximus ponti<f=E>(ex) maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII imperator XII consul II proconsul et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurellius sic Antoninus Pius Augustus tribunicia potestate IIII proconsul et pius Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar miliaria vetustate conlapsa restituerunt curante Marco Iuventio Suro Proculo legato pro praetore ab Iuvavo milia passuum XXXI \n
EDCS-14501025.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Parthicus Maximus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 9th time, emperor for the 12th time, consul for the 2nd time, proconsul, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, holder of tribunician power for the 4th time, proconsul, and pious Lucius Septimius Geta Caesar restored these milestones, collapsed from ancient age, under the supervision of Marcus Iuventius Surus Proculus, legate pro praetore, 31 miles from Iuvavum.
EDCS-14501025.summary: This inscription records the restoration of 31 miles of milestones by Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, likely dating to 203 AD.

EDCS-14501030.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus maximus Britannicus maximus tribunicia potestate XV imperator III consul designatus IIII viam iuxta amnem Danuvium fieri iussit a Boiioduru sic in SALOATO milia passuum XV\n
EDCS-14501030.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, the greatest conqueror of Parthia, the greatest conqueror of Britain, holding tribunician power for the 15th time, acclaimed imperator for the 3rd time, designated consul for the 4th time, ordered a road to be made next to the Danube River from Boiioduru for 15 miles in the direction of Saloato.
EDCS-14501030.summary: This inscription records an imperial order by Marcus Aurelius to construct a road along the Danube River, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-14501035.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Masunno Antonia Nicae sic marito optimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-14501035.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Antonia Nicae had this made for her most excellent husband, Iulius Masunnus.
EDCS-14501035.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to Iulius Masunnus by his wife Antonia Nicae, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-14501036.text_edition: \n Lotucus Cirponis filius vivus fecit sibi et Medsiae coniugi et Spectato Cuc <f=E>(ilio) et Surae coniugi eius vivi fecerunt \n
EDCS-14501036.translation: Lotucus, son of Cirpo, made this while alive for himself and for his wife Medsia and for Spectatus, son of Cuc, and for his wife Sura; they made these while alive.
EDCS-14501036.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Lotucus and his wife for themselves and their family members, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-14501041.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Octaviae Successi nnorum XXV Ingenuus fecit\n
EDCS-14501041.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Ingenuus made this for Octavia Successa, aged 25.
EDCS-14501041.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Octavia Successa, erected by Ingenuus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-14501073.text_edition: \n iusserunt patELV non fore illi nunc matris pietaten sic habet per dominica rura et licuit matri corpus sepe<li>re dolendum quae nunc habet titulum non terrae inimicae iacentem set sic potius donis sollemnibus esse colendum Surilla ego som sic quae peremor sic filia Chloes\n
EDCS-14501073.translation: They ordered that it should not be for her now, for she has her mother's devotion. It was permitted for the mother to bury the body often, which is to be lamented, but now she has a monument not of hostile earth lying there, but rather to be honored with solemn gifts. I, Surilla, daughter of Chloe, thus perish.
EDCS-14501073.summary: This funerary inscription, possibly a poetic epitaph, expresses grief and honors Surilla, daughter of Chloe, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-14501075.text_edition: \n Bruto Gintussae filio annorum LXXXXV fecit sibi et Meleiae Bricosticis filiae uxori et Ressico filio et Arioni filio annorum XXX et Vetuo filio et Vaenoni filiae annorum XXX et Annico Ve filio annorum XXV\n
EDCS-14501075.translation: Brutus, son of Gintussa, aged 95, made this for himself and for Meleia Bricostis, his daughter, his wife, and for Ressicus, his son, and for Arionius, son of Arionius, aged 30, and for Vetuus, his son, and for Vaenonia, his daughter, aged 30, and for Annicus, son of Ve, aged 25.
EDCS-14501075.summary: This funerary monument records the ages and relationships of multiple family members for whom it was erected, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-14501076.text_edition: \n Celatus Tertulli annorum C vibus sic fecit sibi et suis Successa Successi annorum CI--\n
EDCS-14501076.translation: Celatus, aged 100, son of Tertullus, made this for himself and for his family; Successa, daughter of Successus, aged 101...
EDCS-14501076.summary: This funerary epitaph records the long lives of Celatus and Successa, and was made for themselves and their families, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-14501077.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Tapurio Vestali et Mercusenae coniugi eius Lucius Tapurius filius X legionis tribunus parentibus pientissimis\n
EDCS-14501077.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Tapurius, son of Lucius Tapurius, tribune of the 10th legion, for his most dutiful parents, Lucius Tapurius Vestalis and his wife Mercusena.
EDCS-14501077.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by a military officer to his parents, Lucius Tapurius Vestalis and Mercusena, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-14600001.text_edition: \n Iunius Sabini et Ursina Motti Sabinus Iuni filius fecit\n
EDCS-14600001.translation: Iunius Sabinus and Ursina, wife of Motti, made this; Sabinus, son of Iunius, made this.
EDCS-14600001.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by Iunius Sabinus and Ursina, with Sabinus, son of Iunius, identified as the maker, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-14600003.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Secunda Missici viva fecit Lottio Camulio filio defuncto annorum XXIII\n
EDCS-14600003.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Secunda, alive, made this for her son, Lottius Camulius, deceased, aged 23.
EDCS-14600003.summary: This funerary epitaph records Secunda dedicating a monument to her deceased son, Lottius Camulius, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-14600004.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Acceptiano Accepti filio ob voluntatem testamenti Secundus <A=V>cc<e=I>pti fratri piissimo \n
EDCS-14600004.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Secundus Acciptus, in fulfillment of his will, [erected this] for his most devoted brother Acceptianus, son of Acceptus.
EDCS-14600004.summary: A funerary dedication for Acceptianus, set up by his brother Secundus Acciptus according to the terms of a will.

EDCS-14600006.text_edition: \n Botucae Magiovindi filiae mater viva fecit\n
EDCS-14600006.translation: Her mother, while alive, erected [this] for Botucae, daughter of Magiovindus.
EDCS-14600006.summary: A funerary monument erected by a mother for her daughter, Botucae.

EDCS-14600007.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Capitonio Atticio Titia Atimeria fratri karissimo\n
EDCS-14600007.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Titia Atimeria [erected this] for her dearest brother Capitonio Atticio.
EDCS-14600007.summary: A funerary dedication for Capitonio Atticio, set up by his sister Titia Atimeria.

EDCS-14600008.text_edition: \n co Corbi filio et unae Galli filiae uxori vivae filii vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-14600008.translation: Sons, while alive, erected [this] for their father Corbius, son of Corbius, and for their mother Una, daughter of Gallus.
EDCS-14600008.summary: A funerary monument erected by living sons for their deceased parents.

EDCS-14600009.text_edition: \n Cupitus Quinti filius patri fecit et Maturo avunculo\n
EDCS-14600009.translation: Cupitus, son of Quintus, erected [this] for his father and for his uncle Maturo.
EDCS-14600009.summary: A dedication by Cupitus for his father and his uncle Maturo.

EDCS-14600010.text_edition: \n Domesticus et Crescentina servi Cassio patrono\n
EDCS-14600010.translation: Domesticus and Crescentina, slaves, [erected this] for their patron Cassius.
EDCS-14600010.summary: A dedication by two slaves, Domesticus and Crescentina, for their patron Cassius.

EDCS-14600011.text_edition: \n Caius Fabius Advena Fabia Cai liberta Buxa vivi fecerunt sibi et suis\n
EDCS-14600011.translation: Caius Fabius Advena and Fabia Buxa, a freedwoman of Caius, erected [this] for themselves and their families, while alive.
EDCS-14600011.summary: A funerary monument erected by a couple, Caius Fabius Advena and Fabia Buxa, for themselves and their families.

EDCS-14600012.text_edition: \n Ollodevu sic Successius Florianus et Summianius Maximus \n
EDCS-14600012.translation: Ollodevu. Thus Successius Florianus and Summianius Maximus [did the same].
EDCS-14600012.summary: A dedication by Successius Florianus and Summianius Maximus, potentially recording a funerary monument or a shared undertaking.

EDCS-14600014.text_edition: \n Iunio Primi filio et Obiliae Attonis filiae coniugi\n
EDCS-14600014.translation: [Erected by] his wife Obilia, daughter of Atto, for Junius Primus, son of Primus, and for his wife.
EDCS-14600014.summary: A funerary monument erected by Obilia for her husband Junius Primi and possibly others.

EDCS-14600015.translation: Iusta, his sister, [erected this] for Latinus, son of Attunus.
EDCS-14600015.summary: A funerary dedication by Iusta for her brother Latinus.

EDCS-14600016.text_edition: \n Mivilae Adiatulli Secundus Arronis filius uxori vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14600016.translation: Secundus, son of Arronis, made this for his wife Mivila while he was alive.
EDCS-14600016.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Secundus for his wife Mivila, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600017.text_edition: \n Sextus Secundini et Iunia Firmi vivi fecerunt sibi et Mogetio filio annorum IX et Sextiano filio annorum VII\n
EDCS-14600017.translation: Sextus and Iunia, husband and wife, made this for themselves and for their son Mogetius, aged nine, and for their son Sextianus, aged seven.
EDCS-14600017.summary: This funerary inscription records a self-funded tomb for Sextus, Iunia, and their two young sons, Mogetius and Sextianus, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600018.text_edition: \n Publius Veturius Publi libertus Amphio sibei sic et sueis sic fieri iussit arbitratu Publi Veturi Publi liberti Amphioravi fratris\n
EDCS-14600018.translation: Publius Veturius Amphio, freedman of Publius, ordered this to be made for himself and for his family, under the supervision of Publius Veturius Amphioravus, his brother, a freedman of Publius.
EDCS-14600018.summary: This inscription details the arrangements made by Publius Veturius Amphio, a freedman, for his own burial and that of his family, overseen by his brother, under the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600020.translation: SLCVS, son of Deiivora cionis, while alive...
EDCS-14600020.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, possibly funerary, mentioning SLCVS and his father, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600022.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, likely part of a name and patronymic or familial relationship, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600023.text_edition: \n Surus Aetae filiae et Quartae Bovieri filiae uxsori sic et sibi et Secundo filio annorum XX\n
EDCS-14600023.translation: Surus made this for his daughter Aetae and for Quarta Bovieri, his wife, and for himself and for his son Secundus, aged twenty.
EDCS-14600023.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Surus for his daughter, wife (Quarta, daughter of Bovier), and son Secundus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600024.text_edition: \n Adiutorino Adiutoris et Prigmeniae Barbiani filiae\n
EDCS-14600024.translation: For Adiutorinus, son of Adiutoris and Prigmenia, daughter of Barbiana.
EDCS-14600024.summary: This inscription is likely a dedication or a funerary marker for Adiutorinus, son of Adiutoris and Prigmenia, daughter of Barbiana, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600026.text_edition: \n Adnamus Terti filius et Devignata Tituconis filia Adnamatus Adnami et Siora Adnami filia\n
EDCS-14600026.translation: Adnamus, son of Tertius, and Devignata, daughter of Tituco; Adnamatus, son of Adnamus, and Siora, daughter of Adnamus.
EDCS-14600026.summary: This inscription appears to list four individuals, likely family members, with their patronymics or matronymics, possibly for a funerary monument or property record from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600027.text_edition: \n vivus fecit Avareae uxori eius pr T un\n
EDCS-14600027.translation: He made this while alive for his wife Avarea pr T un.
EDCS-14600027.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription stating that someone made a monument while alive for his wife Avarea, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600028.text_edition: \n Quartinus Quarti et Messia Crescentis et Saxxuni et Secundino filiis \n
EDCS-14600028.translation: For Quartinus, son of Quarti, and Messia Crescentis, and for Saxxus and Secundinus, his sons.
EDCS-14600028.summary: This inscription is likely a dedication or funerary marker for Quartinus, his wife Messia Crescentis, and their sons Saxxus and Secundinus, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600032.text_edition: \n Flori Cai fili annorum XL Ianuari o optimo fecit et sibi\n
EDCS-14600032.translation: Florus, son of Caius, aged 40, made this for Ianuarius the excellent and for himself.
EDCS-14600032.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Florus, dedicated by an unnamed person to Ianuarius and also for the dedicator.

EDCS-14600038.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording Ulpius Saturninus making arrangements for his own burial while alive.

EDCS-14600043.text_edition: \n Vitalis Hilari filius vivus fecit sibi et Surae Lupponis filiae coniugi\n
EDCS-14600043.translation: Vitalis, son of Hilarius, alive, made this for himself and for Sura, wife of Luppus.
EDCS-14600043.summary: This funerary inscription records Vitalis, son of Hilarius, dedicating a monument for himself and his wife Sura.

EDCS-14600045.text_edition: \n Vitali Festi filio coniugi et Atecto filio annorum XXX Aiiu Macci filia et Priscus Vitalis filius vivi fecerunt et sibi et Hel(viae) Bouniae et Helviae Litunae\n
EDCS-14600045.translation: Aiiu Macci and her son Priscus Vitalis made this for Vitalis Festus, their son and husband, aged 30, and for themselves and for Helvia Bounia and Helvia Lituna.
EDCS-14600045.summary: This funerary inscription records Aiiu Macci and her son Priscus Vitalis dedicating a monument for Vitalis Festus, their son/husband, and for themselves and others.

EDCS-14600046.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio domini nostri Antonini Pii Felicis Augusti Restitutius Tutor beneficiarius consularis legionis II Italicae Antoninianae votum solvit libens merito Laeto II et Ceriaile sic consulibus\n
EDCS-14600046.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to the Genius of our lord Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, Restitutius, a beneficiary official of the consular legate of the II Italica Antoniniana legion, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow, Laetus II and Ceriaile being consuls.
EDCS-14600046.summary: This is a votive inscription by Restitutius, an official of the II Italica legion, to Jupiter and the Emperor Antoninus Pius, dating to the consulship of Laetus II and Ceriaile.

EDCS-14600047.text_edition: \n Aesonius Crescens votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14600047.translation: Aesonius Crescens willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-14600047.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aesonius Crescens who has fulfilled a vow.

EDCS-14600048.text_edition: \n Locus sepulturae cultorum Genii municipii Aguntensium Secundus Antoni Pudentis titulum sumptu suo memoriae causa donum dedit IVC\n
EDCS-14600048.translation: A burial place for the worshippers of the Genius of the municipality of Aguntum. Secundus, son of Antonius Pudentis, gave this monument at his own expense as a gift for remembrance.
EDCS-14600048.summary: This is a dedication of a burial plot for the worshippers of the Genius of Aguntum, donated by Secundus at his own expense.

EDCS-14600049.text_edition: \n Titus Pollius Severus Titus Pollius Severus\n
EDCS-14600049.translation: Titus Pollius Severus [dedicates this]. Titus Pollius Severus.
EDCS-14600049.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragment or a very simple dedication by Titus Pollius Severus.

EDCS-14600051.text_edition: \n Lucius Herennius Luci libertus Oclatius sibi et Veneriae uxori optimae vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14600051.translation: Lucius Herennius Oclatius, freedman of Lucius, made this for himself and for Veneria, his excellent wife, while alive.
EDCS-14600051.summary: This funerary inscription records Lucius Herennius Oclatius, a freedman, dedicating a monument for himself and his wife Veneria while alive.

EDCS-14600052.summary: This funerary inscription records Saturnina dedicating a monument for her husband Secundus.

EDCS-14600053.text_edition: \n Lucio Seccio Summo |(obito) et Secciae Cupitae coniugi eius parentibus optimis Lucius Seccius Summus filius vivus fecit et sibi et Iuniae Fuscae coniugi optimae hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-14600053.translation: Lucius Seccius Summus made this monument for his parents, Lucius Seccius Summus (deceased) and Seccia Cupita, his wife, his very best parents, and for himself and Junia Fusca, his very best wife; he will not be followed by an heir.
EDCS-14600053.summary: This funerary inscription records a self-purchased tomb for Lucius Seccius Summus, his wife Junia Fusca, and his parents Lucius Seccius Summus and Seccia Cupita, who are also honored.

EDCS-14600054.text_edition: \n Antoniae Iuli Prisci ancillae nnorum XII Cloutius pater vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14600054.translation: Cloutius made this for his daughter Antonia, slave of Julius Priscus, aged 12.
EDCS-14600054.summary: This funerary inscription records a tomb made by Cloutius for his 12-year-old daughter, Antonia, a slave of Julius Priscus.

EDCS-14600059.text_edition: \n Bacacu Atuni filia viva fecit sibi et Arimano filio Arioni filio et Cotuni Messici filio coniugi carissimo vivo\n
EDCS-14600059.translation: Bacacu, daughter of Atunus, made this for herself, for her son Arimanus, for her son Arion, and for Cotunus Messicus, her most beloved husband, alive.
EDCS-14600059.summary: This funerary inscription records a tomb made by Bacacu for herself, her sons Arimanus and Arion, and her husband Cotunus Messicus.

EDCS-14600060.text_edition: \n Asclepio Augusto sacrum Volusius Ursus votum solvit merito\n
EDCS-14600060.translation: Volusius Ursus willingly paid his vow to Asclepius Augustus, as was deserved.
EDCS-14600060.summary: This votive inscription records Volusius Ursus fulfilling a vow to the god Asclepius.

EDCS-14600061.text_edition: \n Cautopati Gavillius Respectus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14600061.translation: Gavillius Respectus willingly paid his vow to Cautopates, as was deserved.
EDCS-14600061.summary: This votive inscription records Gavillius Respectus willingly fulfilling a vow to the god Cautopates.

EDCS-14600062.text_edition: \n Genio pro salute Successini et Proximinae eius Primitivus libertus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14600062.translation: Primitivus, the freedman, willingly paid his vow to the Genius for the well-being of Successinus and his wife Proximina, as was deserved.
EDCS-14600062.summary: This votive inscription records Primitivus, a freedman, fulfilling a vow to the household gods (Genius) for the health of Successinus and Proximina.

EDCS-14600063.text_edition: \n Genio domestico Augusto sacrum Tertinius Firminus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14600063.translation: Tertinius Firminus willingly paid his vow to the Divine Household Genius, as was deserved.
EDCS-14600063.summary: This votive inscription records Tertinius Firminus fulfilling a vow to the household gods (Genius).

EDCS-14600065.text_edition: \n Herculi Augusto sacrum Quartus Burri filius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14600065.translation: Quartus, son of Burrus, willingly paid his vow to Hercules Augustus, as was deserved.
EDCS-14600065.summary: This votive inscription records Quartus, son of Burrus, fulfilling a vow to the god Hercules.

EDCS-14600072.summary: This inscription fragment likely records the name and title of Petronius Urbicus, an imperial procurator.

EDCS-14600073.text_edition: \n Caius Cassius Maximus veteranus cohortis VI praetoriae sibi et Messiae Belatussae uxori testamento fieri iussit\n
EDCS-14600073.translation: Gaius Cassius Maximus, a veteran of the VI Praetorian cohort, ordered this to be made by his will for himself and for Messia Belatussa, his wife.
EDCS-14600073.summary: This funerary inscription records a tomb commissioned by will for Gaius Cassius Maximus, a veteran of the Praetorian Guard, and his wife Messia Belatussa.

EDCS-14600074.text_edition: \n Firmino militi legionis II Italicae piae fidelis nnorum XXV mater et soror pientissimo \n
EDCS-14600074.translation: To Firminus, soldier of the Second Italian Legion, pious and faithful, aged 25. His most devoted mother and sister [erected this].
EDCS-14600074.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a soldier of the Second Italian Legion, set up by his mother and sister.

EDCS-14600075.text_edition: \n Tiberius Iulius Giamilli filius Sextus miles cohortis Montanorum stipendiorum XL hic situs est et Ingenuae filiae vixit Iulia Sexti liberta A\n
EDCS-14600075.translation: Tiberius Iulius Sextus, son of Giamillus, a soldier of the Cohors Montanorum with 40 years of service, lies here, and for Ingenua, daughter [of Iulia, Sextus' freedwoman].
EDCS-14600075.summary: This epitaph commemorates Tiberius Iulius Sextus, a soldier with 40 years of service in the Cohors Montanorum, and also mentions Ingenua.

EDCS-14600078.summary: This is a brief epitaph for a child named Albinus, son of Secundus, who died at the age of 10.

EDCS-14600079.text_edition: \n Atara Noeibionis filius Bulluni uxori et sibi et suis vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14600079.translation: Atara, son of Noeibion, made this for his wife Bullunia, for himself, and for his descendants, while alive.
EDCS-14600079.summary: This is a funerary monument self-funded by Atara, son of Noeibion, for himself, his wife Bullunia, and their family.

EDCS-14600080.text_edition: \n Atuco Mattonis filio Ateia liberta viva fecit sibi et suis\n
EDCS-14600080.translation: Ateia, a freedwoman, made this for her son Atucus, son of Matto, for herself and her descendants, while alive.
EDCS-14600080.summary: This funerary monument was self-funded by Ateia, a freedwoman, for her son Atucus and their family.

EDCS-14600081.text_edition: \n Caius Baebius Adiectus vivus fecit sibi et Iuliae Melissae coniugi carissimae et pientissimae\n
EDCS-14600081.translation: Gaius Baebius Adiectus made this for himself and for his dearest and most devoted wife, Iulia Melissa, while alive.
EDCS-14600081.summary: This is a self-funded funerary monument established by Gaius Baebius Adiectus for himself and his wife, Iulia Melissa.

EDCS-14600082.text_edition: \n Publius Barbius Publi filius Niger et mater sua\n
EDCS-14600082.translation: Publius Barbius Niger, son of Publius, and his mother [erected this].
EDCS-14600082.summary: This epitaph records Publius Barbius Niger, son of Publius, and his mother, likely indicating a family grave or memorial.

EDCS-14600083.text_edition: \n Publius Barbius Spuri filius Primus Barbia Publi filia Rustica Barbia Publi filia Casta Quintus Barbius Publi filius Mercator uxor et filii sibi et suis vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-14600083.translation: Publius Barbius Primus, son of Spurius, Barbia Rustica, daughter of Publius, Barbia Casta, daughter of Publius, and Quintus Barbius Mercator, son of Publius, and his wife and sons, made this for themselves and their descendants, while alive.
EDCS-14600083.summary: This is a self-funded funerary monument for multiple family members, including Publius Barbius Primus, his sisters Barbia Rustica and Barbia Casta, and Quintus Barbius Mercator along with his family.

EDCS-14600084.text_edition: \n Publius Barbius Spuri filius Cirro Barbia Publi liberta Amma mater viva Publius Barbius Publi libertus Amphio Publius Barbius Spuri filius Boio Publius Barbius Spuri filius Rusticus fratres de suo fecerunt vivi\n
EDCS-14600084.translation: Publius Barbius, son of Spurius, and his freedwoman Cirro, his mother Amma, freedwoman of Publius, his freedman Amphio, son of Publius, and his brothers Boio, son of Spurius, and Rusticus, son of Spurius, made this at their own expense, while alive.
EDCS-14600084.summary: This funerary monument records several members of the Barbius family, including Publius Barbius, his freed people, and his brothers, who funded it together.

EDCS-14600085.text_edition: \n Publio Barbio Spuri filio Rustico Publius Barbius Publi filius Proculus vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14600085.translation: Publius Barbius Proculus, son of Publius, made this for Publius Barbius Rusticus, son of Spurius, while alive.
EDCS-14600085.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Publius Barbius Proculus for Publius Barbius Rusticus.

EDCS-14600086.text_edition: \n Barbiae Malusiae Publius Barbius Publi filius Firmus matri et sibi et suis vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14600086.translation: Publius Barbius Firmus, son of Publius, of the Barbii Malusii tribe, made this [monument] while alive for his mother, himself, and his own.
EDCS-14600086.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Publius Barbius Firmus for his mother, himself, and his family.

EDCS-14600087.text_edition: \n vivus fecit sibi et Caio Bottio Mercatori et Caio Bottio Adiutori annorum XXIIII et Bottio annorum VII Bottio Paulino\n
EDCS-14600087.translation: He made this while alive for himself, for Gaius Bottius Mercator, for Gaius Bottius Adiuutor, aged 24, and for Bottius, aged 7, and Bottius Paulinus.
EDCS-14600087.summary: This inscription records a self-funded funerary monument dedicated by an unnamed individual for himself and several other men named Bottius, including two children.

EDCS-14600088.text_edition: \n Catroni Allonis filio et Singoriae Busturi filiae filii fecerunt\n
EDCS-14600088.translation: The sons made this for Catronius Allonius and Singoria Busturis.
EDCS-14600088.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by sons for their parents, Catronius Allonius and Singoria Busturis.

EDCS-14600089.text_edition: \n Ceudo Ariomani filius Caesiae sorori et Suavi nepoti de suo vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14600089.translation: Ceudo Ariomani's son made this from his own property while alive for his sister Caesius and his granddaughter Suavia.
EDCS-14600089.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Ceudo Ariomani for his sister Caesius and granddaughter Suavia.

EDCS-14600090.text_edition: \n Chiloni Magio parens sibi et suis vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14600090.translation: Chilo Magius made this while alive for himself and his own.
EDCS-14600090.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Chilo Magius for himself and his family.

EDCS-14600091.text_edition: \n Consagioni Saturionis filio et Uppiae Adiatulli filiae filii vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-14600091.translation: The sons made this while alive for Consagius Saturionis and Uppia Adiatulla.
EDCS-14600091.summary: This funerary monument was erected by sons for their parents, Consagius Saturionis and Uppia Adiatulla.

EDCS-14600092.text_edition: \n Dumiae Ateponis filiae et Adnamae Anectionis filiae coniugi et sibi vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14600092.translation: He made this while alive for Dumia Ateponis, for his wife Adnama Anectionis, and for himself.
EDCS-14600092.summary: This funerary monument was erected by an unnamed man for his wife Dumia Ateponis, his wife Adnama Anectionis, and himself.

EDCS-14600093.text_edition: \n Quinto Fabio Cai filio Tertio Caius Fabius Cai libertus Zeno parens sibi et sueis sic et nepotibus vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14600093.translation: Caius Fabius Zeno, freedman of Caius Fabius Tertius, son of Caius, made this while alive for himself, his own, and his grandsons.
EDCS-14600093.summary: This funerary monument was erected by the freedman Caius Fabius Zeno for himself, his family, and his grandsons.

EDCS-14600094.text_edition: \n Caio Fabricio Cai liberto Primo Pomponia Luci filia Matta uxor viva fecit et filii filiae parenti Caio Fabricio Covincio\n
EDCS-14600094.translation: The wife Pomponia Matta, daughter of Lucius, and the sons and daughters made this while alive for their parent Gaius Fabricius Primus, freedman of Gaius.
EDCS-14600094.summary: This funerary monument was erected by the wife Pomponia Matta and their children for their parent Gaius Fabricius Primus.

EDCS-14600096.text_edition: \n Itria Achillis filia Tertio Viredonis filio coniugi suo viva fecit sibi et suis\n
EDCS-14600096.translation: Itria, daughter of Achilles, made this while alive for her husband Tertius Viredonis and for her own.
EDCS-14600096.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Itria, wife of Tertius Viredonis, for her husband and her family.

EDCS-14600097.text_edition: \n Tito Kanio Titi liberto Philocrati Banona coniunx de suo fecit\n
EDCS-14600097.translation: Banona, his wife, made this at her own expense for Tito Philocrates, freedman of Tito.
EDCS-14600097.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by a wife for her deceased husband, Tito Philocrates, a freedman, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-14600098.text_edition: \n Loco Congoneti et Maesiae Parti filiae coniugi suae vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14600098.translation: He made this while alive for Congonetus and his daughter Maesia Parta, his wife.
EDCS-14600098.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by a man (name lost) during his lifetime for his wife, Maesia Parta, and daughter, Congonetus, possibly dating to the late Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-14600099.text_edition: \n Magiomarus Dononis filius vivus fecit sibi et Buciae Sacronis filiae uxori vivae et Secundo filio\n
EDCS-14600099.translation: Magiomarus, son of Donon, made this while alive for himself, and for Bucia Sacronis, his wife, and for their son Secundus.
EDCS-14600099.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Magiomarus for himself, his wife Bucia, and his son Secundus, indicating a date in the early Imperial period.

EDCS-14600100.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marius Euhodius et Iulia Vegeta Iuliae Euhodiae filiae @(obitae) annorum XVI et Iulio Venantio filio @(obito) annorum X parentes filiis karissimis fecerunt\n
EDCS-14600100.translation: Marius Euhodius and Iulia Vegeta made this for their dearest children, Iulia Euhodia, who died at the age of 16, and Iulius Venantius, who died at the age of 10.
EDCS-14600100.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by parents for their two children, Iulia Euhodia and Iulius Venantius, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-14600101.text_edition: \n Matu Csoni Patronio Urbici filio F vivus fecit sibi et suis\n
EDCS-14600101.translation: F. [son of] Patronius Urbicus, son of Matu Csoni, made this while alive for himself and his family.
EDCS-14600101.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by F. Patronius Urbicus for himself and his relatives, suggesting a date in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600102.text_edition: \n Nigro Marini servo Sura viva fecit sibi et marito optimo\n
EDCS-14600102.translation: Sura made this while alive for Nigrus, freedman of Marinus, and for her excellent husband.
EDCS-14600102.summary: This funerary inscription was set up by Sura for Nigrus, her slave (or freedman), and her husband, dating to the Imperial era.

EDCS-14600103.text_edition: \n Cnaeo Octavio Cnaei liberto Teucro Vetiliai Luci filiae Senecae filii vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-14600103.translation: They made this while alive for Cnaeus Octavius Teucrus, freedman of Cnaeus, son of Vetilia Lucia Seneca.
EDCS-14600103.summary: This funerary monument was erected by unnamed individuals for Cnaeus Octavius Teucrus, a freedman, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600104.text_edition: \n Optatus Nammoni Moderati vivus fecit sibi et Avetae uxori et Maiusae filiae annorum XX\n
EDCS-14600104.translation: Optatus made this while alive for Nammon Moderatus, and for Aveta his wife, and for Maiusa his daughter, aged 20.
EDCS-14600104.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by Optatus for his wife Aveta and daughter Maiusa, as well as for Nammon Moderatus, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-14600105.text_edition: \n Publio Postumio Publi liberto Bromio Postumia Publi liberta Battu patrono et sibi viva fecit et sueis sic\n
EDCS-14600105.translation: Postumia Battu, freedwoman of Publius, made this for her patron Publius Postumius Bromius, freedman of Publius, and for herself while alive and for her family.
EDCS-14600105.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Postumia Battu for her patron and herself, suggesting a social context of freedpersons in the Roman Empire.

EDCS-14600106.text_edition: \n Postumia Publi liberta Adginna Publius Postumius Calonis libertus Donatus sibi et suis vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-14600106.translation: Postumia Adginna, freedwoman of Publius, and Publius Donatus, freedman of Calo, made this for themselves and their families while alive.
EDCS-14600106.summary: This is a joint funerary dedication by two freedpersons, Postumia Adginna and Publius Donatus, for themselves and their families, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600107.text_edition: \n Postumiae Publi libertae Bouni Caius Sempronius Cai libertus Receptus sibi et suis vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14600107.translation: Gaius Sempronius Receptus, freedman of Caius, made this tomb for Postumia Bounis, freedwoman of Publius, and for himself and his family, while alive.
EDCS-14600107.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a freedman for his freedwoman wife and their family.

EDCS-14600108.text_edition: \n Tertius Primigeni et Banona Secundi vivi sibi fecerunt et Primigenio filio karissimo annorum XXV\n
EDCS-14600108.translation: Tertius and Banona Secundi, while alive, made this tomb for themselves and for their dearest son Primigenius, aged 25.
EDCS-14600108.summary: This is a funerary epitaph created by parents for themselves and their deceased son.

EDCS-14600109.text_edition: \n Primigeniae Longinus et Lutetua filiae et Longinae filiae\n
EDCS-14600109.translation: Primigenia, and her daughters Lutetua and Longina.
EDCS-14600109.summary: This is a funerary epitaph commemorating Primigenia and her two daughters.

EDCS-14600110.summary: This is a very brief epitaph for Privatia Vafo Quinta.

EDCS-14600111.text_edition: \n Quarto Lutnaes liberto et Sextae coniugi fili- fec---\n
EDCS-14600111.translation: Quarto, freedman of Lutnaes, and Sexta, his wife, and their son built...
EDCS-14600111.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph recording the builders of a monument for Quarto, his wife Sexta, and their son.

EDCS-14600112.text_edition: \n Quincto Caionis filio Grata uxor Marcio filius Tertius filius Catta filia de suo fecere\n
EDCS-14600112.translation: Grata, his wife, and their son Marcius, and their son Tertius, and their daughter Catta, made this tomb at their own expense for Quinctus, son of Caio.
EDCS-14600112.summary: This funerary epitaph records the construction of a tomb by a wife and her children for her deceased husband.

EDCS-14600113.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Respectianus Respecti Suadro Quartici vivus fecit et sibi\n
EDCS-14600113.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Respectianus made this for himself and for Respecti Suadro Quartici, while alive.
EDCS-14600113.summary: This is a funerary epitaph where Respectianus dedicates a tomb for himself and another individual.

EDCS-14600115.text_edition: \n Satucioni Montaionis filio et Boniae Privati filiae coniugi\n
EDCS-14600115.translation: For Satucio, son of Montaio, and for his wife Bonia, daughter of Privatus.
EDCS-14600115.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicating a tomb to Satucio and his wife Bonia.

EDCS-14600116.text_edition: \n Secundo Cirronis filio filii vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-14600116.translation: His sons made this while alive for Secundus, son of Cirro.
EDCS-14600116.summary: This funerary epitaph records sons setting up a monument for their father, Secundus.

EDCS-14600117.text_edition: \n Somarioni Lottonis filio Castilla Sennonis filia et Corro Somarionis filius vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-14600117.translation: Castilla Sennonis, daughter of Sennon, and Corro, son of Somarion, made this while alive for Somarion, son of Lotton.
EDCS-14600117.summary: This funerary epitaph records Castilla and Corro dedicating a tomb for Somarion.

EDCS-14600118.text_edition: \n Statutae iuveni defunctae annorum XVIII Statutus frater fecit\n
EDCS-14600118.translation: Statutus made this for his sister Statuta, who died at eighteen years of age.
EDCS-14600118.summary: A funerary inscription recording a dedication by a brother for his deceased sister.

EDCS-14600119.translation: Quintus Stlaccius Bassus, son of Lucius.
EDCS-14600119.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name and filiation of Quintus Stlaccius Bassus.

EDCS-14600120.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name of Tattuni, who made the monument during her lifetime.

EDCS-14600121.text_edition: \n Publius Titius Publi libertus Iuvenes sic Publius Postumius Publi libertus Cardacanus Vettidia Marci liberta Flora vivi patrono parenti matri Publius Titius Publi libertus Communis vivos sic fecit de suo\n
EDCS-14600121.translation: Publius Titius Communis, freedman of Publius, and Publius Postumius Cardacanus, freedman of Publius, and Vettidia Flora, freedwoman of Marcus, made this while alive for their living patron, father, and mother, from their own funds.
EDCS-14600121.summary: A funerary inscription recording a joint dedication made by freedmen and freedwomen for their patron and parents.

EDCS-14600122.text_edition: \n Titius Spuri filius Ingenuos hic situs\n
EDCS-14600122.translation: Titius Ingenuos, son of Spurius, lies here.
EDCS-14600122.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name and filiation of Titius Ingenuos.

EDCS-14600123.text_edition: \n Titia Publi liberta Entia Publius Titius Publi libertus Temaio vivi fecerunt sibi et suis\n
EDCS-14600123.translation: Titia Entia, freedwoman of Publius, and Publius Titius Temaio, freedman of Publius, made this while alive for themselves and their families.
EDCS-14600123.summary: A funerary inscription recording a joint dedication made by freedmen for themselves and their relatives.

EDCS-14600124.summary: A funerary inscription recording the name and status of Vetilia Philumina.

EDCS-14600125.translation: Verus, Prefect of the first cohort, dedicated this.
EDCS-14600125.summary: A dedication recording a prefect of the first cohort, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-14600130.summary: A fragmentary inscription beginning with the name Publius Postumius.

EDCS-14600132.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription mentioning parents and a descendant named Postumius.

EDCS-14600134.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the name of the deceased, Sabinus.

EDCS-14600135.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a dedication or funerary marker, mentioning the name Blastio.

EDCS-14600136.text_edition: \n Sacro Cotunis et Quarta Quarti vivi fecerunt sibi et Sacretiae filiae\n
EDCS-14600136.translation: Sacro and Quarta, daughter of Quartus, made this while alive for themselves and for their daughter Sacretia.
EDCS-14600136.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Sacro and Quarta for themselves and their daughter Sacretia.

EDCS-14600137.text_edition: \n FDEEFETIE decurio Iae Titio vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14600137.translation: Iae Titius, a decurion, made this while alive.
EDCS-14600137.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording that Iae Titius, a decurion, built his own tomb.

EDCS-14600139.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Saturionis Fortionis vivus fecit sibi et Vibenae coniugi et Fortioni\n
EDCS-14600139.translation: To the departed spirits. Saturio, son of Fortio, made this while alive for himself, his wife Vibena, and Fortio.
EDCS-14600139.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Saturio for himself, his wife Vibena, and another person named Fortio.

EDCS-14600140.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valentinas sic annorum XXXV Marcellinus fecit compari dulcissimae\n
EDCS-14600140.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcellinus made this for Valentinas, aged 35, his dearest companion.
EDCS-14600140.summary: This is a funerary inscription erected by Marcellinus for his wife Valentinas.

EDCS-14600141.text_edition: \n Victorinus Vebeni vivus fecit sibi et Secundinae coniugi karissimae\n
EDCS-14600141.translation: Victorinus, son of Vebenus, made this while alive for himself and his most beloved wife Secundina.
EDCS-14600141.summary: This is a funerary monument built by Victorinus for himself and his wife Secundina.

EDCS-14600142.text_edition: \n Dis Inferis Manibus Vibenus Quarti et Quintilla Quintiani vivi fecerunt sibi\n
EDCS-14600142.translation: To the gods of the underworld and departed spirits. Vibenus, son of Quartus, and Quintilla, daughter of Quintianus, made this while alive for themselves.
EDCS-14600142.summary: This is a funerary inscription created by Vibenus and Quintilla for their own burials.

EDCS-14600144.text_edition: \n Publius Aelius CE V Receptusonis filius \n
EDCS-14600144.summary: This inscription records the name and parentage of Publius Aelius.

EDCS-14600146.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Saturninae Manneani Optatae veti viva fecit sibi et Optato filio militi l<e=I>gionis II Italicae annorum XXV \n
EDCS-14600146.translation: To the departed spirits. Saturnina, daughter of Manneanus Optatus, made this while alive for herself and for her son Optatus, a soldier of the II Italica Legion, aged 25.
EDCS-14600146.summary: This funerary inscription was made by Saturnina for herself and her son Optatus, a soldier in the II Italica Legion.

EDCS-14600147.text_edition: \n Covnertus sic Cupiti libertus et Venima coniux vivi fecerunt sibi \n
EDCS-14600147.translation: Covnertus, freedman of Cupitus, and his wife Venima made this for themselves while alive.
EDCS-14600147.summary: This funerary epitaph records a dedication made by a freedman and his wife for themselves.

EDCS-14600148.translation: Catta paid her vow to Juno.
EDCS-14600148.summary: This votive inscription records a payment of a vow to the goddess Juno.

EDCS-14600149.text_edition: \n Capito Sirae filius vivus fecit sibi et Mariccae Blendonis filiae uxori et Atedunae filiae\n
EDCS-14600149.translation: Capito, son of Sira, made this for himself while alive, and for his wife Maricca, daughter of Blendon, and for his daughter Ateduna.
EDCS-14600149.summary: This funerary epitaph records a self-dedication and provision for family members.

EDCS-14600150.text_edition: \n ronis filius et Ingenua ontis filia sibi vivi ffecerunt o filio nnorum XXV\n
EDCS-14600150.translation: [...], son of R[...], and Ingenua, daughter of Ontis, made this for themselves alive, and for their son aged 25.
EDCS-14600150.summary: This funerary epitaph records a self-dedication and provision for a deceased son, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-14600152.text_edition: \n Quincto Rudsci filio et Atecinae Sinomari filiae coniugi parentes et filii filiaeque et Viriae Taeti filiae\n
EDCS-14600152.translation: For Quintus, son of Rudscus, and his wife Atecina, daughter of Sinomarus, and for his children, sons and daughters, and for Viria, daughter of Taetus.
EDCS-14600152.summary: This funerary epitaph is a memorial for Quintus, his wife, their children, and another individual named Viria.

EDCS-14600153.text_edition: \n Romani servus Sextilius vivus fecit sibi et Secundinae coniugi karissimae annorum XXXV\n
EDCS-14600153.translation: Romani, freedman of Sextilius, made this for himself while alive, and for his dearest wife Secundina, aged 35.
EDCS-14600153.summary: This funerary epitaph records a self-dedication and provision for a wife by a freedman.

EDCS-14600154.text_edition: \n Cotuni Mocconis filiae Bardo mater et Adiutori fratri et sibi et Banonae obitae annorum II\n
EDCS-14600154.translation: For Cotuni, daughter of Mocconis, Bardo her mother, and for Adiutor her brother, and for herself, and for Banona, deceased at the age of 2.
EDCS-14600154.summary: This funerary epitaph records a memorial for a child, Banona, set up by her mother and brother.

EDCS-14600155.text_edition: \n ILV Vindillae cundi filiae uxori tasi \n
EDCS-14600155.translation: For Vindilla, daughter of Cundus, wife of [---], [---] for herself.
EDCS-14600155.summary: This funerary epitaph is a partial memorial, likely for Vindilla, wife of an unnamed person.

EDCS-14600156.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of a young girl named Instituto.

EDCS-14600157.text_edition: \n Primo Herenni Veri servo Hilaricae uxor\n
EDCS-14600157.translation: For Hilarica, wife of Primo, slave of Herennius Verus.
EDCS-14600157.summary: This funerary epitaph records the wife of a slave.

EDCS-14600158.text_edition: \n Cotulo Patali filius et Vindae Arimani filiae uxori vivus fecit sibi et Iantumaro filio annorum XX\n
EDCS-14600158.translation: Cotulus, son of Patali, and his wife Iantumara, daughter of Arimanus, erected this while alive for himself and for his son Iantumara, aged 20.
EDCS-14600158.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Cotulus for himself, his wife, and his son.

EDCS-14600159.text_edition: \n Sextilius Sexti filius vivus fecit sibi et Terentiae coniugi\n
EDCS-14600159.translation: Sextilius, son of Sextus, erected this while alive for himself and for his wife Terentia.
EDCS-14600159.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Sextilius for himself and his wife.

EDCS-14600162.text_edition: \n Veponiae eriae Atto M fecit et sibi et Quartillae sorori et Tertino et Apollinari nepotibus et Optatae\n
EDCS-14600162.translation: Atto M erected this for Veponia Eria and for himself, his sister Quartilla, his grandsons Tertinus and Apollinaris, and Optata.
EDCS-14600162.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Atto M for his family members.

EDCS-14600165.text_edition: \n Quinto Aurelio Primo et Auruminio Iuliae uxori heres fecit Iulia F F nnrum \n
EDCS-14600165.translation: His heirs erected this for Quintus Aurelius Primus and his wife Iulia Auruminia.
EDCS-14600165.summary: This funerary monument was erected by heirs for a couple.

EDCS-14600166.translation: Primus, son of Celsus, erected this for himself.
EDCS-14600166.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Primus for himself.

EDCS-14600168.text_edition: \n Dianae Augustae pro salute imperatorum Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis et \n
EDCS-14600168.translation: To Diana Augusta for the safety of the emperors Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax and...
