EDCS-14600168.summary: This is a dedication to Diana Augusta for the well-being of the emperors, likely dating to the reign of Septimius Severus.

EDCS-14600169.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Culminali Chresimus Auggustorum \n
EDCS-14600169.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Culminalis, from Chresimus, freedman of the emperors.
EDCS-14600169.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Chresimus, a freedman of the emperors.

EDCS-14600170.text_edition: \n Marti Augusto pro salute Primigeni Auggustorum nnostrorum vilici stationis Atrantinae\n
EDCS-14600170.translation: To Mars Augustus for the safety of Primigenius, our emperor's freedman, supervisor of the Atrantina station.
EDCS-14600170.summary: This is a dedication to Mars Augustus for the safety of a freedman and supervisor of a station.

EDCS-14600172.text_edition: \n us Quintianus beneficiarius consularis legionis II Italicae templum vetustate conlabsum sic et in ruina conversum sumptu suo restituit\n
EDCS-14600172.translation: Quintianus, a beneficiary of the consular legate of the II Italica legion, restored this temple, collapsed by age and ruined, at his own expense.
EDCS-14600172.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a temple by Quintianus, a member of the II Italica legion.

EDCS-14600173.text_edition: \n A Aurelius Asclepiodotus voto soluto donum dedit \n
EDCS-14600173.translation: Aurelius Asclepiodotus gave this gift, having fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-14600173.summary: This is a votive offering made by Aurelius Asclepiodotus.

EDCS-14600174.text_edition: \n OL vixit annos XX eiusdem I karissimo\n
EDCS-14600174.translation: He lived 20 years, to his dearest [---]
EDCS-14600174.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating a person named OL who lived 20 years, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600175.text_edition: \n Adsallutae et Savo Augustis sacrum Caius Cassius Quietus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14600175.translation: To the August deities Adsallutae, Gaius Cassius Quietus gladly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-14600175.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the deities Adsallutae by Gaius Cassius Quietus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600176.translation: To the August goddess Adsalute, Gaius C A [---]
EDCS-14600176.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Adsalute by Gaius C A, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600177.text_edition: \n Quartus Diastulli annorum Titua Quarti annorum XXX \n
EDCS-14600177.translation: Quartus, son of Diastullus, aged [---]; Titua, daughter of Quartus, aged 30 years.
EDCS-14600177.summary: This inscription records the ages of two individuals, Quartus and Titua, likely a funerary epitaph, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600178.translation: Surio made this for the Invincible Mithras.
EDCS-14600178.summary: This is a dedication to the god Mithras by Surio, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600179.text_edition: \n Nymphis Augustis sacrum Appuleius Finiti votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14600179.translation: To the August Nymphs, Appuleius Finiti gladly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-14600179.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the Nymphs by Appuleius Finiti, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600180.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Barbius Finitus miles cohortis I praetoriae ob peregrinationis expeditionum regressus in urbem sacrum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14600180.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Barbius Finitus, a soldier of the first Praetorian cohort, gladly and deservedly paid his vow upon his return to the city from his foreign campaigns.
EDCS-14600180.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Barbius Finitus, a soldier of the Praetorian Guard, upon his return from military campaigns, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600181.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Lottius Felix et Marcia Bonita eius pro se et suis omnibus votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-14600181.translation: Lucius Lottius Felix and Marcia Bonita, his wife, for themselves and all their family, gladly and deservedly paid their vows to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-14600181.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Lucius Lottius Felix and Marcia Bonita on behalf of themselves and their family, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600187.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Victor miles legionis II Italicae bello desideratus hoste Gutica sic annorum XXX Aurelia Lupula coniugi karissimo fecit\n
EDCS-14600187.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Aurelius Victor, a soldier of the II Italica legion, lost in war to the enemy Gutica, aged 30 years. Aurelia Lupula made this for her dearest husband.
EDCS-14600187.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius Victor, a soldier of the II Italica legion, who died in battle against Gutica, and was made by his wife Aurelia Lupula, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600188.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Maximo civis Surus ex regione Zeugma vico Hennia annorum XXV Aurelius Bassus Barathae vivus fecit fratri et Aurelio Sabino civis Surus ex regione Zeugma vico \n
EDCS-14600188.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Aurelius Maximus, a citizen from Surus, from the region Zeugma, village Hennia, aged 25 years. Aurelius Bassus Barathae, while alive, made this for his brother and for Aurelius Sabinus, a citizen from Surus, from the region Zeugma, village [---].
EDCS-14600188.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Aurelius Bassus Barathae for his deceased brother Aurelius Maximus and for Aurelius Sabinus, both citizens from the Zeugma region, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600189.text_edition: \n Caio Iunio Venusto patri sanctissimo Trosia Successa uxor pientissimo et Caio Iunio Potenti nnorum III Marcus Pompeius Po de suo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-14600189.translation: To Gaius Junius Venustus, most holy father, Trosia Successa, his devoted wife, and to Gaius Junius Potens, aged three years, Marcus Pompeius Po, at his own expense, took care to have this made.
EDCS-14600189.summary: This funerary monument, likely a tombstone, was erected by Marcus Pompeius Po in memory of his father-in-law Gaius Junius Venustus, his wife Trosia Successa, and a young child, Gaius Junius Potens.

EDCS-14600191.summary: This inscription records the name and age of Osica, likely on a tombstone, dated to her lifetime or shortly after her death.

EDCS-14600192.text_edition: \n Vindu Comatillae viva fecit sibi et Satullae filiae annorum XXX\n
EDCS-14600192.translation: Vindu made this for herself and her daughter Satulla, aged 30 years, while alive.
EDCS-14600192.summary: This is a funerary epitaph where Vindu dedicated the monument for herself and her daughter Satulla while still alive.

EDCS-14600193.translation: [text illegible] O to [his/her] son IA A [text illegible]
EDCS-14600193.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to record a dedication to a son by an individual whose name is largely lost.

EDCS-14600194.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Baebius Speratinus obitus annorum LXX Speratus filius et Candidae uxori\n
EDCS-14600194.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Baebius Speratinus, who died aged 70 years, [and] Speratus, his son, and his wife Candida.
EDCS-14600194.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Baebius Speratinus at age 70 and also commemorates his son Speratus and wife Candida.

EDCS-14600195.text_edition: \n Maximus Masculi annorum LX et Amatia Kalandina coniux Excingomaro Valentis filio annorum C et Tertiae Comati filiae uxori annorum LX Masculus Excingomari filius annorum LXXX Ecouta Sexti annorum LXX Maximilla filia Adiutus Excingomari annorum LXXX Marcianus Masculi annorum L Excingomari et Tertia et Maxumilla\n
EDCS-14600195.translation: Maximus Masculi, aged 60 years, and Amatia Kalandina, his wife, to Excingomarus Valentis, son, aged 100 years, and to Tertia Comati, daughter, his wife, aged 60 years, Masculus Excingomari, son, aged 80 years, Ecouta Sexti, aged 70 years, Maximilla, daughter, Adiutus Excingomari, aged 80 years, Marcianus Masculi, aged 50 years, Excingomari and Tertia and Maxumilla.
EDCS-14600195.summary: This lengthy inscription appears to be a family tombstone commemorating multiple individuals with their names, ages, and familial relationships, likely dating from the late Roman period.

EDCS-14600197.text_edition: \n Ausca Adiutoris viva fecit sibi et Ianuario filio annorum XXV Calandina Calandini filia annorum LX obita\n
EDCS-14600197.translation: Ausca Adiutoris made this for herself and her son Ianuarius, aged 25 years, [and] Calandina Calandini, daughter, who died aged 60 years.
EDCS-14600197.summary: This funerary inscription records that Ausca Adiutoris dedicated the monument for herself, her son Ianuarius, and her deceased daughter Calandina.

EDCS-14600198.text_edition: \n et Primus filius vivi fecerunt sibi Iulius Iustus annorum XV\n
EDCS-14600198.translation: and Primus, his son, while alive, made this for themselves. Julius Iustus, aged 15 years.
EDCS-14600198.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Primus and his son Iulius Iustus (aged 15) for themselves while alive.

EDCS-14600199.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valentinus libertus vivus fecit sibi et suis Ursulae coniugi carissimae annorum XXV et Ursi filio annorum V\n
EDCS-14600199.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Valentinus, freedman, made this for himself and his dearest wife Ursula, aged 25 years, and their son Ursius, aged 5 years, while alive.
EDCS-14600199.summary: This funerary epitaph records that the freedman Valentinus dedicated the monument for himself, his wife Ursula, and their son Ursius.

EDCS-14600201.text_edition: \n Ausscus Saturnini et Verecundae coniugi optimae vivus fecit sibi et Avito filio annorum XXV\n
EDCS-14600201.translation: Ausscus Saturnini made this for himself and Verecunda, his excellent wife, and for his son Avitus, aged 25 years, while alive.
EDCS-14600201.summary: This inscription records that Ausscus Saturnini dedicated a monument for himself, his wife Verecunda, and their son Avitus.

EDCS-14600202.text_edition: \n Dis deabusque omnibus Aurelius Surus et Aelius Atticianus votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-14600202.translation: To all the gods and goddesses, Aurelius Surus and Aelius Atticianus gladly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-14600202.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to all gods and goddesses by Aurelius Surus and Aelius Atticianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600205.text_edition: \n Quinto Curiatio Cai filio Festo anni I Appia Brigia mater fecit\n
EDCS-14600205.translation: Appia Brigia, his mother, made this for Quintus Curiatius Festus, son of Caius, aged one year.
EDCS-14600205.summary: This is a funerary monument for an infant named Quintus Curiatius Festus, made by his mother, Appia Brigia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600206.text_edition: \n Lucio Memmio Luci liberto Andriae annorum LXXXX et Acauconi coniugi et Lucio Memmio Scorpo annorum LX et Musae coniugi annorum LV Cosmus filius faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-14600206.translation: Cosmus, son of Lucius Memmius, arranged for the construction of this monument for Lucius Memmius Andria, freedman of Lucius, aged 90 years, and his wife Acauconis, and for Lucius Memmius Scorpus, aged 60 years, and his wife Musa, aged 55 years.
EDCS-14600206.summary: This is a funerary inscription commissioned by Cosmus for his father Lucius Memmius Andria, his father's wife Acauconis, and his own brother Lucius Memmius Scorpus and his wife Musa, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600207.text_edition: \n Noiibio sic Docnimari annorum XXXV hic situs est\n
EDCS-14600207.translation: Noiibius, son of Docnimarus, aged 35 years, lies here.
EDCS-14600207.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription stating that Noiibius, son of Docnimarus, aged 35, is buried here, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600208.text_edition: \n Quartus Cattaes filius vivus fecit sibi et Sammuni Castionis filiae coniugi pientissimae et fidelissimae et Sexto filio obito annorum XX\n
EDCS-14600208.translation: Quartus, son of Cattaes, made this while alive for himself, and for his most dutiful and faithful wife Sammunis, daughter of Castio, and for his son Sextus, who died at the age of 20.
EDCS-14600208.summary: This is a funerary epitaph commissioned by Quartus, son of Cattaes, for himself, his wife Sammunis, and his deceased son Sextus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600209.text_edition: \n Vitalis Secundi libertus et Annia Botta vivi fecerunt sibi et Secundinae filiae annorum VII defunctae et Ingenuo Sabini et Vitalini \n
EDCS-14600209.translation: Vitalis, freedman of Secundus, and Annia Botta, made this while alive for themselves, and for their deceased daughter Secundina, aged 7 years, and for Ingenuus, son of Sabinus, and Vitalina.
EDCS-14600209.summary: This is a funerary epitaph commissioned by Vitalis, a freedman of Secundus, and Annia Botta, for themselves, their deceased daughter Secundina, and two other individuals, Ingenuus and Vitalina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600212.text_edition: \n Triccus Marini filius et Spectata coniux vivi fecerunt sibi et Aelio Valerino annorum XX\n
EDCS-14600212.translation: Triccus, son of Marinus, and his wife Spectata, made this while alive for themselves and for Aelius Valerinus, aged 20 years.
EDCS-14600212.summary: This is a funerary epitaph commissioned by Triccus, son of Marinus, and his wife Spectata, for themselves and for Aelius Valerinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600213.text_edition: \n Quintus Veratius Honoratus decurio Iuli Carnici sibi et Paganiae Piae uxori optimae et Quintus Veratius Iuventianus sibi et Caeliae Cai filiae Ritullae uxori pudentissimae\n
EDCS-14600213.translation: Quintus Veratius Honoratus, decurion of Iulius Carnicus, for himself and for his excellent wife Pagania Pia, and Quintus Veratius Iuventianus, for himself and for his most modest wife Caelia Ritulla, daughter of Caius.
EDCS-14600213.summary: This is a funerary epitaph commissioned by two individuals, Quintus Veratius Honoratus and Quintus Veratius Iuventianus, for themselves and their respective wives, Pagania Pia and Caelia Ritulla, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600214.text_edition: \n Cabalio Sauri filio et Dresiae Busturi filiae coniugi\n
EDCS-14600214.translation: For Cabalio, son of Saurus, and his wife Dresia, daughter of Busturus.
EDCS-14600214.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Cabalio, son of Saurus, and his wife Dresia, daughter of Busturus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600217.text_edition: \n Herculi et Victoriae Augustae sacrum pro salute et adventu Marci Munati Sullae Cerialis clarissimi viri optimi praesidis et integerrimi Vibena Vibeni et Finitus Corbi maritus libentes votum solverunt\n
EDCS-14600217.translation: To Hercules and Victoria Augusta, sacred for the safety and arrival of Marcus Munatius Sulla Cerialis, a most distinguished, excellent, and upright governor. Vibena, wife of Vibenus, and Finitus Corbi, husband, gladly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-14600217.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Hercules and Victoria Augusta by Vibena and Finitus Corbi for the safety and arrival of the governor Marcus Munatius Sulla Cerialis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600218.text_edition: \n Victoriae Augustae Publius Aelius S et Vindia Vera votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-14600218.translation: Publius Aelius S and Vindia Vera gladly and deservedly paid their vow to Imperial Victory.
EDCS-14600218.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by Publius Aelius S and Vindia Vera to Imperial Victory, likely on a votive monument.

EDCS-14600219.text_edition: \n Attus Oiconis filius Victoriae Augustae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14600219.translation: Attus, son of Oiconis, gladly and deservedly paid his vow to Imperial Victory.
EDCS-14600219.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by Attus, son of Oiconis, to Imperial Victory, likely on a votive monument.

EDCS-14600220.text_edition: \n nio Marco Valerio Cottae militi legionis XX Valeriae victricis \n
EDCS-14600220.translation: to Marcus Valerius Cotta, soldier of the Twentieth Legion Valeria Victrix.
EDCS-14600220.summary: This inscription is a dedication or memorial to Marcus Valerius Cotta, a soldier of the Twentieth Legion Valeria Victrix.

EDCS-14600222.text_edition: \n Baro Masculi annorum LXX et Antoniae Respecti libertae annorum et Baro filio Respect[us ------\n
EDCS-14600222.translation: Baro, son of Masculus, aged 70, and Antonia Respecta, freedwoman, aged [and] Baro, son Respectus [...]
EDCS-14600222.summary: This inscription records the ages of Baro, son of Masculus, and Antonia Respecta, a freedwoman, along with a mention of a son named Baro Respectus, likely on a tombstone.

EDCS-14600223.text_edition: \n Candidus Vibeni filius vivus fecit sibi et Mentitae Amoeni filiae coniugi optimae et Candidiano filio militi alae Aurianae annorum XX\n
EDCS-14600223.translation: Candidus, son of Vibenus, made this living for himself and for Mentita, daughter of Amoenus, his excellent wife, and for Candidianus, a soldier of the Ala Auriana, aged 20.
EDCS-14600223.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication made by Candidus, son of Vibenus, for himself, his wife Mentita, and his son Candidianus, a soldier.

EDCS-14600224.text_edition: \n Tertio Quadrati Tertulla Optati viva fecit sibi et marito\n
EDCS-14600224.translation: Tertulla, daughter of Tertius Quadratus, living, made this for herself and her husband.
EDCS-14600224.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication made by Tertulla, daughter of Tertius Quadratus, for herself and her husband.

EDCS-14600226.text_edition: \n Gratus et Ursicinus et Paternus filii fecerunt sibi et parentibus Urso et Optatae coniugi\n
EDCS-14600226.translation: Gratus, Ursicinus, and Paternus, sons, made this for themselves and for their parents Ursa and Optata, his wife.
EDCS-14600226.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication made by three sons, Gratus, Ursicinus, and Paternus, for themselves and their parents.

EDCS-14600227.text_edition: \n Asclepio Augusto Quintus Sabinius Onesimus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14600227.translation: Quintus Sabinius Onesimus gladly and deservedly paid his vow to Asclepius Augustus.
EDCS-14600227.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by Quintus Sabinius Onesimus to the god Asclepius Augustus, likely on a votive monument.

EDCS-14600228.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et omnibus dibus Marcus Ulpius Philippus beneficiarius Egnati Prisci procuratoris Augusti votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14600228.translation: Marcus Ulpius Philippus, a *beneficiarius* of Egnatius Priscus, imperial procurator, gladly and deservedly paid his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest and all the gods.
EDCS-14600228.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by Marcus Ulpius Philippus, an official under the procurator Egnatius Priscus, to Jupiter and all the gods, likely on a votive monument.

EDCS-14600229.text_edition: \n Victoriae Augustae sacrum Fortunatus VRVL IFORI votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14600229.translation: Fortunatus VRVL IFORI gladly and deservedly paid his vow to Imperial Victory.
EDCS-14600229.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by Fortunatus to Imperial Victory, likely on a votive monument, with partially restored text for the dedicator's name and origin.

EDCS-14600230.text_edition: \n tinus Marci Artori Iactualis libertus III coni<i>ugi fecit \n
EDCS-14600230.translation: Tinus, freedman of Marcus Artorius Iactualis, made this for his wife.
EDCS-14600230.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by a freedman to his wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600232.text_edition: \n Faustus Passeris obitus annorum LX Ategenta Iuvenalis filia sibi et marito viva fecit\n
EDCS-14600232.translation: Faustus, aged 60, died. Ategenta Iuvenalis, daughter, made this for herself and her husband while alive.
EDCS-14600232.summary: A funerary monument set up by Ategenta Iuvenalis for herself and her husband, recording the death of Faustus, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600233.text_edition: \n Ianuarius Lolli Prisci libertus obitus annorum LXXV Candida marito carissimo viva fecit\n
EDCS-14600233.translation: Ianuarius, freedman of Lollius Priscus, aged 75, died. Candida made this for her dearest husband while alive.
EDCS-14600233.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Candida for her husband Ianuarius, a freedman, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600234.text_edition: \n Lollius Martius et Lollius Antolianus sibi et Lolliae Martiae obitae annorum XXXVII\n
EDCS-14600234.translation: Lollius Martius and Lollius Antolianus [made this] for themselves and for Lollia Martia, who died at the age of 37.
EDCS-14600234.summary: A funerary monument for Lollius Martius, Lollius Antolianus, and Lollia Martia, likely set up by the first two for themselves and the deceased, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600236.text_edition: \n Peregrino Iuli Moderati servo nnorum XXI Speratus et Peregrina parentes vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-14600236.translation: Peregrinus, slave of Iulius Moderatus, aged 21. Speratus and Peregrina, parents, made this while alive.
EDCS-14600236.summary: A funerary monument for a slave named Peregrinus, erected by his parents, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600241.text_edition: \n Bedaio Augusto Publius Domitius Flaccus et Quintus Lucretius Optatus IIviri Pompeiano et Avito consulibus\n
EDCS-14600241.translation: To Bedaio Augustus: Publius Domitius Flaccus and Quintus Lucretius Optatus, duovirs, under the consulship of Pompeianus and Avitus.
EDCS-14600241.summary: A dedication to the deified emperor Bedaio Augustus by two duovirs, dating to the consulship of Pompeianus and Avitus in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600242.text_edition: \n Bedaio Augusto sacrum Alonarum Lucius Cassius Lucullus et Caius Vindius Constans IIviri Fusco II et Dextro consulibus\n
EDCS-14600242.translation: Sacred to Bedaio Augustus: Lucius Cassius Lucullus and Caius Vindius Constans, duovirs, under the consulship of Fuscus II and Dexter.
EDCS-14600242.summary: A sacred dedication to the deified emperor Bedaio Augustus by two duovirs, dating to the consulship of Fuscus II and Dexter in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600243.text_edition: \n Sacro Alounarum sic Augustarum Nonius Iuvenalis et Lucius Vigrius Victorinus Gordiano et Pompeiano consulibus votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-14600243.translation: To the Sacred Alounae, indeed the August ones: Nonius Iuvenalis and Lucius Vigrius Victorinus fulfilled their vow willingly and deservedly, under the consulship of Gordianus and Pompeianus.
EDCS-14600243.summary: A fulfillment of a vow by two individuals to the Sacred Alounae, dated to the consulship of Gordianus and Pompeianus in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600244.text_edition: \n XII IIviri Glabrione et Homullo consulibus\n
EDCS-14600244.translation: Under the consulship of Glabrio and Homullus.
EDCS-14600244.summary: A very short inscription possibly marking the year of a public office or event, dated to the consulship of Glabrio and Homullus.

EDCS-14600245.text_edition: \n Mariae Cai filiae Avitae Publius Septimius Ursus coniugi testamento poni iussit Annia Avita mater posuit Aeliis Ovilavis Annia Avita posuit\n
EDCS-14600245.translation: To Maria, daughter of Caius, Avita. Publius Septimius Ursus ordered this to be set up by testament. Annia Avita, mother, set this up. Aelius Ovilavis, Annia Avita, set this up.
EDCS-14600245.summary: A funerary monument for Maria, ordered by her husband and set up by her mother and another individual, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600247.text_edition: \n Caius Betuus Communis vivus fecit sibi et Flaviae Crescentinae coniugi @(obitae) annorum XXXV\n
EDCS-14600247.translation: Gaius Betuus Communis made this for himself and his wife Flavia Crescentina, who died at the age of 35.
EDCS-14600247.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Gaius Betuus Communis for himself and his deceased wife, Flavia Crescentina.

EDCS-14600248.text_edition: \n Lucio Papio Silvestri patri Papio Hilarianoanoeterano? ex? b(ene)f(iciario) consularis anno--- LI heres ex testamento faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-14600248.translation: Papio Hilarianus, former beneficiarius of the consular governor, had this made by his heir according to the will, in his 51st year.
EDCS-14600248.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Papio Hilarianus, indicating he was a former beneficiarius and the monument was erected by his heir as per his will.

EDCS-14600249.text_edition: \n Sagittiae Cai filiae Florentinae annorum XX Titus Iulius Verecundus coniugi vivus fecit sibi et suis\n
EDCS-14600249.translation: Titus Iulius Verecundus made this for his wife Sagittia Florentina, daughter of Gaius, who died at the age of 20, and for himself and his family while he was alive.
EDCS-14600249.summary: This is a funerary epitaph erected by Titus Iulius Verecundus for his wife Sagittia Florentina and himself, indicating it was made during his lifetime.

EDCS-14600254.text_edition: \n ymphi Pri Titus Flavius Magister T F \n
EDCS-14600254.translation: To the departed nymphs, Titus Flavius Magister, son of Titus.
EDCS-14600254.summary: This is a dedication to nymphs by Titus Flavius Magister.

EDCS-14600255.text_edition: \n sacrum Caius Orgetius Quartus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14600255.translation: Gaius Orgetius Quartus willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-14600255.summary: This is a votive inscription by Gaius Orgetius Quartus fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-14600256.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo dis deabusque omnibus Publi Aeli Saturninus Naevianus Cupitus\n
EDCS-14600256.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, and to all the gods and goddesses, Publius Aelius Saturninus Naevianus Cupitus.
EDCS-14600256.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter and all gods and goddesses by Publius Aelius Saturninus Naevianus Cupitus.

EDCS-14600259.text_edition: \n Valeriae matri et Sois filiae annorum et V Crispi filio annorum et Vir\n
EDCS-14600259.translation: To Valeria, mother, and to [his] daughter Sois, aged [?] and to [his] son Crispus, aged [?].
EDCS-14600259.summary: This is a funerary epitaph fragment mentioning a mother, Valeria, and her children, Sois and Crispus.

EDCS-14600260.text_edition: \n Aur<el=I>iae Ianuar<i>ae filiae obitae annorum X Aur<e=I>(lii) Ianuarius <e=L>t parentes fecerunt \n
EDCS-14600260.translation: Auriliae Ianuariae, daughter, who died at the age of 10. Her parents Aurelius Ianuarius and [?] made this.
EDCS-14600260.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurilia Ianuaria, erected by her parents.

EDCS-14600261.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudio Cupito annorum XIII Novellae sorori annorum XII Claudius Messor et Musso parentes filiis et sibi vivi fecerunt hic siti sunt\n
EDCS-14600261.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Claudius Cupitus, aged 13, and Novella, sister, aged 12. Claudius Messor and Musso, parents, made this for their sons and for themselves while alive. They are buried here.
EDCS-14600261.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for two children, Claudius Cupitus and Novella, set up by their parents who also intended it for themselves.

EDCS-14600262.text_edition: \n Verino Verionis filio beneficiario Useni Secundi annorum XL Dubitata Cupiti filia marito fecit et sibi et Carminio Restituto\n
EDCS-14600262.translation: To Verinus, son of Verio, beneficiarius of Usenius Secundus, aged 40. Dubitata, daughter of Cupitus, made this for her husband and for herself and Carminius Restitutus.
EDCS-14600262.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Verinus, erected by his wife Dubitata, who also included herself and Carminius Restitutus.

EDCS-14600264.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II pater patriae consul proconsul et Marcus Opellius Antoninus Diadumenianus nobilissimus Caesar princeps iuventutis providentissimi Augusti fecerunt a Teurnia milia passuum \n
EDCS-14600264.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the second time, father of the fatherland, consul, proconsul, and Marcus Opellius Antoninus Diadumenianus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, our most provident emperor, had this constructed from Teurnia for a distance of [X] miles.
EDCS-14600264.summary: This inscription records a public work, likely a road, commissioned by Emperors Macrinus and Diadumenianus, and likely dates to their joint reign (217-218 AD).

EDCS-14600326.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo dis deabusque omnibus et Genio huiusce loci Cai filius \n
EDCS-14600326.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, all gods and goddesses, and to the Genius of this place, Caius, son of [father's name], made [this offering].
EDCS-14600326.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and other deities, as well as the Genius loci, likely made by a private individual.

EDCS-14600327.text_edition: \n Nibonia ILIANO filia Conina votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-14600327.translation: Nibonia Iliana, daughter of Conina, fulfilled her vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-14600327.summary: This is a votive inscription by Nibonia Iliana, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-14600328.text_edition: \n Cocusioni Luteivaes Monnus nepos vivus fecit\n
EDCS-14600328.translation: Monnus, grandson of Luteiva, made this while alive.
EDCS-14600328.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a monument built by Monnus for himself during his lifetime.

EDCS-14600329.text_edition: \n Caius Priscius Vegetus vivus fecit sibi et N\n
EDCS-14600329.translation: Caius Priscius Vegetus made this for himself and N[ame] while alive.
EDCS-14600329.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a monument built by Caius Priscius Vegetus for himself and another person during his lifetime.

EDCS-14600330.text_edition: \n Tattus Sexti filius annorum L fecit munimentum sibi et suis Tatto annorum L Mocio anni I Instituto annorum XV\n
EDCS-14600330.translation: Tattus, son of Sextus, aged 50, made this protective structure for himself and his family: for Tatto, aged 50, for Mocio, aged 1, and for Instituto, aged 15.
EDCS-14600330.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a tomb built by Tattus for himself and his family members of various ages.

EDCS-14600331.text_edition: \n Divae Iuliae Lucius Cassius Claudia Maximus |(centurio) legionis VI ferratae testamento fieri iussit Domitiae Augustae Lucius Cassius Claudia Maximus |(centurio) legionis VI ferratae testamento fieri iussit Lucius Cassius Eudaemon faciendas curavit\n
EDCS-14600331.translation: Lucius Cassius Claudia Maximus, centurion of the VI Ferrata legion, ordered this to be made by testament for the divine Julia; Lucius Cassius Claudia Maximus, centurion of the VI Ferrata legion, ordered this to be made by testament for Domitia Augusta; Lucius Cassius Eudaemon saw to its construction.
EDCS-14600331.summary: This inscription records a testament stipulating the construction of a monument for Divia Julia and Domitia Augusta, executed by Lucius Cassius Claudia Maximus and supervised by Lucius Cassius Eudaemon.

EDCS-14600333.text_edition: \n Publius Vedius Publi filius Potitus fecit sibi et Vediae Titi filiae Paullae coniugi et Publio Vedio Avito filio nnorum XXX\n
EDCS-14600333.translation: Publius Vedius Potitus, son of Publius, made this for himself, for his wife Vedia Paula, daughter of Titus, and for his son Publius Vedius Avitus, aged 30.
EDCS-14600333.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a monument built by Publius Vedius Potitus for himself, his wife, and his son.

EDCS-14600335.text_edition: \n Flavius Ianuarius miles vivus fecit condita sepulcro hic pausat Ursa Chrestiana sic fidelis annorum XXXVIII per partum subito ducente inpio fato est tradita Tartaris imis et me subito linquit sibi coniugem pro tempore iunctum quem sic ambulo et quaero miser quem sic ipse aeterna condidi terra o quit sic tribuat genesis qui sic separat convirginios dulcis sic ut non licuit nobis iugiter supernam frunisci caritatem hoc dico legentibus et lacrimis prosequor verba coniuncti amantis sic semper se bene dicere debent quia nihil erit dulcius quam prima iuventus\n
EDCS-14600335.translation: Flavius Ianuarius, a soldier, made this tomb while alive. Ursa Chrestiana, so faithful, aged 38, rests here; she was suddenly taken by childbirth, delivered to the deepest Tartarus, and has suddenly left me. I myself wander and search for my eternal spouse, whom I have thus joined. May Genesis grant that those who separate sweet married couples, since it was not permitted for us to enjoy our everlasting love, never do so. I say this to those who read, and I follow these words of a united lover with tears. They should always speak well of themselves, because nothing will be sweeter than first youth.
EDCS-14600335.summary: This is a poignant and somewhat complex funerary inscription by a soldier, Flavius Ianuarius, mourning his wife Ursa Chrestiana who died in childbirth and reflecting on their love and the fleeting nature of life.

EDCS-14600336.text_edition: \n Candido Tassarni filio annorum LXX et Noveniae Camuli filiae parentibus filii fecerunt\n
EDCS-14600336.translation: The sons made this for their parents, Candido, son of Tassarnus, aged 70, and Novenia, daughter of Camulus, aged [age not specified].
EDCS-14600336.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a monument built by sons for their parents, Candido and Novenia.

EDCS-14600337.text_edition: \n VI Nonas Maias deposi<t=C>io Valeriae viduae \n
EDCS-14600337.translation: On the 6th of May, the burial of Valeria, the widow.
EDCS-14600337.summary: This inscription records the burial of a woman named Valeria, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600351.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et dis deabusque et Genio dominorum nnostrorum Auggustorum Caius Aemilius Respectus beneficiarius consularis legionis II Italicae piae fidelis pro se et suis votum solvit libens merito Pompeiano et Avito consulibus Idibus Maiis\n
EDCS-14600351.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, and to the gods and goddesses, and to the genius of our Emperors Augustus, Caius Aemilius Respectus, a beneficiarius of the consular governor of the Second Italian Legion, the Pious and Faithful, paid his vow willingly and deservedly for himself and his family, with Pompeianus and Avitus as consuls, on the Ides of May.
EDCS-14600351.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter and the imperial genius by a soldier of the Legio II Italica during the consulship of Pompeianus and Avitus, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-14600353.text_edition: \n Aiuni Castici filiae Caius Iulius Cai libertus Clymeno vivus fecit de suo\n
EDCS-14600353.translation: Caius Iulius Clymenus, freedman of Caius, made this during his lifetime at his own expense for Aiunia Castica, his daughter.
EDCS-14600353.summary: This is a funerary dedication set up by a freedman for his daughter, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600355.text_edition: \n Primo Calandina filia viva fecit et sibi\n
EDCS-14600355.translation: Primo, the daughter, made this during her lifetime for herself.
EDCS-14600355.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by a woman named Primo for herself, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600356.text_edition: \n Rufius Musicus et Vibena Ingenui filia uxor vivi fecerunt sibi et Masculo filio annorum VII\n
EDCS-14600356.translation: Rufius Musicus and Vibena, daughter of Ingenuus, his wife, made this during their lifetime for themselves and for their son Masculus, aged 7.
EDCS-14600356.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by a couple for themselves and their young son, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600359.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Priminus vivus fecit sibi et Veneriae coniugi karissimae\n
EDCS-14600359.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Priminus, alive, made this for himself and for Veneria, his dearest wife.
EDCS-14600359.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by a man named Priminus for himself and his wife Veneria, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600362.text_edition: \n Clemenoni sic Poti servo et Sporae Pametae libertae uxori filii fecerunt Fin et Messori\n
EDCS-14600362.translation: Fin and Messor, his sons, made this for Clemenon, their slave, and for Sporae Pametae, his freedwoman wife.
EDCS-14600362.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication made by sons for their deceased slave father and his freedwoman wife, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600363.text_edition: \n Borissus sibi et Secundinae Vercai filiae uxori et Secundino filio et Mussae uxori eius N\n
EDCS-14600363.translation: Borissus for himself and for Secundina Vercai, his wife, and for Secundinus, his son, and for Musa, his wife.
EDCS-14600363.summary: This inscription appears to be a family tomb record or dedication, naming Borissus, his wives Secundina and Musa, and his son Secundinus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600364.text_edition: \n Maro Tertulli annorum LXL Bononia Secundiini annorum XXXIII Marcianus annorum LXXXV Verekundus sic <a>nnorum L\n
EDCS-14600364.translation: Maro, aged 50, Bononia, aged 33, Marcianus, aged 85, Verekundus, aged 50.
EDCS-14600364.summary: This inscription lists the names and ages of four individuals, likely representing a family or group buried together, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600365.text_edition: \n Dibus sic Manibus Valerius Tertuliliani vibus sic fecit coniugii Secundinae obitae <an=NA>(norum) XXX Valentinus annorum XXX Valerius annorum XL Tertulla \n
EDCS-14600365.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Valerius Tertuliliani made this for his wife Secundina, who died at the age of 30, for Valentinus, aged 30, for Valerius, aged 40, and for Tertulla.
EDCS-14600365.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication made by Valerius for his deceased wife Secundina and other family members, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14600366.text_edition: \n Tertius Rigonis filius nnorum C Tertia Belatulli annorum C Latinus filius fecit Specetatus Terti filius annorum X\n
EDCS-14600366.translation: Tertius, son of Rigonis, aged 100. Tertia, daughter of Belatulla, and Latinus, her son, made this. Specetatus, son of Tertius, aged 10.
EDCS-14600366.summary: This inscription records the ages of three individuals, Tertius, Tertia, and Latinus, and that Specetatus, son of Tertius, erected it, likely a tombstone.

EDCS-14600367.summary: This inscription records the name and parentage of Acutus, likely a tombstone indicating his age or his memorial.

EDCS-14600368.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Adiutor MPIIS coniugi cundi fecit\n
EDCS-14600368.translation: To the departed spirits. Adiutor made this for his wife, MPIIS.
EDCS-14600368.summary: This funerary inscription records Adiutor dedicating a monument to his deceased wife, MPIIS.

EDCS-14600369.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus di bae sibi et Seli marito karissimo nnorum XXXV\n
EDCS-14600369.translation: To the departed spirits. Dibae made this for herself and for her dearest husband Selius, aged 35.
EDCS-14600369.summary: This funerary inscription records Dibae making provision for herself and her husband Selius, who died at the age of 35.

EDCS-14600370.text_edition: \n Cassius Primianus vivus fecit sibi et Terentiae Iuliae coniugi iucundissimae\n
EDCS-14600370.translation: Cassius Primianus made this while alive for himself and for his most beloved wife Terentia Julia.
EDCS-14600370.summary: This inscription records Cassius Primianus making provision for himself and his wife Terentia Julia during his lifetime, likely a tomb or memorial.

EDCS-14600371.text_edition: \n ASIIari fili-- viv-- fecit Otu Senonis filia hic sita est annorum XX\n
EDCS-14600371.translation: ASIIarius, son of [---], lived [---]. Otu, daughter of Seno, erected this. She is buried here, aged 20.
EDCS-14600371.summary: This inscription records the death of Otu, daughter of Seno, aged 20, and possibly mentions ASIIarius, likely a tombstone.

EDCS-14600373.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Septimus Titi Vari Surionis servus vivus fecit sibi et Quintae coniugi pientissimae annorum XXXV\n
EDCS-14600373.translation: To the departed spirits. Septimus, slave of Titius Varius Surio, made this while alive for himself and for his most dutiful wife Quinte, aged 35.
EDCS-14600373.summary: This funerary inscription records Septimus, a slave, making provision for himself and his wife Quinte, who died at age 35.

EDCS-14600388.text_edition: \n Urso Ursuli filio annorum XXX et Suadra uxor Calventi filia viva fecit et Ursulus et Restuta uxor\n
EDCS-14600388.translation: To Urso, son of Urculus, aged 30. Suadra, wife of Calventus, made this while alive, and Ursulus and Restuta his wife.
EDCS-14600388.summary: This inscription records the memorial of Urso, son of Urculus, aged 30, erected by Suadra, wife of Calventus, and possibly others named Ursulus and Restuta.

EDCS-14600390.text_edition: \n Caius Florentinius Censorinus vivus et Aelia Brenturia coniux vivi fecerunt sibi et Florentinio Floro filio carissimo equiti alae Commagenorum primae singulari stupendiorum sic XX annorum XL et Caio Aelio C et Florentinia Florentina annorum LXX\n
EDCS-14600390.translation: Gaius Florentinius Censorinus and his wife Aelia Brenturia made this while alive for themselves, and for their dearest son Florentinius Florus, a cavalryman of the first ala of Commageni, a singular and astonishing soldier, aged 20, and for Gaius Aelius C., aged 40, and for Florentinia Florentina, aged 70.
EDCS-14600390.summary: This extensive funerary monument inscription records Gaius Florentinius Censorinus and his wife Aelia Brenturia providing for themselves, their son Florentinius Florus (an accomplished cavalryman who died at 20), Gaius Aelius C. (aged 40), and Florentinia Florentina (aged 70).

EDCS-14600391.text_edition: \n Satico Censoris annorum LXXX Candida Celvi annorum LX\n
EDCS-14600391.translation: Saticus, son of Censor, aged 80. Candida, daughter of Celvus, aged 60.
EDCS-14600391.summary: This inscription records the ages of two individuals, Saticus and Candida, likely a tombstone memorial.

EDCS-14600392.translation: Calventius, freedman of Mauso, and Calandina.
EDCS-14600392.summary: This inscription likely records the names of Calventius and Calandina, possibly on a tombstone.

EDCS-14600393.text_edition: \n Calventius Mercatoris libertus sibi vivus fecit et Candidae coniugi obitae\n
EDCS-14600393.translation: Calventius Mercatoris, freedman, made this for himself while alive, and for his deceased wife Candida.
EDCS-14600393.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication made by Calventius Mercatoris for himself and his deceased wife.

EDCS-14600394.text_edition: \n Cavecio Loi filio obito annorum L Uragiso Tessilli filia <c=G>oniugi et sibi viva fecit \n
EDCS-14600394.translation: Uragiso, daughter of Tessillus, made this for her deceased son Cavecius, aged 50, and for herself while alive.
EDCS-14600394.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication made by Uragiso for her deceased son Cavecius and herself.

EDCS-14600395.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Amanda Quinti Tessi liberta obita annorum L Firmus Litugenae maritus ex proprio coniugi carissimae fecit et sibi\n
EDCS-14600395.translation: To the Divine Shades. Amanda, freedwoman of Quintus Tessi, aged 50, and her husband Firmus Litugena, made this from his own property for his dearest wife and for himself.
EDCS-14600395.summary: This funerary dedication was made by Firmus Litugena for his deceased wife Amanda and himself.

EDCS-14600396.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Maximinus Constantis et Annilio Litugenae vivi sibi fecerunt et posterisque suis\n
EDCS-14600396.translation: To the Divine Shades. Maximinus and Annilio Litugena, while alive, made this for themselves and for their descendants.
EDCS-14600396.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication made by Maximinus and Annilio Litugena for themselves and their descendants.

EDCS-14600398.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Paterna viva fecit sibi \n
EDCS-14600398.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aurelia Paterna made this for herself while alive.
EDCS-14600398.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication made by Aurelia Paterna for herself.

EDCS-14600402.text_edition: \n Melissa viva fecit Cassio Severino legionis X geminae piae fidelis obito annorum XXXV Serenae coniugi @(obitae) annorum XXV\n
EDCS-14600402.translation: Melissa made this while alive for Cassius Severinus, of the X Gemina Pia Fidelis legion, deceased at age 35, and for Serena his wife, deceased at age 25.
EDCS-14600402.summary: This funerary dedication was made by Melissa for Cassius Severinus, a soldier of the X Gemina legion, and his wife Serena.

EDCS-14600406.text_edition: \n Sextus Iuliae Litugenae libertus vivus fecit sibi et suis\n
EDCS-14600406.translation: Sextus, freedman of Iulia Litugena, while alive, made this for himself and his family.
EDCS-14600406.summary: This funerary dedication was made by Sextus for himself and his family.

EDCS-14600407.text_edition: \n Primus Cai Veponi Praesentis servus vivus fecit sibi et Primulae coniugi et Firmae filiae annorum XIIII et Insecuventi sic filio annorum XVIII\n
EDCS-14600407.translation: Primus, slave of Caius Veponius Praesens, made this while alive for himself, for his wife Primula, for his daughter Firma aged 14, and for his son Insecuventus aged 18.
EDCS-14600407.summary: This funerary dedication was made by Primus, a slave, for himself and his family.

EDCS-14600411.text_edition: \n Silvano Tertii filio et Togiae Vercombogi filiae vivus fecit sibi et coniugi\n
EDCS-14600411.translation: Made this while alive for Silvanus, son of Tertius, and for Togia, daughter of Vercombonus, for himself and his wife.
EDCS-14600411.summary: This funerary dedication was made by an unnamed individual for Silvanus, Togia, himself, and his wife.

EDCS-14600418.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Ulpius Primus annorum LX testamento poni iussit Ulpiae Sisiuni uxori et Ulpiae \n
EDCS-14600418.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Marcus Ulpius Primus, aged 60, ordered this to be set up in his will for his wife Ulpia Sisiuna and for Ulpia.
EDCS-14600418.summary: A funerary epitaph, likely from the Roman imperial period, recording the wishes of Marcus Ulpius Primus for his burial plot.

EDCS-14600420.text_edition: \n Genio legionis II Italicae piae Marcus Gavius Firmus primus pilus Vellina sic Firmo Piceno dedicante Caio Memmio Fido Iulio Albio consule designato legato Augusti pro praetore XIIII Kalendas Octobres Aproniano et Bradua consulibus\n
EDCS-14600420.translation: To the Genius of the Second Italian Legion, pious. Marcus Gavius Firmus, primus pilus (first spear officer), from Vellina, in his own name and that of Firmo Piceno, dedicated this while Gaius Memmius Fidus Iulius Albius, designated consul, was legate of the emperor pro praetore, on the 18th day before the Kalends of October (September 14th), with Apronianus and Bradua as consuls.
EDCS-14600420.summary: An altar dedicated to the Genius of the II Italica legion by an officer, Marcus Gavius Firmus, dated to the consulship of Apronianus and Bradua.

EDCS-14800149.text_edition: \n Masculus Ianuari libertus vivus fecit sibi et Daglandrunae coniugi pientissimae\n
EDCS-14800149.translation: Masculus, freedman of Ianuarius, made this while alive for himself and for his most devoted wife, Daglandruna.
EDCS-14800149.summary: A funerary monument commissioned by Masculus for himself and his wife, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-14800150.translation: Celsus, son of Camio, OAE IIC
EDCS-14800150.summary: A very fragmentary inscription, possibly funerary, from the Roman imperial period, with personal names and unclear abbreviations.

EDCS-14800151.text_edition: \n tonius i libertus Itius EMA I \n
EDCS-14800151.summary: A fragmentary inscription, likely funerary or honorary, from the Roman imperial period, mentioning a freedman and an unclear abbreviation.

EDCS-14800153.summary: An extremely fragmentary inscription, only preserving parts of names and possibly numerical notations, from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-14800154.summary: A dedicatory inscription to the goddess Victoria Augusta, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-14800161.text_edition: \n SA SIRA PirenAEIAS garrulae munus A puer--- Asclepiad in pectore consens A dedit lapidem inscripsit Verone---\n
EDCS-14800161.translation: SA SIRA PirenAEIAS garrulae munus A puer--- Asclepiad in pectore consens A dedit lapidem inscripsit Verone---
EDCS-14800161.summary: A heavily damaged inscription, likely votive or funerary, mentioning gifts, a child, and possibly a doctor or a place name (Verona), from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-14800162.summary: A dedication or commission made by Sentia Secunda at Aquileia, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-14800163.summary: A dedication or commission made by Sentia Secunda at Aquileia, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15000089.text_edition: \n Pluton sivem sic Iovem infernum dici opornotet Aeracura Iuno inferna acciete iam celerius infra scribtum sic et tradite I Manibus Aurelium Sinnianum Caeserianum Sic Silvia inversum maritum ceernis sic quommodi sic nomen illius scribtum sic est\n
EDCS-15000089.translation: Pluton, who should be called Infernal Jupiter, and Juno Inferna Aeracura, having been invoked, now more quickly as written below, hand over Aurelius Sinnianus Caeserianus to the Underworld Spirits; Silvia, having reversed, you see your husband, as his name is written.
EDCS-15000089.summary: This inscription is a ritualistic text or epitaph, perhaps addressing the spirits of the underworld and identifying the deceased as Aurelius Sinnianus Caeserianus, with a dedication to Pluton and Juno Inferna Aeracura.

EDCS-15100059.translation: The First Cohort of Aelii, the Britons, the Antoninian.
EDCS-15100059.summary: This inscription records the name of a Roman military unit, the Cohors I Aelia Brittonum, with the honorific title Antoniniana, likely dating to the Antonine period.

EDCS-15100060.text_edition: \n Domino fartori Victoriano salutem mox litteras meas perceperis ut statuemus demes litteras meas felicissime excipas sic cum Livia fuimus\n
EDCS-15100060.translation: To Dominus fartor Victor, greetings. You will soon receive my letters as we decide; send me letters back most happily. With Livia we were.
EDCS-15100060.summary: This is a private letter, possibly a fragment of a correspondence, between individuals, likely concerning business or personal matters, with a greeting and instructions for reply.

EDCS-15800386.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Marr(ius) Ursinus veteranus ex iussu posuit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-15800386.translation: Marr(ius) Ursinus, a veteran, placed this willingly, happily, and deservedly at the command of Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus.
EDCS-15800386.summary: This dedication records a veteran, Marr(ius) Ursinus, setting up an offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, likely in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-15800387.text_edition: \n Castori et Polluci sigillum ex iusso sic Iovis Optimi Maximi Dolicheni Vindicii Florentinus et Moderatus votum solverunt libentes laeti merito\n
EDCS-15800387.translation: Vindicius Florentinus and Moderatus fulfilled their vow willingly, happily, and deservedly to Castor and Pollux, by order of Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus.
EDCS-15800387.summary: This inscription records Vindicius Florentinus and Moderatus fulfilling a vow to Castor and Pollux at the behest of Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus.

EDCS-15800388.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Tiberius Vibius Messinus pro salute votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-15800388.translation: Tiberius Vibius Messinus fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly for his well-being to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus.
EDCS-15800388.summary: This dedication records Tiberius Vibius Messinus fulfilling a vow for his health to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus.

EDCS-15800389.text_edition: \n Iovi Dolicheno Postumius Celer decurio ex imperio\n
EDCS-15800389.translation: Postumius Celer, a decurion, by command, to Jupiter Dolichenus.
EDCS-15800389.summary: This dedication records Postumius Celer, a decurion, making an offering to Jupiter Dolichenus by order.

EDCS-15800390.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Maria ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-15800390.translation: Maria placed this by vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus.
EDCS-15800390.summary: This dedication records Maria setting up an offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus in fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-15800391.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Ulpia Iuliana pro salute Gai Vibi Honorati fili votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-15800391.translation: Ulpia Iuliana fulfilled her vow willingly and deservedly for the well-being of her son Gaius Vibius Honoratus to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus.
EDCS-15800391.summary: This dedication records Ulpia Iuliana fulfilling a vow for the health of her son Gaius Vibius Honoratus to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus.

EDCS-15800392.text_edition: \n Dulicheno sic Marrius Ursinus pro se suisque ex iussu votum solvit\n
EDCS-15800392.translation: Marr(ius) Ursinus fulfilled his vow by order for himself and his family to Dolichenus.
EDCS-15800392.summary: This dedication records Marr(ius) Ursinus fulfilling a vow for himself and his family to Jupiter Dolichenus, likely by divine command.

EDCS-15800393.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Dolicheno Iusta Pastoris votum solvit libens laeta\n
EDCS-15800393.translation: Iusta Pastoris gladly and joyfully fulfilled her vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus.
EDCS-15800393.summary: A votive inscription dedicating a fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter Dolichenus by Iusta Pastoris, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-15800394.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Claudius Maternus ex voto pro se et suis votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-15800394.translation: Claudius Maternus, in accordance with his vow, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for himself and his family to Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus.
EDCS-15800394.summary: A votive inscription from Claudius Maternus fulfilling a vow for himself and his family to Jupiter Dolichenus, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-15800395.text_edition: \n Iunoni Reginae Victura votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-15800395.translation: Victura gladly and deservedly fulfilled her vow to Juno Regina.
EDCS-15800395.summary: A votive inscription dedicating a fulfillment of a vow to Juno Regina by Victura, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-15800396.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Vera votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-15800396.translation: Vera gladly and deservedly fulfilled her vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus.
EDCS-15800396.summary: A votive inscription dedicating a fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter Dolichenus by Vera, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-15800397.text_edition: \n Iovi Dulicheno sic Probus et Marina pro se et pro suis\n
EDCS-15800397.translation: Probus and Marina, for themselves and their families, [fulfilled their vow] to Jupiter Dolichenus.
EDCS-15800397.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Dolichenus by Probus and Marina, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-15800398.text_edition: \n Lucius Cassius Ambrosius fecit in circo Flaminio Lucius Cassius Ambrosius\n
EDCS-15800398.translation: Lucius Cassius Ambrosius made this in the Circus Flaminius. Lucius Cassius Ambrosius.
EDCS-15800398.summary: An inscription recording the construction or dedication of something in the Circus Flaminius by Lucius Cassius Ambrosius, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-16100281.text_edition: \n Cai Tituri Festi |(centurionis) cohortis XIIII praetoriae\n
EDCS-16100281.translation: Of Caius Titurius Festus, centurion of the 14th praetorian cohort.
EDCS-16100281.summary: An inscription recording the name and rank of Caius Titurius Festus, a centurion of the Praetorian Guard, likely from the 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-16201562.text_edition: \n Teurniae sanctissimae Augustae Lucius Herennius Epictetus\n
EDCS-16201562.translation: Lucius Herennius Epictetus [dedicated this] to the most sacred Augusta Teurnia.
EDCS-16201562.summary: An inscription dedicating something to Augusta Teurnia by Lucius Herennius Epictetus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-16201563.text_edition: \n Iunoni Reginae iussu dei Dolicheni Claudius Rufinus et Octavia Compsilla pro se dono dederunt\n
EDCS-16201563.translation: Claudius Rufinus and Octavia Compsilla, by order of the god Dolichenus, gave this offering for themselves.
EDCS-16201563.summary: A votive inscription from Claudius Rufinus and Octavia Compsilla, dedicated to Juno Regina by order of Jupiter Dolichenus, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-16500266.translation: Gemellianus made this at Aquae Helveticae.
EDCS-16500266.summary: An inscription recording the construction or dedication of something at the Roman baths of Aquae Helveticae by Gemellianus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-20601146.translation: The First Aelian Cohort of Britons
EDCS-20601146.summary: This inscription names the First Aelian Cohort of Britons, likely a military unit.

EDCS-20601147.translation: The First Aelian Cohort of Britons Antoniniana
EDCS-20601147.summary: This inscription names the First Aelian Cohort of Britons Antoniniana, likely a military unit with an honorific title.

EDCS-20601149.translation: The Jovensian Potteries of the First Legion of Noricans
EDCS-20601149.summary: This inscription refers to the Jovensian Potteries associated with the First Legion of Noricans, likely related to military supply or production.

EDCS-20601150.summary: This inscription names the Second Italian Legion, with an incomplete or abbreviated designation following.

EDCS-20601152.summary: This inscription records a quantity of 520 rings belonging to Antiochus, likely a record of production or inventory.

EDCS-20601153.summary: This inscription mentions Antiochus and his age of 18, possibly a funerary or personal marker.

EDCS-20601158.summary: This inscription identifies Falernian wine belonging to Herennius Priscus, indicating ownership or origin of a product.

EDCS-20601161.text_edition: \n ntin plena pondo CXV Cai Iuli Communis\n
EDCS-20601161.translation: full measure, 115 pounds, by Caius Iulius Communis
EDCS-20601161.summary: This inscription records a full measure of 115 pounds attributed to Caius Iulius Communis, likely a record of weight or quantity for trade or production.

EDCS-20601162.text_edition: \n Lumphae sic pondo CX Luci L \n
EDCS-20601162.translation: Lumphae: so, 112 pounds, from Lucius L.
EDCS-20601162.summary: This inscription records 'Lumphae' weighing 112 pounds from Lucius L., likely a record of a specific product or commodity and its weight.

EDCS-20601166.summary: This inscription mentions Vernio Pomp and 'finest oil', likely indicating a producer or vendor of high-quality olive oil.

EDCS-20601167.translation: Olive oil for food, [weighing] a pound.
EDCS-20601167.summary: This is a fragment of a record, likely for an accounting or inventory, noting a quantity of olive oil, dated to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-20601170.text_edition: \n X excellens Auli B S Cai Iuli Pisonis\n
EDCS-20601170.translation: Excellent X, [son of] Caius Julius Piso, from Aulus B S.
EDCS-20601170.summary: This inscription is a fragment that likely commemorates a person of excellence named X, possibly associated with Aulus B S and Caius Julius Piso, from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-20601171.text_edition: \n Garum Hispanicum QQuintorum Caeciliorum Publius M C\n
EDCS-20601171.translation: Garum Hispanicum, from Quintus Caecilius, Publius M C.
EDCS-20601171.summary: This inscription records a type of fish sauce, Garum Hispanicum, and names likely producers or vendors, Quintus Caecilius and Publius M C, from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-24200330.text_edition: \n Tiberius Cassius Tiberi filius Cri T \n
EDCS-24200330.translation: Tiberius Cassius, son of Tiberius, from Cri T.
EDCS-24200330.summary: This inscription records the name Tiberius Cassius, identifying his father Tiberius and a place of origin or association, Cri T, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-24200331.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae Telesphorus Cai Antoni Rufi servus publici portorii vilicus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-24200331.translation: To the Unconquered God Mithras, Telesphorus, freedman of Caius Antonius Rufus and steward of the public customs office, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-24200331.summary: This is a dedication to the god Mithras by Telesphorus, a freedman and tax collector, who fulfilled a vow, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-24200341.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Genio Augusti Cnaeus Firmininus Publius Antonius Avitus Publius Firminius Veruicius Maria Vindilla Aurelia Vivia Domitia Iusta qui pro se et suis omnib(us) votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-24200341.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to the Genius of the Emperor, Gnaeus Firmininus, Publius Antonius Avitus, Publius Firminius Veruicius, Maria Vindilla, Aurelia Vivia, Domitia Iusta, who for themselves and all their family fulfilled their vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-24200341.summary: This is a communal religious dedication to Jupiter and the Emperor's Genius made by a group of individuals, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-24200342.summary: This inscription records the name Lucilius, possibly followed by a cognomen or title like Principis, from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-24200344.summary: This inscription appears to be a personal name, Theomnestus, possibly on a tombstone or other marker, from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-24200348.text_edition: \n Adiectus Aduci libertus vivus sibi fecit et Botuni coniugi et Iantullae annorum XX\n
EDCS-24200348.translation: Adiectus, freedman of Aducus, made this while alive for himself, and for his wife Botuni, and for Iantulla, aged 20.
EDCS-24200348.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a self-funded tomb for Adiectus, his wife Botuni, and a young woman Iantulla, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-24200352.text_edition: \n III V mihi m elius ne tua r aurum in cr genis I \n
EDCS-24200352.translation: [---] three [---] me [---] elius [---] not your gold [---] on the hearths [---].
EDCS-24200352.summary: This is a heavily damaged and fragmentary inscription that mentions numbers, personal names, and potentially a description of personal wealth or objects, from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-24900098.summary: This is a funerary inscription naming Acastus, a slave, and his owner Albius Quintus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26400012.text_edition: \n Lucius Stlaccius Luci filius Secundus C\n
EDCS-26400012.translation: Lucius Stlaccius Secundus, son of Lucius.
EDCS-26400012.summary: This is a personal name inscription, likely a tombstone or dedication, identifying Lucius Stlaccius Secundus and his father, Lucius.

EDCS-27200237.text_edition: \n Adginnos Vercombogi Aeso votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-27200237.translation: Adginnos, son of Vercombogius, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-27200237.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Adginnos, son of Vercombogius, possibly a dedication to a deity, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-27200243.translation: Weight 20 pounds for his wife. Finitianus. E.
EDCS-27200243.summary: This inscription seems to record the weight of an item (20 pounds) and is dedicated to a wife by Finitianus, possibly related to a funerary context or a trade record.

EDCS-27300214.text_edition: \n Dextro Bottonis filio Nemeta Iuni filia uxor viva fecit et Silvestro Iuni filio et sibi\n
EDCS-27300214.translation: Nemeta, daughter of Iunius, wife, made this for her living son Dextrus, son of Botto, and for herself.
EDCS-27300214.summary: This funerary inscription records Nemeta dedicating a monument to her son Dextrus and herself, indicating family relationships and possibly a date in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-27300219.translation: First Cohort of the Aelian Brittones, Antoniniana.
EDCS-27300219.summary: This inscription identifies a military unit, the First Cohort of the Aelian Brittones, with the honorific title 'Antoniniana', likely dating to the period of the Antonine emperors.

EDCS-27300223.translation: Tiberius Claudius Saecularis is... [perhaps 'well' or 'peacefully' or 'alive']
EDCS-27300223.summary: This inscription begins with a name, Tiberius Claudius Saecularis, and appears to be incomplete, possibly a dedication or funerary epitaph.

EDCS-27300243.summary: This inscription records the name Pollia and her relationship to Pollio, likely a funerary epitaph or a dedication.

EDCS-27300245.translation: For Rusticus, son of Secundus, aged 60, and for Gaixunus, his spouse. [Son]...
EDCS-27300245.summary: This incomplete funerary inscription commemorates Rusticus, son of Secundus, aged 60, and his spouse Gaixunus, with a partial mention of a son.

EDCS-27300251.text_edition: \n Finitus Silvani Solliae Satullae coniugi annorum XXI\n
EDCS-27300251.translation: Finitus to Silvanus, and to his wife Sollia Satulla, aged 21.
EDCS-27300251.summary: This funerary inscription records Finitus commemorating Silvanus and his wife Sollia Satulla, who died at the age of 21.

EDCS-27300253.text_edition: \n Soli deo Invicto Publius Tapp(ius) Pollinus votum solvit\n
EDCS-27300253.translation: Publius Tappius Pollinus has fulfilled his vow to the unconquered god Sol.
EDCS-27300253.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to the god Sol by Publius Tappius Pollinus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-27300259.summary: A brief inscription likely marking a product, possibly an olive oil, of high quality, date unknown.

EDCS-27800669.text_edition: \n Cre nus A Delicia vivi fecerunt sibi et Valentinae coniugi annorum XVII Variila mater viva fecit\n
EDCS-27800669.translation: Cre and A Delicia made this for themselves and for their wife Valentina, aged 17; Variila the mother made it while alive.
EDCS-27800669.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valentina, recording her age and the dedicants, likely her parents or partners, from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-27900213.text_edition: \n Imperatoribus Caesaribus Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto tribunicia potestate XV consuli III et Lucio Aurelio Vero Augusto tribunicia potestate consuli II I\n
EDCS-27900213.translation: To the Emperors Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, in his fifteenth year of tribunician power and third consulship, and Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, in his second year of tribunician power and second consulship.
EDCS-27900213.summary: A dedication to Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, marking their imperial dignities, likely from the period of their joint rule (161-169 CE).

EDCS-27900214.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Ad<i>abenicus Parthicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII imperator XII pater patriae consul et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus maximus Britannicus maximus Germanicus maximus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate XVII imperator III consul IIII miliaria vetustate collapsa restitui iusserunt I\n
EDCS-27900214.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus maximus, pontifex maximus, in his ninth year of tribunician power, imperator XII, father of the country, consul, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus maximus Britannicus maximus Germanicus maximus, pontifex maximus, in his seventeenth year of tribunician power, imperator III, consul IIII, have ordered the restoration of milestones worn out by age.
EDCS-27900214.summary: An imperial rescript from Septimius Severus and Caracalla ordering the restoration of milestones, dating to between 208 and 211 CE.

EDCS-27900215.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II pater patriae consul et Marcus Opellius Antoninus Diadumenianus nobilissimus Caesar princebs sic iuventutis providentissimi Augusti fecerunt a Celeia mille passus\n
EDCS-27900215.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, father of the country, consul, and Marcus Opellius Antoninus Diadumenianus, most noble Caesar, princeps iuventutis, most provident son of the Augusti, have made [this] from Celeia for one thousand paces.
EDCS-27900215.summary: An inscription recording a road construction or marking project ordered by Emperors Macrinus and Diadumenianus, dating to 217-218 CE.

EDCS-27900328.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Invictus Augusto tribunicia potestate II consul II pater patriae proconsul et Caius Iulius Verus Maximus nobilissimus Caesar princeps iuventutis domini indulgentissimo sic pontes refecerunt et vias munierunt et miliaria restituerunt mille passus I\n
EDCS-27900328.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, in his second year of tribunician power, consul II, father of the country, proconsul, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, princeps iuventutis, to their most indulgent lords, have repaired the bridges, paved the roads, and restored the milestones for one thousand paces.
EDCS-27900328.summary: An inscription recording public works including bridge and road repair, and milestone restoration, ordered by Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Maximus, dating to 236 CE.

EDCS-27900337.translation: To our Lord Galerius Valerius Maximinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, twelve miles.
EDCS-27900337.summary: A milestone inscription marking a distance of twelve miles dedicated to Emperor Galerius Valerius Maximinus, dating to the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-27900341.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico Britannico pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate III imperatori VII consuli II patri patriae proconsuli et Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Felici Augusto Parthico Brittannico maximo Germanico maximo tribunicia potestate XIII imperatori IIII consuli IIII patri patriae proconsuli fortissimo principi domino indulgentissimo XXXVI Ob communem dominum et Imperatorem nostri sic Flavium Claudium Iulianum victorem ac triumphatorem semper Augustum XXXVI ECR NDI ab Iuvavo milia passuum XXXVI felicissimo principi \n
EDCS-27900341.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Britannicus, pontifex maximus, in his third year of tribunician power, imperator VII, consul II, father of the country, proconsul, and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Parthicus Britannico maximo Germanico maximo, in his thirteenth year of tribunician power, imperator IIII, consul IIII, father of the country, proconsul, to the most courageous prince, most indulgent lord, thirty-six [miles]. For our common lord and Emperor, thus Flavius Claudius Julianus, victor and triumphant ever Augustus, thirty-six [miles]. From Juvavum, thirty-six miles to the most fortunate prince.
EDCS-27900341.summary: A complex milestone inscription referencing multiple emperors including Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Julian the Apostate, possibly marking distances related to military campaigns or infrastructure projects, dating from the early 3rd century CE to 361-363 CE.

EDCS-27900372.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Augusto XXXXIII XXL[---]\n
EDCS-27900372.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Augustus, forty-three [miles]...
EDCS-27900372.summary: A fragment of a milestone inscription dedicated to Emperor Gordian III, likely indicating a distance of forty-three miles, dating to 238-244 CE.

EDCS-27900373.text_edition: \n Marco Iulio P<hi>lippo nob<i>lissimi sic Ceseri sic Augusto \n
EDCS-27900373.translation: To Marcus Julius Philippus, most noble Caesar Augustus.
EDCS-27900373.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Julius Philippus, referred to as Caesar Augustus, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-27900385.text_edition: \n Marcus Iulius Philippus nobilissimus Caesar Augustus\n
EDCS-27900385.translation: Marcus Julius Philippus, most noble Caesar Augustus.
EDCS-27900385.summary: This inscription names Marcus Julius Philippus, styled as Caesar Augustus, possibly an honorific title or part of an imperial titulature.

EDCS-27900386.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Messius Quintus Troianus sic Deccius sic Pius Felix Invictus Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate consul pater patriae proconsul\n
EDCS-27900386.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul.
EDCS-27900386.summary: This inscription lists the full titulature of Emperor Decius, including his imperial honors and offices, dating to his reign in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28500896.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording that Primitivus dedicated this monument to Pastorina.

EDCS-28701025.text_edition: \n Lucius Boniati filius et Iulia Prisca coniux vivi fecerunt sibi et Bussugnatae filiae nnum XV\n
EDCS-28701025.translation: Lucius, son of Boniatus, and his wife Iulia Prisca, made this while alive for themselves and for their daughter Bussugnata, aged 15.
EDCS-28701025.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a self-dedication and dedication for a daughter, made by a couple named Lucius and Iulia Prisca.

EDCS-28701026.text_edition: \n Speratus Silvani annorum L et Verecunda Suri et Iusto filio annorum XXXX\n
EDCS-28701026.translation: Speratus, son of Silvanus, aged 50, and Verecunda, daughter of Surus, and their son Iustus, aged 40.
EDCS-28701026.summary: This funerary inscription records the names and ages of three individuals: Speratus, Verecunda, and Iustus, possibly a family.

EDCS-28800822.text_edition: \n LIO Rufi quae co Accia V \n
EDCS-28800822.translation: To LIO, of Rufi, who was Accia.
EDCS-28800822.summary: This fragmentary inscription appears to be a funerary dedication, possibly for a woman named Lio, wife of Rufi, who was Accia.

EDCS-28800823.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus ius Successi filius mus annorum LX testamento fieri iussit sibi? et? Ulpiae Suadrae bene meritae faciendum curav---\n
EDCS-28800823.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: Successus, son of Mus, aged 60, ordered this to be made in his will for himself and for Ulpia Suadra, his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-28800823.summary: This funerary inscription records a posthumous dedication ordered by Successus for himself and his wife Ulpia Suadra.

EDCS-28800825.text_edition: \n vinti fili-- CON EREX anno--- XXV \n
EDCS-28800825.translation: [...]vinti, son of [...] CON erected [...] in the year [...] 25.
EDCS-28800825.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription recording the erection of something by an individual named [vinti] in a specific year, possibly related to a building project.

EDCS-29900145.text_edition: \n Ceudo Ariomani filius et Tocia Veriugi filia Admatae filiae sibi et suis vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-29900145.translation: Ceudo, son of Ariomanus, and Tocia, daughter of Veriugus, made this for their daughter Admata and for themselves and their family while alive.
EDCS-29900145.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a self-dedication and dedication for a daughter, made by Ceudo and Tocia.

EDCS-29900146.text_edition: \n et Philomuso socero nnorum LX et Litanunicae\n
EDCS-29900146.translation: To Philomusos, father-in-law, aged 60, and to Litanunica...
EDCS-29900146.summary: This epitaph commemorates Philomusos, a father-in-law, and Litanunica, likely his daughter-in-law, with the age of Philomusos provided.

EDCS-29900148.text_edition: \n Albinio Lucretio et Mansueto Aviti servo Albinia Couso viva fecit\n
EDCS-29900148.translation: Albinia Couso made this while alive for Albinus Lucretius and Mansuetus, slave of Avitus.
EDCS-29900148.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Albinia Couso for Albinus Lucretius and Mansuetus, a slave.

EDCS-29900149.text_edition: \n Tito Flavio Diocaito et Claudiae Finitae coniugi vivi fecerunt sibi et Ingenuo filio annorum XX\n
EDCS-29900149.translation: Titus Flavius Diocaitos and his wife Claudia Finita, while alive, made this for themselves and for their son Ingenuus, aged 20.
EDCS-29900149.summary: A self-funded funerary monument created by Titus Flavius Diocaitos and his wife Claudia Finita for themselves and their son Ingenuus.

EDCS-29900150.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Surio Verecundi filius vivus sibi et AIciae coniugi C filiae et TII- \n
EDCS-29900150.translation: To the departed spirits: Surius Verecundus, while alive, for himself and his wife Aricia, daughter [---], and his daughter T[---]...
EDCS-29900150.summary: A dedication to the 'Dis Manibus' (departed spirits) by Surius Verecundus for himself, his wife Aricia, and their daughter.

EDCS-29900151.translation: ...us Maro of Ovilava, and his wife...
EDCS-29900151.summary: This inscription fragment likely records the name of a person named Maro from Ovilava and his wife.

EDCS-30101179.summary: This fragment seems to record the name Iucundus, son of Rufus, possibly as part of a larger inscription.

EDCS-30101180.summary: A very fragmentary inscription indicating that someone, potentially associated with the number 20 or a place name, created something.

EDCS-30101193.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vitalis Finiti et Ingenua Crispi coniunx vivi sibi fecerunt et Verino annorum XXV et Fuscae annorum VII et Valentino annorum annorum II filiis \n
EDCS-30101193.translation: Vitalis Finitus and his wife Ingenua Crispi, while alive, made this for themselves and for their sons Verinus, aged 25, Fusca, aged 7, and Valentinus, aged 2.
EDCS-30101193.summary: This funerary monument was created by Vitalis Finitus and his wife Ingenua Crispi for themselves and their children, Verinus, Fusca, and Valentinus.

EDCS-30101194.summary: This is a dedication to the god Belenus by Lucius Sentius Forensis.

EDCS-30101201.text_edition: \n Lucius Varius Claudia Pr PRO PRO\n
EDCS-30101201.translation: Lucius Varius, Claudia Pr[---] PRO PRO...
EDCS-30101201.summary: This inscription fragment likely records the names Lucius Varius and Claudia Pr[---], possibly in a dedicatory or funerary context.

EDCS-30101202.text_edition: \n cius V Secundinae Primigeni filiae coniugi optimae annorum XXX\n
EDCS-30101202.translation: To Ciia, daughter of Vipsanius Secundinus Primigenius, his most devoted wife, aged 30.
