EDCS-30101202.summary: This is a tombstone for Ciia, wife of Vipsanius Secundinus Primigenius, who died at the age of 30.

EDCS-30101204.summary: This is a tombstone for Publius Alfius Priamus.

EDCS-30301340.text_edition: \n Titus Iulius Bellicus decurio alae Asturum II decurio municipii Claudiae Celeiae testamento fieri iussit Tiberius Iulius Frontonis filius Civis frater et heres fecit\n
EDCS-30301340.translation: Titus Julius Bellicus, decurion of the Second Astures cavalry unit, decurion of the municipium of Celeiae, ordered this to be made by his will. His brother and heir, Tiberius Julius Frontonis, had it made.
EDCS-30301340.summary: This monument was commissioned by Titus Julius Bellicus, a military officer and local official, and completed by his brother and heir.

EDCS-30400932.text_edition: \n Caio Cabalionio Masculi TIONEIN et Claudiae filiae AITAVXKI et Tim Mas\n
EDCS-30400932.translation: To Gaius Cabalionius, son of Masculus, and Claudia, daughter of Auitauks, and Tim, son of Mas...
EDCS-30400932.summary: This fragmentary inscription appears to be a dedication or tombstone for several individuals, possibly family members.

EDCS-31500094.text_edition: \n Titus Ambisavi filius hannorum sic LXXX hic situs Uxela Griponi filia annorum L\n
EDCS-31500094.translation: Titus Ambisavus, son of..., aged 80, is buried here; Uxela, daughter of Gripo, aged 50.
EDCS-31500094.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Titus Ambisavus and Uxela, with their ages.

EDCS-32200531.text_edition: \n Pro salute Imperatoris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti collegiati quorum nomina infra scripta sunt ius Severinus onius Secundinus ius Optatus ius Mandatus ius Quinctus Annius Respectus ius Extricatus ius Ferrox Titius Ianuarius Iulius Candidus s Exsuperans s Iuncinus s Secundinus ius Candidus s Homuncio s Restitutus ius Primitivus s Fuscus us Crescens s Messor Ulpius Rufius [ ------\n
EDCS-32200531.translation: For the health of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, the collegiates, whose names are written below: Severinus, Secundinus, Optatus, Mandatus, Quinctus, Annius Respectus, Extricatus, Ferrox, Titius Ianuarius, Iulius Candidus, Exsuperans, Iuncinus, Secundinus, Candidus, Homuncio, Restitutus, Primitivus, Fuscus, Messor, Ulpius Rufius, [illegible names]...
EDCS-32200531.summary: This inscription records a dedication made for the Emperor's health by a group of collegiates (members of a professional or religious association).

EDCS-32300401.text_edition: \n Caio Vitorio Iucundo et Primiae Privatae Caius Vitorius Ingenuus parentibus optimis vivus fecit et sibi et suis\n
EDCS-32300401.translation: Caius Vitorius Ingenuus made this for his excellent parents, Gaius Vitorius Iucundus and Primia Privata, during his lifetime, and for himself and his family.
EDCS-32300401.summary: This is a dedication made by Caius Vitorius Ingenuus for his parents and for himself and his family.

EDCS-32300437.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Pacilius Privatus et Ursa Ursuli coniunx et Ianuaria filia vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-32300437.translation: To the divine spirits. Pacilius Privatus, Ursa his wife, and Ianuaria his daughter, made this during their lifetime.
EDCS-32300437.summary: This tombstone is dedicated to the departed spirits by Pacilius Privatus, his wife Ursa, and their daughter Ianuaria.

EDCS-32300479.translation: [Dedication] for the municipium of Claudius Aguntum.
EDCS-32300479.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly a dedication or record relating to the Roman municipality of Aguntum.

EDCS-32300635.text_edition: \n Publio Cassio Materniano filio annnorum III\n
EDCS-32300635.translation: To Publius Cassius Maternianus, son of..., aged 3.
EDCS-32300635.summary: This is a tombstone for Publius Cassius Maternianus, who died at the age of 3.

EDCS-32300650.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Saprius Secundus Ulpiae Domtillae coniugi @(obitae) annorum L\n
EDCS-32300650.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Saprius Secundus, for his wife Ulpia Domtilla, who died at the age of 50.
EDCS-32300650.summary: A tombstone inscription dedicated to a deceased wife, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300651.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Restuti Quarti equitis alae Commagenorum singularis nnorum XXXV heres Respectus Dubitati faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-32300651.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Restutus, son of Quartus, a troop leader (singularis) of the cavalry wing (ala) of Commageni, aged 35. The heir, Respectus Dubitati, arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-32300651.summary: A tombstone inscription for a cavalryman, set up by his heir, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300685.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Iulio Ursino decurioni alae I Commagenorum annorum XL Titia Procula marito optimo fecit et sibi\n
EDCS-32300685.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Gaius Julius Ursinius, decurion of the first cavalry wing of Commageni, aged 40. Titia Procula made this for her excellent husband and for herself.
EDCS-32300685.summary: A tombstone inscription for a decurion, set up by his wife for both of them, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300695.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelio Aemilio veterano annorum LXV Aelia Amuca coniux marito faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-32300695.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Aelius Aemilius, a veteran, aged 65. His wife, Aelia Amuca, arranged for this to be made for her husband.
EDCS-32300695.summary: A tombstone inscription for a veteran, set up by his wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32400005.text_edition: \n Potens Savariensis vivus fecit sibi et Maeciae coniugi optimae et Maecio Primitivo filio et Aug Suadrae coniugi\n
EDCS-32400005.translation: Potens Savariensis made this while alive for himself, for his excellent wife Maecia, for his son Maecius Primitivus, and for his wife Aug[---] Suadra.
EDCS-32400005.summary: A funerary inscription recording a man setting up a monument for himself and his family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32400010.text_edition: \n Tertio Valentis et Samuconi Cupiti et Valentino filio annorum L et Suadrae coniugi\n
EDCS-32400010.translation: For Tertius, son of Valens and Samuconis Cupitus, and for his son Valentinus, aged 50, and for his wife Suadra.
EDCS-32400010.summary: A funerary inscription recording a man, his son, and his wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32400011.summary: A very short inscription, possibly a name and profession or a nickname, the meaning of which is uncertain.

EDCS-32400014.text_edition: \n Nemesi Augustae Aelius Donatus pro salute sua et Octavies sic coniugis\n
EDCS-32400014.translation: To Augusta Nemesis. Aelius Donatus for his own health and that of his wife Octavies.
EDCS-32400014.summary: A dedication to the goddess Nemesis for the health of the dedicator and his wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32400015.text_edition: \n Nemesi Augustae sacrum Martialis venator votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-32400015.translation: To Augusta Nemesis. Martialis, the hunter, willingly, joyfully, and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-32400015.summary: A votive inscription to the goddess Nemesis by a hunter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32400016.text_edition: \n Campestribus sacrum Verecundus eques alae I Thracum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32400016.translation: To the Campestres [gods]. Verecundus, a cavalryman of the first cavalry wing of Thracians, willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-32400016.summary: A votive inscription to the Campestres gods by a cavalryman, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32400017.summary: A dedication to the goddess Fortuna, likely on a shrine or monument.

EDCS-32400018.text_edition: \n Nemesi Reginae Augustae Cassius Sextus quod voberat sic libenti sic animo posuit\n
EDCS-32400018.translation: To Nemesis Regina Augusta, Cassius Sextus, having vowed it, placed this with a willing and joyful spirit.
EDCS-32400018.summary: A votive offering to Nemesis Regina Augusta by Cassius Sextus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32400019.text_edition: \n Nemesi Augustae sacrum Marcus venator ex voto libens merito fecit\n
EDCS-32400019.translation: Sacred to Nemesis Augusta; Marcus the hunter made this voluntarily and deservedly in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-32400019.summary: A votive inscription to Nemesis Augusta by Marcus, a hunter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32400025.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Aurelio Valerio Diocletiano invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate iterum consuli patri patriae proconsuli ordo coloniae Ovilavensium devoti sic numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-32400025.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletian, unconquered Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the second time, consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul, the council of the colony of Ovilava, devoted to his divine will and majesty.
EDCS-32400025.summary: A formal dedication by the town council of Ovilava to Emperor Diocletian, likely in the late 3rd or early 4th century AD.

EDCS-32400026.text_edition: \n Gentilis Finiti filius et Crispa Atti vivi fecerunt sibi et Censorinae filiae annorum X\n
EDCS-32400026.translation: Gentilis, son of Finitus, and Crispa, wife of Attus, made this for themselves and for their daughter Censorina, aged 10.
EDCS-32400026.summary: A funerary monument for Gentilis, Crispa, and their daughter Censorina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32400240.translation: To Septumus and his wife Quincta.
EDCS-32400240.summary: A dedication or funerary commemoration for Septumus and his wife Quincta.

EDCS-32400264.translation: Of the century of Domitius Flavius Emeritus.
EDCS-32400264.summary: An inscription identifying a property or boundary related to the century of a military figure named Domitius Flavius Emeritus.

EDCS-32400268.text_edition: \n Herculi Aug(usto) sacrum Quintus Luccius Maximus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32400268.translation: Sacred to Hercules Augustus; Quintus Luccius Maximus fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-32400268.summary: A votive inscription to Hercules Augustus by Quintus Luccius Maximus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32400271.summary: A brief dedication to Hercules Augustus by Postumius.

EDCS-32400273.text_edition: \n Flaviae Tertullae coniugi pientissimae Metilius Ianu<a>rius vir vivus fecit\n
EDCS-32400273.translation: For Flavia Tertulla, his most dutiful wife, Metilius Ianuarius, her husband, made this while alive.
EDCS-32400273.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Metilius Ianuarius to his wife Flavia Tertulla, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32400274.text_edition: \n Cai Iuni Luci filii Paeti ab Carthaginis LXXXXVIII\n
EDCS-32400274.translation: Caius Junius Paetus, son of Lucius, from Carthage, aged 98.
EDCS-32400274.summary: This inscription records the age and origin of Caius Junius Paetus, likely a tombstone.

EDCS-32400324.text_edition: \n Marcus Iunius -- Sextus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32400324.translation: Marcus Junius Sextus paid his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-32400324.summary: This inscription records Marcus Junius Sextus fulfilling a vow, possibly on a votive monument.

EDCS-32500204.translation: To the Divine Spirits, Quintus Aurelius Aurelianus.
EDCS-32500204.summary: This is a dedication to the departed spirits, likely marking the tomb of Quintus Aurelius Aurelianus.

EDCS-32500210.text_edition: \n Butta Septimi filia sibi et Felicioni Primigeni filio viro optimo\n
EDCS-32500210.translation: Butta, daughter of Septimius, for herself and for her excellent husband Felicionius Primigenius.
EDCS-32500210.summary: This inscription is a dedication for Butta and her husband, likely a tombstone.

EDCS-32500217.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus onio ino IIviro nno-- L \n
EDCS-32500217.translation: To the Divine Spirits, Onio [---], duovir, aged [---].
EDCS-32500217.summary: This fragmented inscription is a dedication to the departed spirits, likely for a magistrate named Onio.

EDCS-32500293.text_edition: \n Publio Lepidio Publi filio Prisco domo Vicetia Prisca et Sabina filiae fecerunt\n
EDCS-32500293.translation: Prisca and Sabina, daughters, made this for Publius Lepidius Priscus, son of Publius, from Vicetia.
EDCS-32500293.summary: This inscription records Prisca and Sabina dedicating a monument to Publius Lepidius Priscus.

EDCS-32600001.text_edition: \n Belino Augusto sacrum Marcus dius Iunus aedilicia potestate Claudi Viruni et Restitutia Restuta pro se et suis voto posuerunt laeti libentes merito Praesente et Extricato consulibus\n
EDCS-32600001.translation: Sacred to Belinus Augustus. Marcus dius Iunus, holding the aedilician power, and Claudius Virunus and Restitutia Restuta, for themselves and their families, joyfully, willingly, and deservedly set this up, in the consulship of Praesens and Extricatus.
EDCS-32600001.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a sacred monument to Belinus Augustus by Marcus dius Iunus, Claudius Virunus, and Restitutia Restuta during the consulship of Praesens and Extricatus.

EDCS-32600103.translation: Faustinus of the First Cohort, (rank) posterior pilus.
EDCS-32600103.summary: This inscription identifies Faustinus, a soldier of the First Cohort holding the rank of posterior pilus, likely on a military tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-32601995.text_edition: \n Aureli-- Plu Aureli-- Dem Aureli-- Ch Aureli-- Fer Aureli-- Aureli-- A[ureli-- ------\n
EDCS-32601995.translation: Aurelius ---, Aurelius ---, Aurelius ---, Aurelius ---, Aurelius ---, Aurelius ---, Aurelius --- [---].
EDCS-32601995.summary: This fragmented inscription lists multiple individuals named Aurelius, likely members of the same family, possibly on a funerary monument.

EDCS-32602005.text_edition: \n Pro salute ddominorum nnostrorum Imperatorum Maximini et Maximi Caesarum Auggustorum Caius Cassius Honoratus murum longitudinis pedum XXXX ruina conlapsum a solo restituit et podium amp<h>itheatri opere tectorio cum pictura muneris sui exornavit et portam novam fecit Idibus Maiis Perpetuo et Corneliano consulibus\n
EDCS-32602005.translation: For the health of our lords the Emperors, Maximius and Maximus Caesars Augusti, Caius Cassius Honoratus restored from the ground a wall 40 feet long which had collapsed, adorned the amphitheater's podium with plasterwork and paintings as his own munificence, and built a new gate, on the Ides of May, in the consulship of Perpetuus and Cornelius.
EDCS-32602005.summary: This inscription records Caius Cassius Honoratus restoring a wall, decorating an amphitheater, and building a new gate for the health of Emperors Maximius and Maximus, during the consulship of Perpetuus and Cornelius.

EDCS-32602006.text_edition: \n Lucio Sestio Sexto et Valeriae Quinctionis filiae Lutumarae Moderatus filius vivus fecit\n
EDCS-32602006.translation: Moderatus, son of Lucius Sestius Sextus and Valeria Quinctionis Lutumara, made this while alive.
EDCS-32602006.summary: This is a funerary monument made by Moderatus for his parents, Lucius Sestius Sextus and Valeria Quinctionis Lutumara, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32602011.text_edition: \n Tiberius? Iulius Ti(beri) libertus Quintallus vivus fecit s(ibi) et Iul(iae) Severae coniug(i) optimae et Quintiano filio\n
EDCS-32602011.translation: Tiberius Julius Quintallus, freedman of Tiberius, made this while alive for himself, his excellent wife Julia Severa, and his son Quintianus.
EDCS-32602011.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Tiberius Julius Quintallus, a freedman, for himself and his family, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32602012.translation: and Suadr, son of... wife... made...
EDCS-32602012.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to record family members, possibly in a funerary context, but is too incomplete for a definitive summary.

EDCS-32700002.summary: This is a severely fragmented inscription, possibly naming individuals, but too little remains to determine its purpose or date.

EDCS-32700147.text_edition: \n Caio Iulio Cai filio Iant Tiberi- Ia \n
EDCS-32700147.translation: To Gaius Julius Iant, son of Gaius, from Tiberi- ...
EDCS-32700147.summary: This is a dedication or funerary inscription to Gaius Julius Iant, son of Gaius, but it is too fragmentary to provide further details or a date.

EDCS-35100008.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a dedication to a deity or individuals named Vecconirapio and another, its context and date are unknown.

EDCS-37500140.summary: This is a very short inscription that likely records a dedication to Jupiter Maximus, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-38000292.text_edition: \n Luci AtI m IV pondo III |(unciis duabus Philimuso sic\n
EDCS-38000292.translation: Lucius Atilius [---] three pounds two ounces. Philimuso [made this] thus.
EDCS-38000292.summary: This inscription records a weight of three pounds and two ounces associated with Lucius Atilius, possibly related to a transaction or offering, made by Philimuso.

EDCS-38000297.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Minerva Augusta, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-38000298.summary: This fragmentary inscription mentions Menelavi and gods, possibly part of a religious dedication or invocation, but its context is unclear.

EDCS-44200174.summary: This inscription likely records the status of Iunianus, a freedman of the emperors, possibly on a tomb or a dedication he made.

EDCS-44200175.text_edition: \n ciae filiae carissimae fili-- viv-- fec---\n
EDCS-44200175.translation: To my dearest daughter, Cia, her... made [this].
EDCS-44200175.summary: This fragmented inscription is a dedication to a daughter named Cia, made by a parent, likely on a funerary monument.

EDCS-44200178.summary: This very fragmented inscription appears to be the beginning of a dedication or funerary text, identifying a man named Pompeius and his father Titus.

EDCS-44200180.text_edition: \n Iunianus vivus fecit sibi et Quinto Cen filio quaestori Claudiae Celeiae annorum XXXV et nae filiae annorum XXVIII\n
EDCS-44200180.translation: Iunianus made this for himself and for his son Quintus Cen, quaestor, and for Claudia Celeia, aged 35, and for Nae, daughter, aged 28.
EDCS-44200180.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument set up by Iunianus for himself, his son Quintus Cen, and two women, Claudia Celeia and Nae, with their ages.

EDCS-44200181.summary: This brief inscription records a transaction where Verus purchased something from a person or entity named ResT.

EDCS-44300014.text_edition: \n Alba Cisioni viva fecit sibi et Rata Alba annorum XXX X\n
EDCS-44300014.translation: Alba Cisioni, while alive, made this for herself and for Rata Alba, aged 30.
EDCS-44300014.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument erected by Alba Cisioni for herself and Rata Alba, indicating it was made during Alba Cisioni's lifetime.

EDCS-44300015.summary: This is a simple dedication or funerary inscription honoring Gaius Julius Visonius.

EDCS-44300017.summary: This fragmented inscription mentions the Ambisontes, likely referring to a people or a place, possibly in a geographical or historical context.

EDCS-44300018.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus ius Potens signifer legionis INtutia na uxor Titus Sentus filii vivi SENTIS \n
EDCS-44300018.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Potens, standard-bearer of the legion INtutia, his wife Na, and his sons Titus Sentus and Sentis.
EDCS-44300018.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to the 'Dis Manibus' and names Potens, a legionary standard-bearer, his wife Na, and his sons Titus Sentus and Sentis.

EDCS-44300019.summary: This fragmented inscription begins to record the details of Lucius Marcius Verecundus, likely on a funerary monument.

EDCS-44300020.text_edition: \n Ulpio Ronodeo veterano alae I Augustae annorum LV Memmia Sibulla coniugi pientissimo viva fecit\n
EDCS-44300020.translation: Memmia Sibulla had this made for her devoted husband Ulpius Ronodeus, a veteran of the I Augusta cavalry wing, aged 55.
EDCS-44300020.summary: This is a funerary monument for a veteran of the I Augusta cavalry wing, commissioned by his wife while she was alive.

EDCS-44300021.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Iulio Straboni sesquiplicario alae I Augustae annorum XXXV stipendiorum XII Marcus Attonius Seranus heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-44300021.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Marcus Attonius Seranus, heir, took care to have this made for Quintus Julius Strabo, a sesquiplicarius (a rank in the cavalry) of the I Augusta cavalry wing, aged 35, with 12 years of service.
EDCS-44300021.summary: This is a funerary monument for a sesquiplicarius of the I Augusta cavalry wing, commissioned by his heir.

EDCS-44300022.text_edition: \n Haldius Atti filius eques alae I Thracum annorum XXXVII stipendiorum XVIII heres <f=E>(aciendum) curavit Macer signifer \n
EDCS-44300022.translation: Haldius, son of Atti, a cavalryman of the I Thracian cavalry wing, aged 37, with 18 years of service, had this made; Macer the standard-bearer took care of it.
EDCS-44300022.summary: This is a funerary monument for a cavalryman of the I Thracian cavalry wing, commissioned by his standard-bearer who acted as heir.

EDCS-44300023.summary: This inscription appears to be a label or advertisement for high-quality black olives.

EDCS-44300025.text_edition: \n Insequens Apti vivus fecit sibi et Mansuetae Tironis filiae coniugi optimae annorum XXX\n
EDCS-44300025.translation: Insequens Apti made this for himself and for his excellent wife Mansueta, daughter of Tironis, aged 30, while he was alive.
EDCS-44300025.summary: This is a funerary monument commissioned by a man for himself and his wife, indicating they were alive at the time of commissioning.

EDCS-44300029.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that names Secundinus, a freedman, possibly as the subject or dedicator.

EDCS-46600012.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that likely indicates ownership or a dedication by Flavius Fontanus.

EDCS-46600014.text_edition: \n Aulus Iunius Car Cereri donum dedit\n
EDCS-46600014.translation: Aulus Iunius Car gave this as a gift to Ceres.
EDCS-46600014.summary: This records a votive offering made by Aulus Iunius Car to the goddess Ceres.

EDCS-46600018.summary: This is a simple inscription indicating that Catulus was the maker of an object.

EDCS-46600019.summary: This is a simple inscription indicating that Ianuarius was the maker of an object.

EDCS-48700106.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Amoenus, a freedman of Priamus.

EDCS-48800035.translation: Novellius, standard-bearer of Singular, from the Astures.
EDCS-48800035.summary: This inscription likely records the name and origin of a military standard-bearer.

EDCS-48900538.text_edition: \n Rufinus L Serus NINIIINIII Ingenui IISILIIIICIIIVV\n
EDCS-48900538.translation: Rufinus, son of L. Serus, [---] of Ingenuus.
EDCS-48900538.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that likely records personal names and familial relationships.

EDCS-48900539.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Adiutor, a freedman of Ingenuus.

EDCS-48900543.text_edition: \n Panna Verecundaes sic empta vigessi XXV\n
EDCS-48900543.summary: This record likely documents the sale of a slave named Panna Verecunda for 25 units of currency.

EDCS-48900553.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iuvavo pro salute Mari Aniceti et negotiationis eiius sic\n
EDCS-48900553.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Iuvavus, for the health of Marius Anicetus and his business.
EDCS-48900553.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter and Iuvavus for the well-being of Marius Anicetus and his enterprise.

EDCS-48900561.summary: This inscription records the name and parentage of an individual, likely on a tombstone.

EDCS-48900565.summary: This inscription records the name and patronymic of an individual, likely on a tombstone.

EDCS-48900567.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that likely records a personal name and parentage.

EDCS-48900568.text_edition: \n Censorino Terti filio et Secundinae ionis filiae Acceptus? f(ilius)\n
EDCS-48900568.translation: For Censorinus, son of Tertius, and for Secundina, daughter of Ionis, Acceptus? (son).
EDCS-48900568.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or memorial for Censorinus and Secundina, possibly by Acceptus.

EDCS-48900595.text_edition: \n Iulius Iulianus miles cohortis I Aureliae Brittonum magister figulinae Loco Felicis SA\n
EDCS-48900595.translation: Julius Julianus, soldier of the First Aurelian Cohort of Britons, manager of the Loco Felix pottery works.
EDCS-48900595.summary: This inscription likely records a tombstone for Julius Julianus, who served in the military and managed a pottery workshop.

EDCS-48900597.text_edition: \n Cotunno Tiberi Iuli Resp<e>cti servo per Septumum sic fratrem impensa dominica\n
EDCS-48900597.translation: To Cotunno, slave of Tiberius Julius Respectus, through Septumus, his brother, at the expense of the master.
EDCS-48900597.summary: This is an epitaph set up by Septumus for his brother Cotunno, a slave of Tiberius Julius Respectus.

EDCS-48900598.text_edition: \n IAIA f(ecit) sibi et Sao coniugi et Masculo filio\n
EDCS-48900598.translation: IAIA made this for herself, for her wife Sao, and for her son Masculus.
EDCS-48900598.summary: This is a self-dedicatory inscription for IAIA, her wife Sao, and son Masculus.

EDCS-48900599.text_edition: \n Bussumaro Atevali filio Aelius filius Secundus vivus fecit\n
EDCS-48900599.translation: Aelius Secundus, son of Aelius, son of Bussumarus Atevali, made this while alive.
EDCS-48900599.summary: This is a dedication made by Aelius Secundus for himself, indicating it was set up during his lifetime.

EDCS-48900600.text_edition: \n Primo Septumi sic Marci liberto Restuta Re<s>tuti filia coniugi viva fecit et Caerul<o=E> coniugi et Solito filio \n
EDCS-48900600.translation: Restuta, daughter of Restutus, wife of Primo Septumi, freedman of Marci, made this for herself and for her husband Caerulus and for her son Solitus while alive.
EDCS-48900600.summary: This is a dedication by Restuta for herself and her family, mentioning her husband Caerulus and son Solitus, and establishing her status as a freedwoman.

EDCS-48900605.translation: Ursus, a gift to Jupiter, a vow.
EDCS-48900605.summary: This is a votive offering dedicated to Jupiter by Ursus.

EDCS-48900606.text_edition: \n Marcus Iunius Iustinus IGN Sen Ursinus\n
EDCS-48900606.translation: Marcus Junius Iustinus, along with Sen Ursinus.
EDCS-48900606.summary: This inscription records the names Marcus Junius Iustinus and Sen Ursinus, possibly as a dedication or as part of a larger monument.

EDCS-48900607.translation: Cai, a gift, a dinner for Iustinus.
EDCS-48900607.summary: This inscription records a gift or expenditure, possibly a dinner, for Iustinus, dedicated by Cai.

EDCS-48900609.text_edition: \n Sura Suri Sura Masculi CIONI Suri\n
EDCS-48900609.translation: Sura, daughter of Suri, Sura, daughter of Masculus Cioni, daughter of Suri.
EDCS-48900609.summary: This inscription lists the names Sura, daughter of Suri, and another Sura, daughter of Masculus Cioni, daughter of Suri, likely as part of a family record or memorial.

EDCS-49100360.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Pius Augustus et Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus Pollieno Sebenno legato beneficia quae amplissimo ordine vel aliquo principe iubente collegiis centonariorum concessa sunt temere convelli non oportet sed quod legibus eorum sanxum sic est custodiatur et ii quos dicis divitiis suis sine onere uti publica subire munera compellantur neque enim collegiorum privilegium prosit aut iis qui artem non exercent aut iis qui maiores facultates praefinito modo possident adversus divites dum legis adhibendum est remedium non propter hos minuendus numerus alioquin tenuiores perfruantur vacatione quae non competit beneficiis collegiorum derogari s Tertulli Severi Priscus Quarti nis Ursulus Propincus sic Calvisi Victoriani Vibeni itae Sexti Atilius Iunianus Castrucius Castruci Crispius Quartus Respectinus Restituti Severinus Severi Quintus Castruci Congius Cosalus Ursus Publi Dubitanus Masculi Secundinus Adnamati Valerius Commodus Maternus Crispi Maleio Maturi Primianus Primitivi Secundinus Quarti Sertorius Karus Claudius Leo Ulpius Vitalis Atilianus Attici Emeritus Serotini Iulius Saturninus Vitalis Ruccessi Kanius Dignus Veranus Iuniani Classicianus Canionis Rettius Heracla Nonius Tertullinus Insequens Taciti Vibius Catussa Secundus Tertulli Finitus Valentionis Sertorius Karus iunior Calendinus Angulati Spectatianus Spectati Valentinus Vitalis Iulius Ianuarius Iulius Marinianus Ulpius Quietus Crispinus Vibeni Pompeius Vitalis Masculinus Surionis Tertullinus Tutoris Potentinus Potentinae Sextius Atilis Flavius Annianus Kanius Valentinus Aurelius Sabinianus Sabinianus Sabini Sacretius Sextus Secundianus Secundi Iulius Secundinus Iulius Tacitianus Vitalis Ingenui Sextius Maximus Rutilius Rutilianus Severus Senilis Primigenianus Primigeni Longinius Paterio Domitius Adnamatus Titius Restutus Valerius Valerianus Tertius Vitalis Iunius Tertullus Ingenuus Adnami Rufius Optatus Marcianus Ingenui Flavius Genialis Terentinus Marini Iustus Iustini Iunius Paterio Aurelius Maximus Crispius Honoratus Ingenuus Maleionis Surianus Secundini Iulius Valentinus <L=I>icinius Iunius Quintianus Quinti Iunius Secundinus Macedo Optati ordo et populus in honorem Marci Secundi Secundini patris tabulam posuit ex pecunia collegii centonariorum decreto decurionum rei publicae Solvensium pridie Idus Octobres Imperatore Antonino II et Geta consulibus curam agente Ursino \n
EDCS-49100360.translation: Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Pius Augustus and Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, to Pollienus Sebenno, governor. The benefits which were granted to the guilds of centonarii by the most ample order or by the command of any prince ought not to be rashly overturned, but what has been decreed by their laws should be guarded, and those whom you say are compelled to undergo public burdens with their wealth without any charge. For the privilege of the guilds should not benefit either those who do not practice the art or those who possess greater wealth beyond the prescribed limit; against the rich, the remedy of the law must be applied, not because of them should the number be reduced, otherwise the poorer may enjoy the exemption which does not apply to them. To derogate from the benefits of the guilds. Tertullus Severi, Priscus Quarti nis, Ursulus Propincus, Sic Calvisi Victoriani, Vibeni itae Sexti, Atilius Iunianus Castrucius, Crispius Quartus, Respectinus Restituti, Severinus Severi, Quintus Castruci, Congius Cosalus, Ursus Publi, Dubitanus Masculi, Secundinus Adnamati, Valerius Commodus Maternus, Crispi Maleio, Maturi Primianus, Secundinus Quarti, Sertorius Karus, Claudius Leo, Ulpius Vitalis Atilianus, Attici Emeritus, Serotini Iulius Saturninus, Vitalis Ruccessi, Kanius Dignus, Veranus Iuniani Classicianus, Canionis Rettius Heracla, Nonius Tertullinus Insequens Taciti, Vibius Catussa Secundus, Tertulli Finitus Valentionis, Sertorius Karus junior, Calendinus Angulati, Spectatianus Spectati, Valentinus Vitalis, Iulius Ianuarius, Iulius Marinianus, Ulpius Quietus Crispinus Vibeni, Pompeius Vitalis Masculinus, Surionis Tertullinus Tutoris, Potentinus Potentinae, Sextius Atilis Flavius Annianus, Kanius Valentinus, Aurelius Sabinianus Sabinianus, Sabini Sacretius Sextus, Secundianus Secundi, Iulius Secundinus, Iulius Tacitianus Vitalis, Ingenui Sextius Maximus, Rutilius Rutilianus Severus Senilis, Primigenianus Primigeni, Longinius Paterio, Domitius Adnamatus, Titius Restutus Valerius, Valerianus Tertius, Vitalis Iunius Tertullus Ingenuus, Adnami Rufius Optatus Marcianus Ingenui, Flavius Genialis Terentinus Marini, Iustus Iustini, Iunius Paterio, Aurelius Maximus Crispius Honoratus Ingenuus, Maleionis Surianus Secundini, Iulius Valentinus Licinius, Iunius Quintianus Quinti, Iunius Secundinus Macedo Optati. The order and people erected this tablet in honor of Marcus Secundus Secundinus, father, from the money of the guild of centonarii, by decree of the decurions of the public treasury of the Solvensians, on the day before the Ides of October, when Antoninus II and Geta were consuls, under the administration of Ursine.
EDCS-49100360.summary: This is a decree issued by Emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, affirming the privileges of the centonarii (a guild of weavers or clothiers), and also records the erection of a tablet in honor of Marcus Secundus Secundinus by the guild and the people of Solvensia during the consulship of Antoninus II and Geta.

EDCS-49100366.text_edition: \n Fortunae Augustae sacrum Flavius Covnertus et Nonius in honorem collegii Manliensium titulum donum dederunt Hilarus Secundi talis s Varius Ursus Naevius Fortunatus Domitius Primus Iulius Vibianus Iulius Secundinus dius Calendini Ingenuus unus Aulus A Iulius Iulius I P s s us s Ingenuus Rediti Virunensium Mi Ursus ato Maximus Vitalius Successianus Claudius Hilarus Romanius Ligur Speratius Maximi Baebius Fortunatus Cornelius Dubitatus Crescentius Tertullus erc E Atunus Butti Vettius Secundus Ursus Itti Martius Covnerti Publicus Aviti Ursus Lucconis Genetivus Marini Quintius Quintilianus Aurelius Ingenuus Flavius Ingenuus Lucanius Secundus Flavius Ursinus Septimius Successi Ulpius Valerius Speratus Hilari Maternius Acutio Tappius Firmus Aurelius Iulius Fortunatus Septimius Claudianus Do Iulius Candidus Murcianus Aurelius Antonius Catronius Aurelius Ulpianus Campanius Securianus\n
EDCS-49100366.translation: Sacred to Imperial Fortune. Flavius Covnertus and Nonius, in honor of the Manlian association, gave this inscribed monument as a gift. [List of names follows]
EDCS-49100366.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Fortuna Augusta and lists the names of individuals associated with the Manlian collegium, likely a collegium or association, who gifted the monument.

EDCS-49100368.text_edition: \n Nomina collegiatorum subaedianorum Aterius Calendinus Curionius Cautus Gaius Alfius Granius Satrius Tertianus Secundinus Obilonis Titius Quartus Tertullus Calvonis Octavius Vitalis Postumius Ripanus Successus Iuliani Aelius Axio Primigenius Secundini Crispius Tertianus Ulpius Petronianus Corido Ingenui Ingenuus Cupiti Iulius Vicarius Respectus Maximi Lucanus Secundini Pompeius Verinus Liberalis Axionis Viatorius Castus Barbius Primigenius Britannius Candianus Crescentinus Maximi Attonius Pictor [] Mogetimarus Mogeti Successus Crescentis Messorinus Rufini [] Pompeius Antenor Acceptus Cassiani Ursulus Cauti Claudius Iucundus [] Flora Cauti Iucunda Grani Priscia Tertiani Quinta Surionis Tertulla Secundini Benigna Quarti Sucelu Calvonis Veponia Quintilla Gemina Successi Rantia Ianuaria Secundina Secundini [] Primitiva Petroniani Secundina Ingenui [] Calida Casti Restituta Primigeni Ingenua Mogetimari Sabinilla Cassiani Sabina Iucundi Dubitata Crescentis\n
EDCS-49100368.translation: Names of the subaedian collegiates. [List of names follows]
EDCS-49100368.summary: This inscription lists the names of members of a collegium, likely an association or guild, possibly associated with a subaedium (a subordinate building or a semi-underground structure).

EDCS-49300002.text_edition: \n Olei Histrici flos pondo V Quinti Lusidi Dext[ri ------\n
EDCS-49300002.translation: Five pounds of the finest Histrian oil of Quinti Lusidius Dext[er...]
EDCS-49300002.summary: This inscription records a quantity of high-quality oil (Olei Histrici flos) weighing five pounds, attributed to Quinti Lusidius Dext[er], likely a merchant or producer, possibly dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-49300003.summary: This inscription simply names a type of fermented fish sauce, Rennian garum, likely indicating a product for sale or trade, possibly from the Imperial period.

EDCS-49300005.summary: This inscription records a quantity (ten measures) of garum produced in Ostia, likely indicating a commercial transaction or inventory, possibly from the Imperial period.

EDCS-49300008.summary: This inscription likely refers to a unit of land or a division of soldiers belonging to Curenus, associated with a group named Magi, possibly of the Republican or early Imperial period.

EDCS-49300009.text_edition: \n Poma Raetica officina L modii II\n
EDCS-49300009.summary: This inscription records Raetian apples, specifying a workshop and a quantity of two modii, likely related to trade or storage, possibly from the Imperial period.

EDCS-49300010.summary: This inscription mentions a silo belonging to Caius M[...], indicating storage, likely for grain or other agricultural products, possibly from the Imperial period.

EDCS-49300011.summary: This inscription likely records a statement or decision made by Tillius Alfenus, possibly in a legal or administrative context, possibly from the Imperial period.

EDCS-49300012.summary: This inscription lists two personal names, Publius Postumius Frequens and Atpomarus, possibly indicating joint ownership, a business partnership, or a dedication, possibly from the Imperial period.

EDCS-49300019.text_edition: \n Opsta sic feliciter leo patiens is\n
EDCS-49300019.summary: This is likely an epitaph or a private inscription for a lion named Opsta, possibly dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49300022.summary: This inscription records that Potens traveled to Rome, perhaps as part of a journey or a military posting, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49300023.summary: This appears to be a personal inscription or perhaps a funerary epigram for Anxur Carpoforus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49300025.text_edition: \n De fide Ombrionis secures CCCLV pondo II semis\n
EDCS-49300025.translation: From the property of Ombrionis, 355 pounds and 2.5 ounces of axes.
EDCS-49300025.summary: This inscription records an inventory or sale of axes belonging to Ombrionis, with a weight specified in pounds and ounces, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49300030.text_edition: \n Aurum bonum secures pondo XI CCCXCIII Filenus sic Roma\n
EDCS-49300030.translation: Good gold, 11 pounds and 393 ounces of axes, Filenus, from Rome.
EDCS-49300030.summary: This inscription describes the sale or quality of axes, noting their weight and origin (Rome), possibly indicating a commercial transaction from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49300032.text_edition: \n Aulus Pontius Vetulonia Publius Pontius Vitalio filius Firminus Vergilianus Cilo\n
EDCS-49300032.translation: Aulus Pontius Vetulonia, Publius Pontius Vitalio, his son, Firminus Vergilianus, Cilo.
EDCS-49300032.summary: This inscription lists several individuals, likely patrons and beneficiaries or family members, possibly related to a funerary monument or a legal document, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49300034.summary: This inscription likely marks a distance of 15 paces in Bononia (modern Bologna, Italy), possibly related to land division or a boundary marker, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49300035.summary: This is likely a simple funerary inscription or personal marker for Septimianus Taulus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49300036.summary: This inscription appears to be a funerary marker or a personal inscription for Volsenus Vindelicus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49300038.text_edition: \n s cados CL uncos DLX anulos DLXX s anulos DL uncos DX anulos DLX uncos DLXV ius Albanus DX Orobius d[iscos ------\n
EDCS-49300038.translation: 60 skins, 560 rings, 570 rings, 50 rings, 500 rings, 565 rings, juice. Albanus 50. Orobius... [---] dishes.
EDCS-49300038.summary: This inscription seems to be an inventory of items including skins, rings, and dishes, possibly related to trade or a household list, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49300039.translation: To Mercury. Proclus, son of Proculus, [dedicated this].
EDCS-49300039.summary: This is a dedication to Mercury by Proclus, son of Proculus.

EDCS-49300040.translation: Mercury, in fulfillment of a vow, to Mercury.
EDCS-49300040.summary: This inscription records a vow made to Mercury.

EDCS-49300043.summary: This inscription records 160 discs belonging to Abudius.

EDCS-49300044.summary: This inscription records 110 discs belonging to Orosius.

EDCS-49300045.summary: This inscription records 110 tubs belonging to Audonius.

EDCS-49300046.text_edition: \n Pecunia locata ex II Kalendis Februariis in Kalendas Iulias nus Sexti filius Lanivias\n
EDCS-49300046.translation: Money lent from the Kalends of February to the Kalends of July, by Sextus, son of Lanivius.
EDCS-49300046.summary: This is a record of a loan of money from February to July, by Sextus, son of Lanivius.

EDCS-49300048.summary: This inscription records the number 110 associated with Rullus Proculus Verulanus.

EDCS-49300049.summary: This inscription records 115 anvils and pitchers belonging to Antipalus.

EDCS-49300056.text_edition: \n Lucius Opaius Verrucosus sacrum Mercurio fecit sua manu\n
EDCS-49300056.translation: Lucius Opaius Verrucosus made this sacred offering to Mercury with his own hand.
EDCS-49300056.summary: This is a personal dedication to Mercury by Lucius Opaius Verrucosus.

EDCS-49300061.text_edition: \n Recognitum? Astigis arca pondo LXX VIII Kalendas Martias Pansa\n
EDCS-49300061.translation: Accounted? seventy-eight pounds from the chest of Astigis, from the Kalends of March, Pansa.
EDCS-49300061.summary: This inscription records an accounting of 78 pounds from the chest of Astigis, dated to the Kalends of March, possibly by Pansa.

EDCS-49400001.summary: A private dedication by Genuinus Pisus, likely a funerary or votive monument, date unknown.

EDCS-51100470.text_edition: \n Herculi Augusto Gemellus Biraconis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-51100470.translation: Gemellus, son of Biraco, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Hercules Augustus.
EDCS-51100470.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Hercules Augustus by Gemellus, son of Biraco, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51100472.translation: Titus dedicated this to Mars Ultor.
EDCS-51100472.summary: A private dedication to Mars Ultor by Titus, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51100473.text_edition: \n Marti Augusto r Covnertus |(centurio) legionis VII geminae felicis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-51100473.translation: Covnertus, a centurion of the Seventh Gemina Felix legion, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Mars Augustus.
EDCS-51100473.summary: A military votive inscription dedicated to Mars Augustus by Covnertus, a centurion of the VII Gemina Felix legion, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51100474.text_edition: \n Apulo Croutae filio et Boniatae Attonis filiae filii fecerunt\n
EDCS-51100474.translation: The sons dedicated this to Apulus, son of Crouta, and Boniata, daughter of Atto.
EDCS-51100474.summary: A funerary monument erected by sons for their parents, Apulus and Boniata, date unknown.

EDCS-51100475.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo dis deabusque omnibus et Genio I legionis II Italicae piae fidelis cum suis votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-51100475.translation: He willingly, gladly, and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, all the gods and goddesses, and the Genius of the I Italica Pia Fidelis legion, along with his own.
EDCS-51100475.summary: A comprehensive votive inscription dedicated to the highest gods and the Genius of the I Italica legion by an individual, likely military personnel, date unknown.

EDCS-51100478.text_edition: \n Capito Satulli filio et Belatullae Covnerti filiae Belatullus filius fecit parentibus\n
EDCS-51100478.translation: Belatullus, son of Belatulla, daughter of Covnertus, erected this for his parents.
EDCS-51100478.summary: A funerary monument erected by a son, Belatullus, for his parents, Capito and Belatulla, date unknown.

EDCS-51100479.text_edition: \n Icuinus et Claudia Restuta parentibus filii fecerunt\n
EDCS-51100479.translation: Icuinus and Claudia Restuta, sons, erected this for their parents.
EDCS-51100479.summary: A funerary monument erected by sons, Icuinus and Claudia Restuta, for their parents, date unknown.

EDCS-51100480.text_edition: \n Cupito et Tertiae coniugi filii fecerunt et Restutae\n
EDCS-51100480.translation: The sons erected this for Cupito and Tertia his wife, and also for Restuta.
EDCS-51100480.summary: A funerary monument erected by sons for their parents, Cupito and Tertia, and also for Restuta, date unknown.

EDCS-51100481.text_edition: \n Secundinus et Tertullavivi? fecerunt? et Ingenuo Secundini filio? et Crescenti Secundini filio? et Secundini f(ili--)? --- \n
EDCS-51100481.translation: Secundinus and Tertulla [or son?] erected [this?] and for Ingenuus, son of Secundinus? and for Crescens, son of Secundinus? and for Secundinus, son of--? [restored].
EDCS-51100481.summary: A partially preserved funerary monument likely erected by Secundinus and Tertulla for deceased family members, including sons named Ingenuus and Crescens, date unknown.

EDCS-51100482.translation: To T. S., son of Matus.
EDCS-51100482.summary: This is a dedication to T. S. by his father, Matus, likely on a tombstone.

EDCS-51100484.text_edition: \n Aureliae Verecundae sorori Marci Aureli Surii\n
EDCS-51100484.translation: To Aurelia Verecunda, sister of Marcus Aurelius Surius.
EDCS-51100484.summary: This is a tombstone dedicated by Marcus Aurelius Surius to his sister, Aurelia Verecunda.

EDCS-51400033.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Publius Aelius Adiutor, possibly on a tomb or monument.

EDCS-51400043.translation: Martinus to the Unconquered God Mithras.
EDCS-51400043.summary: This is a dedication by Martinus to the god Mithras.

EDCS-51600011.text_edition: \n Iussu dei Mithrae F ROSAVIINI Aug Aurelius Aquila et Aurelius Flavianus sacerdotes posuerunt\n
EDCS-51600011.translation: By order of the god Mithras, F. Rosaviini, Aug. Aurelius Aquila and Aurelius Flavianus, priests, erected this.
EDCS-51600011.summary: This is a dedication erected by the priests Aurelius Aquila and Aurelius Flavianus, by order of the god Mithras.

EDCS-54300073.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Lucius VV \n
EDCS-54300073.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus, Lucius V. V. [erected this].
EDCS-54300073.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus by Lucius V. V.

EDCS-55501425.summary: This fragmentary inscription seems to record a dedication or memorial for at least two individuals, one of whom is named -minia.

EDCS-58000092.summary: This inscription likely marks the property or tomb of Petronius Severus.

EDCS-59200185.text_edition: \n SENOIVNOS coniugi dulcissimae finivit vitam annorum XXVIII IEus Adiuti filius SEI carissimae\n
EDCS-59200185.translation: Ieus Adiutus dedicated this to his dearest wife, who finished her life at 28 years of age.
EDCS-59200185.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a woman who died at 28, dedicated by her husband Ieus Adiutus.

EDCS-59200188.text_edition: \n Attius Vitalis atinius I<u=A>venis FA posuit OL LM VS \n
EDCS-59200188.translation: Attius Vitalis, an Atinius Iuvenis, erected this.
EDCS-59200188.summary: This is a dedication by Attius Vitalis, identifying himself with the Atinii Iuvenes clan.

EDCS-59900156.text_edition: \n Deo I(nvicto) Mithrae Martialis Augustorum pro salute Augustorum nostrorum vilici stationis Atrantinae\n
EDCS-59900156.translation: To the unconquered god Mithras, Martialis, a supervisor of the Atrantine customs post, for the well-being of our Emperors.
EDCS-59900156.summary: This is a dedication to the god Mithras by a customs official for the well-being of the emperors, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-62900040.text_edition: \n IIII Nonas Augustas Isauricus Legio II\n
EDCS-62900040.translation: The fourth day before the Nones of August. Isauricus, of the Second Legion.
EDCS-62900040.summary: This inscription records a date (August 2nd) and identifies an individual named Isauricus from the Second Legion, possibly commemorating an event or person related to that legion.

EDCS-65100034.summary: This is a very short inscription, likely a dedication to the god Poseidon, possibly found on a monument or object related to him.

EDCS-66300064.text_edition: \n Lucana Lucani filia dat ex numin-- monitu\n
EDCS-66300064.translation: Lucana, daughter of Lucanus, dedicates this at the command of the divine.
EDCS-66300064.summary: This dedication by Lucana, daughter of Lucanus, indicates an offering made in response to a divine instruction, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-67300027.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, Abvius.
EDCS-67300027.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating a monument to the deceased named Abvius, intended for the underworld deities.

EDCS-67600211.translation: Dubricus, son of Mithmius, and Maximus, of the First Cohort of Scutarii, fulfill their vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-67600211.summary: This is a votive inscription from soldiers, possibly of Germanic origin, fulfilling a vow for their well-being, likely military.

EDCS-67600239.text_edition: \n TOTV NA ISPA VII XVII pondo VIII semis\n
EDCS-67600239.summary: This inscription records a weight measurement, likely for a quantity of metal or goods, possibly related to trade or a specific item.

EDCS-68300254.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Aquilina Iuliae Calliopeni matri pientissimae vixit nnos LXX\n
EDCS-68300254.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Iulia Aquilina for her most devoted mother, Iulia Calliope, who lived 70 years.
EDCS-68300254.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Iulia Calliope, erected by her daughter Iulia Aquilina, recording her age at death, from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-68300255.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae pro salute Crescentis \n
EDCS-68300255.translation: To the unconquered god Mithras for the well-being of Crescens.
EDCS-68300255.summary: This is a dedication to the god Mithras for the well-being of an individual named Crescens, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-68300256.text_edition: \n templum Dei vetustate conla<p=B>sum pro salute Imperatoris nostri restituit Thallus \n
EDCS-68300256.translation: Thallus restored this temple of the god, worn out by age, for the well-being of our Emperor.
EDCS-68300256.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a temple by Thallus for the emperor's well-being, indicating a period of repair and imperial patronage.

EDCS-68300257.text_edition: \n Thallus Imperatoris nostri vilicus templum vetustate conlapsum pro salute sua et suorumque restituit\n
EDCS-68300257.translation: Thallus, steward of our Emperor, restored the temple, which had collapsed from age, for his own safety and that of his family.
EDCS-68300257.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a temple by a steward named Thallus for his family's well-being.

EDCS-68300258.text_edition: \n pro salute Eusebetis Caesaris nostri Eutyches libens merito\n
EDCS-68300258.translation: For the safety of our Emperor Eusebes, Eutyches willingly and deservedly offered this.
EDCS-68300258.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Eutyches for the safety of Emperor Eusebes.

EDCS-68300259.translation: Linus, servant of the procurator, steward.
EDCS-68300259.summary: This inscription simply identifies Linus as a steward and servant of a procurator.

EDCS-68300260.text_edition: \n Invicto Deo Amandus Cai Antoni Rufi publici portorii |contrascriptor votum solvit\n
EDCS-68300260.translation: Amandus, the counter-scribe for Caius Antonius Rufus, paid his vow willingly and deservedly to the Invictus God.
EDCS-68300260.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by Amandus, a counter-scribe for a tax collector, to the god Invictus.

EDCS-72900014.summary: This inscription is a brief dedication to public prosperity.

EDCS-73200027.text_edition: \n Aquoni Lucius Trosius Abascantus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-73200027.translation: Lucius Trosius Abascantus paid his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-73200027.summary: This inscription records Lucius Trosius Abascantus paying a vow.

EDCS-73200030.summary: This fragmented inscription seems to record something made of silver by a wife.

EDCS-73200034.summary: This is a very fragmented inscription with little readable text.

EDCS-73200036.summary: This fragmented inscription likely refers to daughters.

EDCS-73200040.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Q[...][...]nus [...]
EDCS-73200040.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the departed spirits, with the name of the deceased only partially preserved.

EDCS-73200064.summary: This is a tombstone for Albina, recorded as the daughter of Masclus and aged 80, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-73200065.translation: Saurus, son of Dunosedus, aged 80, is laid here.
EDCS-73200065.summary: This epitaph records the burial of Saurus, son of Dunosedus, who died at the age of 80, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-73200067.translation: For himself and for his most fortunate son, who died at the age of 20.
EDCS-73200067.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates a father's dedication for himself and his son who died at the age of 20, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-73200071.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records the name and parentage of a woman, and identifies her as the freedwoman of a man named Marcus (or similar), dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-73200072.summary: This very fragmentary inscription appears to record a man's name and parentage, with a note that he is alive, likely a funerary marker from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75300005.translation: Quiemon made for herself while alive, and for her wife Venixema, alive, and for their daughter Mai[...], deceased at age 20.
EDCS-75300005.summary: This funerary inscription records Quiemon's self-funded monument for herself, her wife Venixema, and their daughter Mai[...], who died at age 20, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75300225.summary: This inscription lists goods: wine, rue, and pepper, possibly recording inventory or ingredients, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75300228.translation: To the Holy God [Hercules?] [...], whose names [...], Gaius Julius Ingenuus [...], Marcus Aurelius F[...], Aurelius [...], Att[...], Julius Victor [...], Aurelius F[...].
EDCS-75300228.summary: This dedication is made to a Holy God, possibly Hercules, by several individuals including Gaius Julius Ingenuus and Marcus Aurelius F[...], dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75600110.translation: Secundinus // [payment for?] purple dye (?) // 100 sesterces // Bugia // [payment?] I // [payment?] II
EDCS-75600110.summary: This inscription appears to record a transaction, possibly involving the dyeing of cloth (purpurare), with amounts of money and names, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-75700038.translation: [...] Licinius, priest of Jupiter Optimus Maximus [and of] Dolichenus, by order of the Divine Power [...], for himself and his family, on the 6th day before the Kalends of July, in the consulship of Sabinus and Venustus.
EDCS-75700038.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Licinius, priest of Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Dolichenus, for himself and his family, dated to the consulship of Sabinus and Venustus (likely 147 AD).

EDCS-75700039.translation: Publius Aelius Sextinus, decurion and former duovir, dedicated this to his deceased wife Aelia Ovilia and Flavia Nativa, aged 20, his mother-in-law Septimia Honorata, his daughter Aelia Firmina, and his son Aelius Sabinus, aged 10.
EDCS-75700039.summary: This epitaph records the dedication of a monument by Publius Aelius Sextinus to his wife, mother-in-law, daughter, and son.

EDCS-75700041.translation: Secundus, while alive, made [this monument] for Festus, [himself, and...]
EDCS-75700041.summary: This funerary epitaph records Secundus as the maker of the monument for Festus while still alive.

EDCS-79100240.translation: Titus Paetranius Profuturus, while alive, made this for himself and for his freedwoman wife, Paetrania Luteva, most devoted; this monument will not follow an heir.
EDCS-79100240.summary: This epitaph records Titus Paetranius Profuturus as having erected his own tomb for himself and his wife, stipulating that it is not to be inherited.

EDCS-80300008.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Quadratus, Lucius [Claudius?] Privanus, aged 45.
EDCS-80300008.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tiberius Claudius Quadratus, likely set up by Lucius [Claudius?] Privanus.

EDCS-80300009.translation: To Vicarius, and Tertia, and their son Finitus, aged 20, and to Pastor, and his son Pastorius, aged 10.
EDCS-80300009.summary: This epitaph lists several individuals, likely family members, including children who died young.

EDCS-80300021.translation: To the August Hercules, Aelius Marcellus, exempt from compulsory service from the quarry, willingly, gladly, deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-80300021.summary: This dedication records Aelius Marcellus, a quarry worker, fulfilling a vow to Hercules Augustus.

EDCS-82400066.text_edition: \n IIA APIIAL uxor amant fecit NIs argent - I V\n
EDCS-82400066.translation: IA APIIAL, his loving wife, made [this monument]. Nls [son?] Argent [died?] aged 5.
EDCS-82400066.summary: This fragmented epitaph records a wife named Ia Apiial setting up a monument for her husband and possibly a son named Argent, who died young.

EDCS-04200810.translation: Aulus Poblicius, freedman of Decimus, and Aulus Poblicius, freedman of Publius, erected [this].
EDCS-04200810.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the erection of a monument by two freedmen, Aulus Poblicius and Publius Postumius, in honor of someone or for themselves.

EDCS-04900812.translation: Here rests the servant of Christ Nonnosus the deacon, who lived more or less 53 years, died on the 4th day before the Nones of September, and was buried in this place on the 13th day before the Kalends of August in the 11th indiction, third after the consulship of Lampadius and Orestes, most excellent men.
EDCS-04900812.summary: This is a Christian epitaph for a deacon named Nonnosus, providing his age at death, date of death, and date of burial, along with consular dating information from the 6th century AD.

EDCS-11300909.translation: Rejoice, O land, and rejoice, O soil, for a long time through the ages! Receive the peaceful body and flourish, crowned. Under which blessed bishop, we learned the law of the Lord with pious voice singing. Now, see if he gave us these gifts worthy of the Lord. May only balms, not other things, flood his body. But do you remember to pray to the Lord with your prayers, lest a fierce lion enter your flock.
EDCS-11300909.summary: This is a funerary epigram, likely for a bishop, praising him and encouraging prayers for his soul, possibly dating to Late Antiquity.

EDCS-11800774.translation: Who, fulfilling a vow, Cadius Primus Horatius Antipatrus of Patara, [dedicated] bowls to the Chthonic Gods.
EDCS-11800774.summary: This is a votive inscription where Cadius Primus Horatius Antipatrus dedicated bowls to the Chthonic Gods, likely in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13400221.translation: To Caia Septimia Iusta, daughter of Caius, aged 55, wife of Spectatus Priscio.
EDCS-13400221.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caia Septimia Iusta, daughter of Caius and wife of Spectatus Priscio, providing her age, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13700347.translation: Caius Spectalius Priscianus, duumvir with judicial authority, [dedicated this] to Claudia Celeia.
EDCS-13700347.summary: This inscription records an offering or dedication made by Caius Spectalius Priscianus, who held the office of duumvir, likely to Claudia Celeia, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400150.translation: To the Divine Shades. Ulpia Nundina made this for herself while alive, and for Ingenuus the father.
EDCS-14400150.summary: This is a funerary inscription where Ulpia Nundina made a dedication to the Divine Shades for herself and for Ingenuus the father, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400254.translation: [This is for] the slaves Mansuetus and Cogitata, of Fabianus, and [for] Samtunius.
EDCS-14400254.summary: This is a funerary inscription mentioning slaves named Mansuetus and Cogitata, belonging to Fabianus, and another individual named Samtunius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400263.translation: Hilarinus, son of Hilarius, willingly, joyfully, and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the Unconquered God Mithras.
EDCS-14400263.summary: This is a votive inscription where Hilarinus dedicated a gift to the god Mithras, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14400290.translation: For the safety of the Emperors Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, and of the most noble Caesar Publius Septimius Geta, they constructed a small shrine at their own expense.
EDCS-14400290.summary: This inscription records the construction of a small shrine at private expense for the well-being of the Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, likely dating to the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-14400327.summary: This inscription is a tombstone for Martia, daughter of Tetus, who died at age 40.

EDCS-14400328.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Secundinus, son of Atticus, aged 39.
EDCS-14400328.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Secundinus, son of Atticus, who died at age 39.

EDCS-14400357.translation: Aelius Ingenuus, deceased at age 61, and his daughter Iucunda, aged 6, his wife Iulia, and his son Festus, aged 20, and his son Novellus. They made this.
EDCS-14400357.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aelius Ingenuus, his wife Iulia, and their children, including Iucunda and Festus.

EDCS-14400361.translation: Vibes Mosiqu fulfilled his vow to Qupitus willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-14400361.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Vibes Mosiqu to Qupitus.

EDCS-14400466.translation: Lupius, freedman of Severus, deceased at age 40. Uttu, his wife, made this for her husband, herself, and their family while alive.
EDCS-14400466.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lupius, a freedman, and his wife Uttu, who erected the monument for themselves and their family.

EDCS-14400480.translation: Privatus, son of Turtur and Secundina Fannionis, while alive, for himself and his parents.
EDCS-14400480.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Privatus for himself and his parents, Turtur and Secundina Fannionis.

EDCS-14400481.translation: N and Mo, his wife, and Quintinianus, son, aged 25. His sons made this.
EDCS-14400481.summary: This funerary inscription was made by the sons for their father (presumably N or Mo) and their mother.

EDCS-14400490.translation: Gaius Attius Sedatus, son of Caius, for himself and for Dimitia Palumbi, his dearest wife, daughter of Palumbus, and for Attius Vibius, his son, and for Attia Vibia, his granddaughter, and for Titia Successa, his most dutiful daughter-in-law.
EDCS-14400490.summary: This inscription records Gaius Attius Sedatus dedicating a monument for himself, his wife Dimitia, son Attius Vibius, granddaughter Attia Vibia, and daughter-in-law Titia Successa.

EDCS-14400567.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Carus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, and to Marcus Aurelius Carinus, the most noble Caesar, 57 miles from Agunum.
EDCS-14400567.summary: This is a milestone inscription indicating a distance of 57 miles from Agunum, dedicated to Emperors Carus and Carinus.

EDCS-14400576.translation: To his wife, aged 10, and to his son Lucius Fabius Fortunatus, aged 40.
EDCS-14400576.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to an unnamed wife, aged 10, and a son named Lucius Fabius Fortunatus, aged 40.

EDCS-14400577.translation: Successus, 40 years old, is buried here. Successus, 25 years old. Exoratus, 25 years old, is buried here.
EDCS-14400577.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the burial of three individuals named Successus and Exoratus, along with their ages.

EDCS-14400644.translation: To our lord Constantius, the Invincible Augustus.
EDCS-14400644.summary: This is a dedication to the Roman Emperor Constantius, likely from the 4th century CE.

EDCS-14500644.translation: Caius Kanulanius Eumitus made this while alive for himself, and for his wife Nansinia Verecunda, and for his son Caius Kanulanius Nepos, a soldier of the III Thracian cavalry wing, aged 25, with 6 years of service, at the expense and initiative of Annius Festus.
EDCS-14500644.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Caius Kanulanius Eumitus for himself, his wife, and his son, a soldier, indicating the son's age and service length, and noting the financial contribution of Annius Festus.

EDCS-14500961.translation: By order of our lords Valentinian, Valens, and Gratian, everlasting Augusti, auxiliary soldiers of Lauriacum, commanded by him, brought this fort to completion from its foundations, under the administration of the most illustrious Equitius, count and master of both branches of the military, with the active involvement of Leontius, the prefect, during the consulship of our princes and lords, the third.
EDCS-14500961.summary: This inscription records the construction of a fort from its foundations, commissioned by the emperors Valentinian, Valens, and Gratian, and executed by auxiliary soldiers under the command of Count Equitius and Prefect Leontius.

EDCS-14501019.translation: Dedicated to the strength of our lords Flavius Valentinianus, Flavius Valens, and Flavius Gratianus, most invincible princes, established on the sea route from Celeia, at a distance of miles.
EDCS-14501019.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the emperors Valentinian, Valens, and Gratian, likely marking a road or a military installation related to the route from Celeia.

EDCS-14501038.translation: Butto made this for his son Vertron and his daughter-in-law Tetria Adnomata, and for his parents.
EDCS-14501038.summary: This is a dedication made by Butto for his son, his son's wife, and his own parents.

EDCS-14501074.translation: Aelia Iorina lived for 33 years. Aelius Paullinus.
EDCS-14501074.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Aelia Iorina, likely set up by Aelius Paullinus.

EDCS-14501105.translation: Fortunatus of the II Italica Legion.
EDCS-14501105.summary: This is a simple inscription identifying Fortunatus as a member of the II Italica Legion.

EDCS-14501106.summary: This inscription simply names the II Italica Legion.

EDCS-14501107.translation: Quintianus of the II Italica Legion.
EDCS-14501107.summary: This inscription identifies Quintianus as a member of the II Italica Legion.

EDCS-14600050.summary: This is the name of an individual, Titus Pollius Severus.

EDCS-14600263.translation: Lucius Gra, decurio of the ala
EDCS-14600263.summary: This inscription records Lucius Gra, a decurio (cavalry officer) of an ala (cavalry unit).

EDCS-14600278.translation: Pompeianus of the Second Italic Legion
EDCS-14600278.summary: This inscription refers to a soldier named Pompeianus belonging to the Second Italic Legion.

EDCS-14600279.translation: Pompeianus of the Second Italic Legion
EDCS-14600279.summary: This inscription refers to a soldier named Pompeianus belonging to the Second Italic Legion.

EDCS-14600280.translation: Pompeianus of the Second Italic Legion
EDCS-14600280.summary: This inscription refers to a soldier named Pompeianus belonging to the Second Italic Legion.

EDCS-14600282.translation: In the time of Ursicinus, a most perfect man, commander of the Second Italic Legion and the alae
EDCS-14600282.summary: This inscription dates to the command of Ursicinus, a high-ranking official overseeing the Second Italic Legion and auxiliary cavalry units.

EDCS-14600284.translation: In the time of Ursicinus, a most perfect man, commander of the Second Italic Legion and the alae
EDCS-14600284.summary: This inscription dates to the command of Ursicinus, a high-ranking official overseeing the Second Italic Legion and auxiliary cavalry units.

EDCS-14600285.translation: In the time of Ursicinus, commander
EDCS-14600285.summary: This inscription dates to the time when Ursicinus held a command position.

EDCS-14600286.translation: In the time of Ursicinus, commander
EDCS-14600286.summary: This inscription dates to the time when Ursicinus held a command position.

EDCS-14600289.translation: In the time of Ursicinus, the master
EDCS-14600289.summary: This inscription dates to the time when Ursicinus held the position of master.

EDCS-14600291.translation: The First Aelian Cohort of Archers
EDCS-14600291.summary: This inscription names a military unit, the First Aelian Cohort of Archers, likely belonging to the Roman army.

EDCS-14600297.summary: This inscription is a genitive attribution, likely marking ownership or dedication by Titus Catius Maton.

EDCS-14600375.summary: This inscription records a construction project of 120 feet attributed to Justinian the deacon, likely from the later Roman or Byzantine period.

EDCS-14600376.summary: This inscription records a construction project of 240 feet, with the dedicant's name partially lost, likely from the later Roman or Byzantine period.

EDCS-14600382.translation: Abraham the Syrian with his people made 40 feet
EDCS-14600382.summary: This inscription records a construction project of 40 feet by Abraham the Syrian and his associates, likely from the later Roman or Byzantine period.

EDCS-14600383.summary: This inscription records a construction project of 30 feet by Ursicinus and Nonnosa, likely from the later Roman or Byzantine period.

EDCS-14600385.summary: This inscription records a construction project of 120 feet by Severa and Antonianus, likely from the later Roman or Byzantine period.

EDCS-14600387.summary: This inscription records a construction project of 40 feet by an individual whose name is partially lost along with Ursa, likely from the later Roman or Byzantine period.

EDCS-14600427.summary: This inscription names a military unit, the Second Italic Legion, with an additional cognomen or attribute 'Pompeianus', likely belonging to the Roman army.

EDCS-14600428.summary: This inscription names a military unit, the Second Italic Legion, with an additional cognomen or attribute 'Pompeianus', likely belonging to the Roman army.

EDCS-14600431.summary: This is a name or ownership mark associated with the Second Italian Legion, possibly by a soldier named Quintianus.

EDCS-14600433.summary: This is a name or ownership mark associated with the Second Italian Legion, possibly by a soldier named Auspicatus.

EDCS-14600434.summary: This is a name or ownership mark associated with the Second Italian Legion, possibly by a soldier named Auspicatus.

EDCS-14600435.summary: This is a name or ownership mark associated with the Second Italian Legion, possibly by a soldier named Fabianus.

EDCS-14600436.translation: Of the Second Italian Legion, Iuvenis.
EDCS-14600436.summary: This is a genitive phrase, possibly indicating possession or affiliation, related to the Second Italian Legion and someone named Iuvenis.

EDCS-14600438.summary: This is a name or ownership mark associated with the Second Italian Legion, possibly by a soldier named Melissus.

EDCS-16000558.translation: In the time of Ursicinus, a most distinguished man, governor of the Second Italian Legion and its auxiliary units.
EDCS-16000558.summary: This inscription records an event or administrative period during the governorship of Ursicinus over the Second Italian Legion and its auxiliary units.

EDCS-16000559.translation: From the kilns of the Second Italian Legion Sabiniana, in the time of Ursicinus, a most distinguished man, governor.
EDCS-16000559.summary: This inscription relates to the pottery kilns of the Second Italian Legion Sabiniana, during the governorship of Ursicinus.

EDCS-16400106.translation: Ursus, a man of standing, with his wife Sarsina, made this in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-16400106.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated by Ursus and his wife Sarsina.

EDCS-20601151.translation: In the time of Ursicinus, a most distinguished man, governor of the Second Italian Legion.
EDCS-20601151.summary: This inscription records an event or administrative period during the governorship of Ursicinus over the Second Italian Legion.

EDCS-20601155.translation: Twenty-five *scyphi* of wine, from Antioch, on the Kalends of May.
EDCS-20601155.summary: This is a transactional record listing a quantity of wine from Antioch, dated to the Kalends of May.

EDCS-20601160.translation: Cos of Aemilius, of Marcus, of Marcus.
EDCS-20601160.summary: This inscription seems to record a quantity of Cos wine belonging to individuals named Marcus.

EDCS-26400010.translation: Anicetus, slave of Lucius Cominius, and Mandatus, slave.
EDCS-26400010.summary: This inscription records the names of two slaves, Anicetus and Mandatus, with the name of their owner, Lucius Cominius.

EDCS-26400011.summary: This inscription records the name of Bonosus and his father, Pomponius.

EDCS-27200235.translation: The sons made [this] for their mother Iunia.
EDCS-27200235.summary: This inscription records that sons dedicated a monument to their mother, Iunia.

EDCS-27200240.translation: Quartus, son, for himself and for his wife Niae and for his parents Quarto and Bonia and for his most devoted brother Marinus and for his dearest sister Quartilla and for his son Priscus, and for Lasciva and Amanda.
EDCS-27200240.summary: This is a funerary inscription setting up a tomb for the deceased and other family members by Quartus, son.

EDCS-27300207.translation: To the Genius, Rubellius Ursinus, slave, paid his vow willingly, joyfully, deservedly.
EDCS-27300207.summary: This is a votive inscription by Rubellius Ursinus, a slave, to a Genius.

EDCS-27300216.translation: To the Divine Shades, Aurelius Ma [lived] for Lucania, and for himself.
EDCS-27300216.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Aurelius Ma(?), who dedicated a tomb for Lucania and himself.

EDCS-28000537.summary: This inscription records the name of Felix, a slave belonging to Caecilius Bassus.

EDCS-28000852.summary: This is a dedication by Cyrtius.

EDCS-28100377.summary: This is a very brief inscription stating that Atinianus was the maker of the object or monument.

EDCS-28500632.summary: This is a very brief inscription stating that Crassiacus was the maker of the object or monument.

EDCS-28500637.summary: This is a very brief inscription stating that Inpetratus was the maker of the object or monument.

EDCS-28500642.summary: This is a very brief inscription stating that Primitivos was the maker of the object or monument.

EDCS-28500646.summary: This is a very brief inscription stating that Victorinus was the maker of the object or monument.

EDCS-28500778.summary: This is a very brief inscription stating that Herennius was the maker of the object or monument.

EDCS-28500779.translation: Iunius and Paternia Maxima made it.
EDCS-28500779.summary: This inscription records that Iunius and Paternia Maxima were the makers of the object or monument.

EDCS-28500794.summary: This inscription names Tiberius Claudius Saturninus, likely as the dedicator or subject of the monument.

EDCS-28500807.summary: This is a very brief inscription stating that Sollemnis was the maker of the object or monument.

EDCS-28500888.summary: This is a very brief inscription stating that Comitialis was the maker of the object or monument.

EDCS-28500899.summary: This is a dedication by Severianus.

EDCS-28500900.summary: This is a dedication by Severinus.

EDCS-28500927.summary: This is a wish for long life addressed to Severinus.

EDCS-28500957.summary: This inscription names Lucon and Sulernus.

EDCS-28500983.summary: This inscription likely records ownership or a dedication by Caius Laterius Sabinus.

EDCS-29000250.translation: The new workshop of Arles, under the magistracy of Ursicinus.
EDCS-29000250.summary: This inscription records the establishment or operation of a new workshop in Arles under the supervision of Ursicinus.

EDCS-29100341.translation: On the Nones of September, Annamus [dedicated this].
EDCS-29100341.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Annamus on a specific date in September.

EDCS-29100344.translation: Minucius of the Second Italic Legion.
EDCS-29100344.summary: This inscription identifies Minucius as a member of the Second Italic Legion.

EDCS-29100345.summary: This inscription identifies Rogatianus as a member of an unspecified legion.

EDCS-30400628.summary: This inscription seems to record a gift or dedication by Ambudo.

EDCS-30501248.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by Gaius Julius Philo.

EDCS-30501250.translation: Here Janus, having been born, happily and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-30501250.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to the god Janus.

EDCS-30501251.translation: Tertullina for Gaius Tullius Ittio and his wife Quintae and three sons.
EDCS-30501251.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Tertullina to Gaius Tullius Ittio, his wife Quintae, and their three sons.

EDCS-31000374.summary: This inscription records the name of Titus Cascius Ascanius, likely on a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-31000375.translation: Sollers, son of Gallus, with his mark.
EDCS-31000375.summary: This inscription records the name Sollers, son of Gallus, possibly identifying ownership or a maker.

EDCS-31000376.summary: This inscription contains the name Lugidamus followed by initials, likely for a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-31000377.summary: This inscription records the name Lucius Pomponius Trausus, probably on a funerary monument.

EDCS-31000378.translation: Publius Vettius Quartus, son of Publius.
EDCS-31000378.summary: This inscription records the name and parentage of Publius Vettius Quartus.

EDCS-31000465.summary: This inscription identifies the workshop belonging to Gaius Julius.

EDCS-31000472.summary: This inscription identifies the workshop belonging to Severus.

EDCS-31000476.summary: This inscription records that Augustinus was the maker of the object.

EDCS-31000496.summary: This inscription identifies the work as originating from the workshop of Reburrus.

EDCS-31000519.summary: This inscription records that Peregrinus was the maker of the object.

EDCS-31000532.summary: This inscription identifies the work as originating from the workshop of Vitalis.

EDCS-31000574.translation: Recutus I by the hand of Iulius.
EDCS-31000574.summary: This inscription attributes the work to Recutus I, crafted by Iulius.

EDCS-31000594.summary: This inscription likely refers to Emperor Augustus, perhaps as a dedication or an indication of ownership.

EDCS-31000601.summary: This inscription appears to record the name Gaius Casia Secundus, possibly as the dedicator or maker, with some uncertainty due to abbreviation.

EDCS-31000602.summary: This inscription records the name Gaius Cauvicus Secundus, likely as the dedicator or maker.

EDCS-31000603.summary: This inscription records the name Quintus Ettius Catur[ius?], with some uncertainty due to abbreviation, likely as the dedicator or maker.
